In the crossed model, variance is correctly partitioned into firm-level variance, year-level variance, and residual. In the firm-only model, the residual variance is inflated — it has absorbed both the true residual and the year-level economy shocks.


14.2.6 The Key Diagnostic: Standard Error Comparison


Code
se_wrong   <- broom.mixed::tidy(m_firm_only)[2, "std.error"][[1]]
se_crossed <- broom.mixed::tidy(m_crossed)[2,   "std.error"][[1]]
se_fe      <- broom.mixed::tidy(m_year_fe)[2,   "std.error"][[1]]

tibble(
  Model             = c("Firm RE only (wrong)",
                         "Firm + Year RE (correct)",
                         "Firm RE + Year FE"),
  Investment_SE     = c(se_wrong, se_crossed, se_fe),
  Anti_conservative = c("Yes — too small", "Correct", "Correct")
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 4,
               caption = "SE of investment coefficient: year effects ignored vs. modeled")



SE of investment coefficient: year effects ignored vs. modeled


Model
Investment_SE
Anti_conservative




Firm RE only (wrong)
0.0188
Yes — too small


Firm + Year RE (correct)
0.0171
Correct


Firm RE + Year FE
0.0171
Correct





The firm-only model understates uncertainty for the investment coefficient. When economy-wide year variance is left in the residual, the model mistakes correlated year-level noise for independent observations, producing an artificially small standard error.


14.2.7 Visualizing the Problem: Residual Heatmaps
A powerful diagnostic: plot model residuals as a heatmap over the firm × year grid. Under the wrong model, economy-wide shocks create vertical stripes — in a bad year, all firms have negative residuals simultaneously. Under the correct model, residuals are patternless.


Code
df_diag <- df_panel |>
  mutate(
    resid_wrong   = residuals(m_firm_only),
    resid_correct = residuals(m_crossed)
  )

p_wrong <- ggplot(df_diag, aes(x = year_fac, y = firm, fill = resid_wrong)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#2166ac", mid = "white", high = "#d73027",
                       midpoint = 0, name = "Residual") +
  labs(title = "Wrong model (firm RE only)",
       subtitle = "Vertical stripes = year shocks pooled into residuals",
       x = "Year", y = "Firm") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

p_correct <- ggplot(df_diag, aes(x = year_fac, y = firm, fill = resid_correct)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#2166ac", mid = "white", high = "#d73027",
                       midpoint = 0, name = "Residual") +
  labs(title = "Correct model (firm + year RE)",
       subtitle = "No vertical stripes — year shocks absorbed by year RE",
       x = "Year", y = "Firm") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

p_wrong / p_correct









The vertical stripes in the wrong model are the visual signature of an omitted crossed random effect. Each stripe represents a year in which the economy was unusually good or bad — variance that is genuinely year-level but has been misattributed to the residual.


14.2.8 Year Fixed Effects vs. Year Random Effects
In panel data, analysts often absorb year effects using year dummy variables (year fixed effects) rather than a random intercept. The choice has a precise statistical interpretation:








Approach
When to use
Key assumption




Year random effects
Years are a random sample from a broader population of time periods
Normality of year effects; exchangeability


Year fixed effects
These specific years are the complete population of interest
No distributional assumption on year effects


Neither
Years have no systematic shared component
All year variance is idiosyncratic noise



Rule of thumb: With \(\geq 10\) years, random effects and fixed effects give similar coefficient estimates. With \(< 5\) years, fixed effects are more conservative and generally preferred because there is too little information to estimate the year-level variance reliably.
Connection to Module 1 Part 1 (Fixed Effects): Year dummies in a fixed-effects model ARE year fixed effects. The choice between fixed effects and random effects for the year dimension mirrors the general FE vs. RE choice from Part 4 — it depends on whether you want to generalize to years outside your sample.


14.2.9 The Assumptions of Crossed Panel Random-Effects Models






WarningAssumption: Year Effects are Exchangeable



What it means: The year random intercepts \(w_{0t}\) are assumed to be i.i.d. draws from \(\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_{\text{year}})\). This implies no autocorrelation across years — a good year does not predict the next year will also be good.
When it fails: In the presence of multi-year macroeconomic trends (sustained expansions or recessions), year effects are correlated over time. The remedy is to include a linear time trend as a fixed effect, or to model AR(1) residual structure (covered in Module 4).








WarningAssumption: Firm and Year Effects are Independent



What it means: A firm being highly productive should not predict whether it was observed during good or bad years. In observational panels with attrition (firms entering and exiting), this can fail — high-performing firms may be more likely to survive through downturns.
How to check: Examine whether firm-level characteristics (size, industry, age) are systematically different across years of entry and exit. If firms drop out preferentially during bad years, year random effects will be estimated from a selected sample.








WarningAssumption: Crossed Effects are Independent



What it means: The firm random effect and year random effect are assumed to be independent. This fails if certain types of firms are disproportionately observed in certain years (non-random panel composition over time).
How to check: Does the distribution of firm types change substantially over years in your panel? For example, if a regulatory change in year 8 causes small firms to exit the panel, the remaining firms in years 9–15 will be a selected sample. The firm and year random effects are no longer independent.




14.2.10 Experimental vs. Panel: Side-by-Side Summary








Dimension

Firm \(\times\) Year observation

Firm

title: 14  Overview
text: Module 2: Centering & Between/Within Effects

title: 14  Overview
text:
14.1 The Subtlest Assumption
You have built a mixed-effects model, checked your residuals, verified convergence, and are ready to interpret your coefficients. There is one more question you need to answer before you do:
What does your predictor’s value actually mean?
This sounds almost philosophical, but it has concrete, testable consequences. A predictor like “daily sleep hours” can mean two different things in a within-person longitudinal dataset:

Between-person effect: People who tend to sleep more also tend to have higher mood (perhaps because of a third factor — stable health, lifestyle, genetics — or because sleep causally improves mood on average across people)
Within-person effect: On days when this specific person sleeps more than their own average, their mood is higher

These two effects are not the same. They can point in opposite directions. They answer different scientific questions. And the standard random-intercept model, without careful centering, conflates them.
Module 2 is about learning to see the difference — and to model it explicitly.

title: 14  Overview
text:
14.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: Centering Your Variables introduces grand-mean centering (GMC) and cluster-mean centering (CMC), explains what each does to the interpretation of your coefficients, and provides a decision framework.
Part 2: Between-Unit vs. Within-Unit Effects shows how to simultaneously estimate both effects in a single model — the “Mundlak device” — and when the between and within effects diverge.
Part 3: Simpson’s Paradox uses vivid examples to show how the relationship at the aggregate level can be completely opposite to the relationship at the individual level. This is not a statistical curiosity — it is a systematic feature of nested data that researchers frequently misinterpret.

title: 14  Overview
text:
14.3 The Core Insight
Standard regression assumes your predictor has one meaning. In nested data, it has (at least) two: a within-unit meaning and a between-unit meaning. Centering is the tool that separates them.

“What do your means mean?” — this question, asked before every analysis, will save you from some of the most common and consequential errors in social science research.

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
text: 16 Centering as Decomposition

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
text:
16.1 One Variable, Two Mechanisms
When you measure “hours of sleep” across multiple days from multiple people, that variable carries two completely different stories at the same time:

The within-person story: On days when this person sleeps more than they usually do, how does their mood change?
The between-person story: Do people who sleep more overall have higher mood than people who sleep less?

These are different empirical questions. They may have different answers. They probably operate through different mechanisms — the within-person effect may reflect acute physiological recovery, while the between-person effect may reflect stable personality traits (conscientious people both sleep more and have better mood, not because sleep causes mood but because conscientiousness causes both).
Centering is not a cosmetic choice about where the intercept falls. It is a decision about which mechanism you are modeling — and whether you want to see both.

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
section: 16.2 The Equations: From Conflation to Decomposition
text:
16.2 The Equations: From Conflation to Decomposition

16.2.1 The Conflated Model
When you include an uncentered (or grand-mean-centered) predictor in a random-effects model, a single coefficient \(\beta_1\) blends two mechanisms:
\[
\underbrace{Y_{ij}}_{\text{outcome}} = \underbrace{\gamma_{00}}_{\substack{\text{grand} \\ \text{mean}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1}_{\substack{\text{blended} \\ \text{slope}}} \underbrace{X_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{predictor}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{person} \\ \text{RE}}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{residual}}}
\]
\(\beta_1\) absorbs variation from two sources simultaneously. Its value depends on which source dominates and how the two correlate.


16.2.2 The Decomposition
CMC separates the predictor into its two components before entering the model:
\[
\underbrace{X_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{observed} \\ \text{predictor}}} = \underbrace{(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{j})}_{\substack{\text{within-unit} \\ \text{deviation}}} + \underbrace{\bar{X}_{j}}_{\substack{\text{unit mean} \\ \text{(between)}}}
\]
Adding both as separate predictors gives the decomposed model. Notice how the single \(\beta_1\) from before splits into two:
\[
\underbrace{Y_{ij}}_{\text{outcome}} = \underbrace{\gamma_{00}}_{\substack{\text{grand} \\ \text{mean}}} + \underbrace{\beta_W}_{\substack{\text{within-unit} \\ \text{slope}}} \underbrace{(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_j)}_{\substack{\text{deviation from} \\ \text{own mean}}} + \underbrace{\beta_B}_{\substack{\text{between-unit} \\ \text{slope}}} \underbrace{\bar{X}_j}_{\substack{\text{unit's} \\ \text{average level}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{person} \\ \text{RE}}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{residual}}}
\]
Now \(\beta_W\) answers the within-person question and \(\beta_B\) answers the between-person question. They are allowed to be completely different numbers with different interpretations.






NoteThe DGP Interpretation



The decomposed model is a hypothesis about the DGP: it asserts that two distinct mechanisms are at work, one operating at the within-unit level and one at the between-unit level. If \(\beta_W \neq \beta_B\), the DGP truly has two mechanisms. If \(\beta_W \approx \beta_B\), a single mechanism may span both levels — but you only know this after estimating the decomposed model and testing whether they differ.

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
text:
16.3 Three Archetypal Patterns
The most important practical question is: how different are \(\beta_W\) and \(\beta_B\)?

16.3.1 Pattern 1: Convergent Effects — Exercise and Wellbeing
Both mechanisms point the same direction with similar magnitude. The within-person mechanism (acute endorphins, reduced rumination) and the between-person mechanism (fit people have better overall routines and self-efficacy) both increase wellbeing. True: \(\beta_W \approx \beta_B \approx 3\).


Code
n_ppl <- 60; n_days <- 10

person_ex_avg <- rnorm(n_ppl, 45, 12)
person_wb_base <- 50 + 0.3 * person_ex_avg + rnorm(n_ppl, 0, 6)

df_ex <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_ppl, day = 1:n_days) |>
  mutate(
    exercise  = person_ex_avg[person] + rnorm(n(), 0, 8),
    wellbeing = person_wb_base[person] +
                3 * (exercise - person_ex_avg[person]) +
                rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    person = factor(person)
  ) |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(ex_mean = mean(exercise), ex_cmc = exercise - ex_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_ex <- lmer(wellbeing ~ ex_cmc + ex_mean + (1 | person), data = df_ex)
broom.mixed::tidy(m_ex, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(mechanism = c("Within-person", "Between-person")) |>
  select(mechanism, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Pattern 1: within ≈ between ≈ 3. One coherent mechanism at two levels.")



Pattern 1: within ≈ between ≈ 3. One coherent mechanism at two levels.


mechanism
estimate
std.error
p.value




Within-person
2.996
0.030
0


Between-person
0.438
0.089
0





Here, the pooled model would give you a decent approximation — but you only know that after running the decomposition. The convergence itself is substantively informative: the same process operates at both timescales.


16.3.2 Pattern 2: Divergent Effects — Income and Life Satisfaction (Easterlin Paradox)
At the between-person level, wealthier people report substantially higher life satisfaction. But within individuals over time, income growth has a surprisingly modest effect — adaptation, shifting aspirations, and social comparison dampen the within-person response. True: \(\beta_W \approx 0.3\), \(\beta_B \approx 1.5\).


Code
n_ppl2 <- 80; n_yrs <- 8

person_inc_avg  <- rnorm(n_ppl2, 50, 15)
person_sat_base <- 50 + 1.5 * person_inc_avg + rnorm(n_ppl2, 0, 8)

df_inc <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_ppl2, year = 1:n_yrs) |>
  mutate(
    income   = person_inc_avg[person] + rnorm(n(), 0, 6),
    life_sat = person_sat_base[person] +
               0.3 * (income - person_inc_avg[person]) + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    person   = factor(person)
  ) |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(inc_mean = mean(income), inc_cmc = income - inc_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_inc    <- lmer(life_sat ~ inc_cmc + inc_mean + (1 | person), data = df_inc)
m_pooled <- lmer(life_sat ~ income  + (1 | person), data = df_inc)

bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_pooled, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "income") |> mutate(model = "Pooled (blended)"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_inc, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "inc_cmc") |> mutate(model = "Within-person"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_inc, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "inc_mean") |> mutate(model = "Between-person")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Pattern 2: pooled ≈ 1.2, within ≈ 0.3, between ≈ 1.5. Pooled is wrong for both questions.")



Pattern 2: pooled ≈ 1.2, within ≈ 0.3, between ≈ 1.5. Pooled is wrong for both questions.


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Pooled (blended)
0.380
0.026
0


Within-person

Between-person
1.469

The pooled model gives ~1.2 — it overstates the within-person effect by 4× and understates the between-person effect. A policy conclusion based on this (“raising incomes will substantially improve wellbeing”) relies on the between-person relationship but must deliver on the within-person mechanism.


16.3.3 Pattern 3: Sign Reversal — Study Hours and Grades
Within a student, studying more than their own average helps their grades. But between programs, the harder programs have students who study more and earn lower grades — because the programs are harder, not because studying hurts. True: \(\beta_W \approx +2\), \(\beta_B \approx -3\).


Code
n_students <- 200; n_programs <- 5
prog_difficulty <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10)
prog_study_avg  <- c(8, 7, 6, 5, 4)

df_study <- tibble(
  student = 1:n_students,
  program = rep(1:n_programs, each = n_students / n_programs)
) |>
  mutate(
    study = prog_study_avg[program] + rnorm(n_students, 0, 0.8),
    grade = 80 - 3 * prog_difficulty[program] +
            2 * (study - prog_study_avg[program]) + rnorm(n_students, 0, 3),
    program = factor(program)
  ) |>
  group_by(program) |>
  mutate(study_mean = mean(study), study_cmc = study - study_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_study_decomp <- lmer(grade ~ study_cmc + study_mean + (1 | program), data = df_study)
m_study_pooled <- lm(grade ~ study, data = df_study)

bind_rows(
  broom::tidy(m_study_pooled) |>
    filter(term == "study") |> mutate(model = "Pooled OLS"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_study_decomp, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "study_cmc") |> mutate(model = "Within-program"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_study_decomp, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "study_mean") |> mutate(model = "Between-program")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Pattern 3: pooled is negative; within is positive; between is strongly negative. Sign reversal!")



Pattern 3: pooled is negative; within is positive; between is strongly negative. Sign reversal!


model

Code
p1 <- ggplot(df_study, aes(x = study, y = grade)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = program), alpha = 0.4, size = 1.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "black", linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Pooled: studying MORE → LOWER grades?",
       subtitle = "Slope is negative — a sign reversal",
       x = "Study hours/week", y = "Grade", color = "Program") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p2 <- ggplot(df_study, aes(x = study, y = grade, color = program)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Within-program: studying MORE → HIGHER grades",
       subtitle = "Within every program, the slope is positive",
       x = "Study hours/week", y = "Grade", color = "Program") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p1 + p2









The pooled model says studying hurts — which is wrong. The decomposition reveals the truth: within every program, studying helps, but harder programs demand more studying and are harder by definition. Ignoring the nesting produces a Simpson’s Paradox (see Part 3).

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
text:
16.4 The Sleep-Mood Example: Applying Decomposition


Code
n_people <- 50; n_days2 <- 7

person_sleep_avg  <- rnorm(n_people, 7, 1)
person_mood_base  <- 40 + 5 * person_sleep_avg + rnorm(n_people, 0, 5)

df <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, day = 1:n_days2) |>
  mutate(
    sleep_raw = person_sleep_avg[person] + rnorm(n(), 0, 1),
    mood      = person_mood_base[person] +
                2 * (sleep_raw - person_sleep_avg[person]) + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    person    = factor(person)
  ) |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(sleep_mean = mean(sleep_raw), sleep_cmc = sleep_raw - sleep_mean) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(sleep_gmc = sleep_raw - mean(sleep_raw))

m_uncentered <- lmer(mood ~ sleep_raw  + (1 | person), data = df)
m_gmc        <- lmer(mood ~ sleep_gmc  + (1 | person), data = df)
m_cmc        <- lmer(mood ~ sleep_cmc + sleep_mean + (1 | person), data = df)

bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_uncentered, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "sleep_raw") |>
    mutate(model = "Uncentered", mechanism = "Blended"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_gmc, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "sleep_gmc") |>
    mutate(model = "GMC", mechanism = "Blended"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_cmc, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "sleep_cmc") |>
    mutate(model = "CMC", mechanism = "Within-person"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_cmc, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "sleep_mean") |>
    mutate(model = "CMC", mechanism = "Between-person")
) |>
  select(model, mechanism, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Sleep → mood: blended ≈ 4.0; within ≈ 2.0; between ≈ 5.0. Neither blended estimate is correct.")



Sleep → mood: blended ≈ 4.0; within ≈ 2.0; between ≈ 5.0. Neither blended estimate is correct.

The uncentered and GMC models give ~4, which is wrong for both questions. CMC reveals: the within-person mechanism is modest (2 points per extra hour of sleep); the between-person correlation is much larger (5 points) because healthy lifestyle confounds it.

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
text:
16.5 Decision Guide






TipWhich Centering to Use











Goal
Centering
What you get




Interpret the intercept at a meaningful value
GMC
Intercept = E[Y] when X = grand mean


Estimate the within-unit effect
CMC
\(\hat\beta_W\) — deviation from own mean


Separate within and between mechanisms
CMC + unit mean
\(\hat\beta_W\) and \(\hat\beta_B\)


Test if within = between
CMC + unit mean
Wald test on equality


Single-level data (no nesting)
Either
No within/between distinction



Default recommendation for nested data: Always use CMC with the unit mean included. Run it, check whether \(\hat\beta_W \approx \hat\beta_B\), and only then decide whether the pooled model is defensible.








WarningAssumption: No Conflation of Within and Between Effects



Including a predictor without CMC in a random-effects model implicitly assumes \(\beta_W = \beta_B\). When this is wrong — as in Patterns 2 and 3 above — both estimates are biased.
How to test: Fit the CMC model. Compare \(\hat\beta_W\) and \(\hat\beta_B\). A Wald test (see Part 2) tests the null \(H_0: \beta_W = \beta_B\).
The stakes: In the worst case (Pattern 3), conflation completely reverses the direction of the effect.

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
text: 17 Separating Within and Between Effects
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
text:
17.1 The Mundlak Device
Part 1 showed that a predictor in nested data carries two mechanisms. The formal statistical tool for separating them is known as the Mundlak device (Mundlak, 1978) or equivalently the within-between (WB) model.
The key equation — building on Part 1’s decomposition — is:
\[
\underbrace{Y_{ij}}_{\text{outcome}} = \underbrace{\gamma_{00}}_{\substack{\text{grand} \\ \text{mean}}} + \underbrace{\beta_W (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_j)}_{\substack{\text{within-unit effect} \\ \text{(deviation from own mean)}}} + \underbrace{\beta_B \bar{X}_j}_{\substack{\text{between-unit effect} \\ \text{(unit's average level)}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{unit random} \\ \text{intercept}}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{residual}}}
\]
The Mundlak insight: this model is equivalent to including the unit mean \(\bar{X}_j\) alongside the raw predictor \(X_{ij}\) in a random-effects model. The coefficient on \(X_{ij}\) then estimates the within-unit effect, and the coefficient on \(\bar{X}_j\) estimates the additional between-unit effect beyond the within-unit effect.
Both are valid. The CMC parameterization (Part 1) gives \(\beta_W\) and \(\beta_B\) directly and makes interpretation cleaner. The Mundlak parameterization adds \(\bar{X}_j\) alongside the raw \(X_{ij}\) and gives a test of whether \(\beta_W = \beta_B\).
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
section: 17.2 Example 1: The Easterlin Paradox — GDP and Wellbeing
text:
17.2 Example 1: The Easterlin Paradox — GDP and Wellbeing
The classic motivating case. Between countries, richer nations are happier. Within countries over time, economic growth has a modest and inconsistent effect on wellbeing. Two different mechanisms, same predictor.


Code
n_countries <- 40; n_years <- 10

country_gdp_avg       <- rnorm(n_countries, 50, 15)
country_wellbeing_base <- 50 + 0.6 * country_gdp_avg + rnorm(n_countries, 0, 8)

df_gdp <- expand_grid(country = 1:n_countries, year = 1:n_years) |>
  mutate(
    gdp_raw   = country_gdp_avg[country] + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    wellbeing = country_wellbeing_base[country] +
                0.15 * (gdp_raw - country_gdp_avg[country]) + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    country   = factor(country)
  ) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  mutate(gdp_mean = mean(gdp_raw), gdp_cmc = gdp_raw - gdp_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_pooled  <- lmer(wellbeing ~ gdp_raw + (1 | country), data = df_gdp)
m_mundlak <- lmer(wellbeing ~ gdp_cmc + gdp_mean + (1 | country), data = df_gdp)

bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_pooled,  effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "gdp_raw") |> mutate(model = "Pooled"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_mundlak, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "gdp_cmc")  |> mutate(model = "Within-country"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_mundlak, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "gdp_mean") |> mutate(model = "Between-country")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "GDP → wellbeing: pooled ~0.45 blends within ~0.15 and between ~0.60")



GDP → wellbeing: pooled ~0.45 blends within ~0.15 and between ~0.60


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Pooled
0.252
0.039
0


Within-country
0.187
0.041
0


Between-country
0.683
0.082
0





Interpretation: - Within-country (gdp_cmc ≈ 0.15): In years when GDP is above a country’s own average, wellbeing ticks up slightly. Modest — consistent with hedonic adaptation and shifting aspirations. - Between-country (gdp_mean ≈ 0.60): Countries with permanently higher GDP have substantially higher wellbeing. But this reflects institutions, infrastructure, healthcare, and social safety nets that co-occur with wealth — not just income itself. - Pooled (~0.45): An average that describes neither mechanism accurately.
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
section: 17.3 Example 2: Within-Subjects Experiments — Trait vs. State Effects
text:
17.3 Example 2: Within-Subjects Experiments — Trait vs. State Effects
In a within-subjects experiment, the same decomposition applies — but now the “cluster” is the participant and the nesting is observations within person.
Design: 80 participants each evaluate 10 charity appeals. Half feature statistical evidence; half feature personal stories. Outcome: whether they donate (continuous willingness-to-donate scale, 0–100).
The within-person effect (\(\beta_W\)): For a given participant, do story-based appeals get higher donations than statistical appeals — compared to that participant’s own average donation?
The between-person effect (\(\beta_B\)): Do participants who are exposed to more story-based appeals (by random assignment variation) tend to donate more overall? This would pick up trait-level empathizers who respond to everything more generously.
With random assignment, \(\beta_B\) should be near zero — randomization ensures that more story-exposed participants are not systematically more empathetic. A large \(\beta_B\) is a red flag.


Code
n_p_exp <- 80; n_appeals <- 10

# Some participants are trait high-empathy: they donate more AND
# in this (imperfect) design, happen to see more story appeals
person_empathy     <- rnorm(n_p_exp, 50, 10)
person_story_prop  <- 0.5 + 0.01 * (person_empathy - 50) + rnorm(n_p_exp, 0, 0.05)
person_story_prop  <- pmin(pmax(person_story_prop, 0), 1)
person_base_donate <- 40 + 0.8 * person_empathy + rnorm(n_p_exp, 0, 8)

df_exp <- expand_grid(participant = 1:n_p_exp, appeal = 1:n_appeals) |>
  mutate(
    story = rbinom(n(), 1, person_story_prop[participant]),
    donate = person_base_donate[participant] +
             5 * (story - person_story_prop[participant]) +
             rnorm(n(), 0, 6),
    participant = factor(participant)
  ) |>
  group_by(participant) |>
  mutate(story_mean = mean(story), story_cmc = story - story_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_exp <- lmer(donate ~ story_cmc + story_mean + (1 | participant), data = df_exp)
broom.mixed::tidy(m_exp, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(mechanism = c("Within-person (causal effect)", "Between-person (trait confound)")) |>
  select(mechanism, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Story vs. stats: within ≈ 5 (treatment effect); between ≈ near zero (good randomization)")



Story vs. stats: within ≈ 5 (treatment effect); between ≈ near zero (good randomization)


mechanism
estimate
std.error
p.value




Within-person (causal effect)
4.569
0.462
0


Between-person (trait confound)
27.913
5.476
0





With good randomization, \(\beta_B \approx 0\) and \(\beta_W\) recovers the true treatment effect. A significant \(\beta_B\) would suggest a randomization failure or a design confound — worth always checking in within-subjects designs.
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
section: 17.4 Example 3: Ecological Fallacy — Education and Fertility
text:
17.4 Example 3: Ecological Fallacy — Education and Fertility
Between countries: more educated countries have lower fertility (strong negative relationship). Within countries over time: rising education predicts declining fertility (also negative). Here the signs align, but the magnitudes and mechanisms differ.
The between-country effect reflects structural factors: cultural norms, female labor force participation, urbanization, and healthcare access all co-vary with education across nations.
The within-country effect over time reflects a causal mechanism: as individual women gain education, they delay marriage, have fewer children, and invest more per child.
Same direction, but if you use the cross-national correlation to inform policy about what happens when you increase education within a country, you are using the wrong estimate — the between-country relationship is confounded by dozens of co-varying structural factors.


Code
n_countries_ef <- 50; n_years_ef <- 8

country_edu_avg <- rnorm(n_countries_ef, 60, 12)
country_fert_base <- 4.5 - 0.05 * country_edu_avg + rnorm(n_countries_ef, 0, 0.4)

df_ef <- expand_grid(country = 1:n_countries_ef, year = 1:n_years_ef) |>
  mutate(
    education = country_edu_avg[country] + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    fertility = country_fert_base[country] -
                0.01 * (education - country_edu_avg[country]) + rnorm(n(), 0, 0.2),
    country   = factor(country)
  ) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  mutate(edu_mean = mean(education), edu_cmc = education - edu_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_ef <- lmer(fertility ~ edu_cmc + edu_mean + (1 | country), data = df_ef)
broom.mixed::tidy(m_ef, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(mechanism = c("Within-country over time", "Between-country cross-section")) |>
  select(mechanism, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Education → fertility: both negative, but between is 5× larger due to structural confounds")



Education → fertility: both negative, but between is 5× larger due to structural confounds


mechanism
estimate
std.error
p.value




Within-country over time
-0.010
0.002
0


Between-country cross-section
-0.051
0.004
0





The between-country estimate is 5× larger than the within-country estimate. Using the cross-sectional number to predict what happens when you raise education within a country would dramatically overstate the policy effect.
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
section: 17.5 Example 4: Convergent Effects — Training and Performance
text:
17.5 Example 4: Convergent Effects — Training and Performance
Not every decomposition reveals a divergence. When within and between effects are approximately equal, this is itself a substantive finding: the same mechanism operates at both levels.
Employee training hours → job performance. Within an employee: weeks where they receive more training, their performance ticks up (skill acquisition). Between employees: those who invest more in training overall also perform better (both due to training AND because high performers seek out training). Both effects are positive and of similar magnitude.


Code
n_emp <- 100; n_weeks <- 8

emp_train_avg   <- rnorm(n_emp, 4, 1.5)
emp_perf_base   <- 50 + 3 * emp_train_avg + rnorm(n_emp, 0, 6)

df_train <- expand_grid(employee = 1:n_emp, week = 1:n_weeks) |>
  mutate(
    training    = pmax(0, emp_train_avg[employee] + rnorm(n(), 0, 1)),
    performance = emp_perf_base[employee] +
                  2.8 * (training - emp_train_avg[employee]) + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    employee    = factor(employee)
  ) |>
  group_by(employee) |>
  mutate(train_mean = mean(training), train_cmc = training - train_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_train <- lmer(performance ~ train_cmc + train_mean + (1 | employee), data = df_train)
broom.mixed::tidy(m_train, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(mechanism = c("Within-employee", "Between-employee")) |>
  select(mechanism, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Training → performance: within ≈ between ≈ 3. Pooled model is approximately correct here.")



Training → performance: within ≈ between ≈ 3. Pooled model is approximately correct here.


mechanism
estimate
std.error
p.value




Within-employee
2.790
0.194
0


Between-employee
2.035
0.387
0





When \(\beta_W \approx \beta_B\), the pooled model is defensible. But again: you only know this after running the decomposition. The convergence is informative — it suggests training itself (not some trait that drives both training and performance) is the active mechanism.
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
section: 17.6 Testing Whether Within and Between Effects Are Equal
text:
17.6 Testing Whether Within and Between Effects Are Equal
The formal test is a Wald test on the null hypothesis \(H_0: \beta_W = \beta_B\):


Code
# Test on the Easterlin model: are within and between GDP effects equal?
linearHypothesis(m_mundlak, "gdp_cmc = gdp_mean")






Df
Chisq
Pr(>Chisq)




NA
NA
NA


1
29.48468
1e-07






A significant result (p < 0.05) means you must report both effects separately — the pooled model is misspecified. A non-significant result means you cannot rule out that the effects are equal, and a pooled model may be defensible (though the CMC model is always more informative).
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
text:
17.7 A Decision Table






TipWhat to Report: Within, Between, or Both?












Pattern
\(\beta_W\) vs. \(\beta_B\)
Wald test
What to report




Convergent
Similar magnitude and sign
p > .05
Pooled estimate is OK, but note the decomposition confirmed equivalence


Divergent
Different magnitudes
p < .05
Report both; discuss different mechanisms


Sign reversal
Opposite signs
p < .05
Report both; warn about Simpson’s Paradox


Experimental
\(\beta_B \approx 0\), \(\beta_W\) = treatment effect
p < .05
Report within as the treatment effect; \(\beta_B\) is a randomization check



Always run the decomposed model first. Use the Wald test to decide whether to simplify. Do not start with the pooled model.








WarningAssumption: Correct Level Attribution



Including a variable as within-unit (CMC) or between-unit (unit mean) requires knowing which level it genuinely varies at. A variable that appears to vary within units but is actually stable should be treated as a level-2 predictor.
How to check: Compute the ICC of the predictor itself. High ICC (>0.70) means most variation is between units; CMC will give you noisy, low-variance within deviations. Low ICC means the variable genuinely varies at the within-unit level.
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
text: 18 Simpson’s Paradox

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
text:
18.1 When the Aggregate Lies
Simpson’s Paradox occurs when a relationship that exists in the aggregate data reverses (or dramatically changes) when you look within subgroups. It is not a statistical quirk — it is a direct consequence of ignoring nesting, and it is surprisingly common in social science data.
This chapter demonstrates Simpson’s Paradox in three distinct research contexts:

Cross-sectional nesting — the classic case: students nested in programs
Longitudinal panel data — the within vs. between paradox: income and life satisfaction
Within-subjects experiments — the stimulus confound: memory and emotional valence

Each context has a different structural cause and a different remedy. Together, they establish a single diagnostic principle: always decompose before you report.

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
section: 18.2 Context 1: Cross-Sectional Nesting — The Classic Case
text:
18.2 Context 1: Cross-Sectional Nesting — The Classic Case

18.2.1 The Programs × Study Hours Example
Students in harder academic programs study more AND receive lower grades — because the programs are harder, not because studying hurts. When you pool across programs, the between-program confound dominates the within-program relationship.


Code
n_students  <- 200
n_programs  <- 5

program_difficulty <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10)   # harder programs lower average grades
program_study_avg  <- c(8, 7, 6, 5, 4)    # harder programs, students study more

students <- tibble(
  student  = 1:n_students,
  program  = rep(1:n_programs, each = n_students / n_programs),
  study    = program_study_avg[program] + rnorm(n_students, 0, 0.8),
  grade    = 80 - 3 * program_difficulty[program] +
             2 * (study - program_study_avg[program]) +
             rnorm(n_students, 0, 3),
  program_label = factor(program, labels = paste("Program", 1:n_programs))
)




Code
p_pooled <- ggplot(students, aes(x = study, y = grade)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = program_label), alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "black", linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Pooled: studying MORE predicts LOWER grades?!",
       x = "Study hours/week", y = "Grade", color = "Program") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_within <- ggplot(students, aes(x = study, y = grade, color = program_label)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Within program: studying MORE predicts HIGHER grades",
       x = "Study hours/week", y = "Grade", color = "Program") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_pooled / p_within









The pooled slope is negative: students who study more get lower grades. The truth: students in harder programs both study more AND earn lower grades — because the programs are harder. Within any given program, studying more helps. The aggregate correlation is driven entirely by the confound between program difficulty and study time.
The fix: Use a mixed model that explicitly estimates the within-program effect:


Code
m_pooled <- lm(grade ~ study, data = students)
m_mixed  <- lmer(grade ~ study + (1 | program_label), data = students)

tibble(
  Model         = c("Pooled OLS", "Mixed model (within-program)"),
  Study_coeff   = c(coef(m_pooled)["study"],
                    fixef(m_mixed)["study"]),
  Direction     = c("Negative (wrong)", "Positive (correct)")
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Study hours coefficient: pooled vs. within-program")



Study hours coefficient: pooled vs. within-program


Model
Study_coeff
Direction




Pooled OLS
4.889
Negative (wrong)


Mixed model (within-program)
1.745
Positive (correct)







18.2.2 Real-World Examples of Cross-Sectional Simpson’s Paradox
Berkeley admissions (1973): Across departments, women were admitted at lower rates than men. Within departments, women were admitted at the same or higher rates. The paradox arose because women applied disproportionately to competitive departments.
COVID-19 mortality: In some early analyses, countries with more advanced healthcare appeared to have higher mortality rates. Within matched patient groups (controlling for age and severity), better healthcare reduced mortality. The aggregate correlation was driven by age confounds.
Wages across demographic groups: Total wages can fall for every group while rising in the aggregate, if people are shifting from lower-paid to higher-paid occupations.

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
section: 18.3 Context 2: Longitudinal Panel Data — The Within vs. Between Paradox
text:
18.3 Context 2: Longitudinal Panel Data — The Within vs. Between Paradox

18.3.1 Income and Life Satisfaction: The Easterlin Paradox
Simpson’s Paradox in panel data is so common it has its own name: the Easterlin Paradox — and it is the core motivation for the CMC/GMC decomposition covered in Module 2 Parts 1–2.
At any given point in time, richer people report higher life satisfaction. The cross-sectional (between-person) correlation is strongly positive. This has been used to argue that income causes happiness — that policies increasing income will increase well-being.
But when you follow the same people over time, earning more than usual barely predicts higher satisfaction. The within-person effect is near zero. This is the Easterlin Paradox: nations get richer over decades without becoming much happier; individuals earn more over their careers without becoming much happier.
The pooled OLS regression conflates these two very different stories into a single, misleading number.


18.3.2 Simulation: 100 People × 8 Annual Waves


Code
n_people <- 100
n_waves  <- 8

person_income_mean <- rnorm(n_people, 50, 15)  # stable between-person income level

df_panel_sp <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, wave = 1:n_waves) |>
  mutate(
    person_mean  = person_income_mean[person],
    income       = person_mean + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),       # small within-person variation
    income_gmc   = income - mean(income),                # grand-mean centered
    income_cmc   = income - person_mean,                 # within-person centered
    income_mean  = person_mean - mean(person_income_mean), # person mean centered on grand mean
    # True DGP: large between-person effect, small within-person effect
    satisfaction = 5 + 3.0 * income_mean + 0.4 * income_cmc + rnorm(n(), 0, 2),
    person       = factor(person)
  )




Code
# 1. Pooled OLS: conflates between and within
m_pooled <- lm(satisfaction ~ income_gmc, data = df_panel_sp)

# 2. Between-person: regress person means on person mean income
person_means <- df_panel_sp |>
  group_by(person) |>
  summarise(
    across(c(satisfaction, income_mean), mean),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

m_between <- lm(satisfaction ~ income_mean, data = person_means)

# 3. Within-person (CMC mixed model)
m_within  <- lmer(satisfaction ~ income_cmc + (1 | person), data = df_panel_sp)

# Summary table
tibble(
  Model          = c("Pooled OLS", "Between-person", "Within-person (CMC)"),
  Income_slope   = c(coef(m_pooled)["income_gmc"],
                     coef(m_between)["income_mean"],
                     fixef(m_within)["income_cmc"]),
  Interpretation = c("Blend of between and within (misleading)",
                     "Richer people are happier (selection effect)",
                     "Earning more than usual barely helps (causal estimate)")
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Income–satisfaction slope: pooled vs. decomposed")



Income–satisfaction slope: pooled vs. decomposed







Model
Income_slope
Interpretation




Pooled OLS
2.764
Blend of between and within (misleading)


Between-person
2.988
Richer people are happier (selection effect)


Within-person (CMC)
0.402
Earning more than usual barely helps (causal estimate)







18.3.3 Visualizing the Easterlin Paradox


Code
# Sample 25 people for visual clarity
sampled_people <- sample(levels(df_panel_sp$person), 25)

p_spaghetti <- df_panel_sp |>
  filter(person %in% sampled_people) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = income, y = satisfaction, group = person)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3, color = "#4393c3") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, color = "#4393c3", size = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(data = df_panel_sp |> filter(person %in% sampled_people),
              aes(group = NULL), method = "lm",
              color = "black", linewidth = 1.2, se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Panel: income and life satisfaction",
       subtitle = "Blue lines = within-person trajectories (nearly flat, \u03b2\u2248 0.4)\nBlack line = pooled OLS (follows between-person differences)",
       x = "Income", y = "Life satisfaction") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_between_scatter <- person_means |>
  ggplot(aes(x = income_mean, y = satisfaction)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = "#d73027") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#d73027", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Between-person: richer people report higher satisfaction",
       subtitle = paste0("Slope \u2248 ", round(coef(m_between)["income_mean"], 2),
                         " — large, but reflects WHO is rich, not what income does"),
       x = "Person-mean income (centered)", y = "Person-mean satisfaction") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_spaghetti / p_between_scatter









The spaghetti plot makes the paradox visible: individual trajectories over time are nearly flat (low within-person effect \(\beta_W \approx 0.4\)), but the scatter of person-level averages shows a strong positive slope (\(\beta_B \approx 3.0\)). The pooled OLS line follows the person-means, not the within-person trajectories.


18.3.4 Why This Is Simpson’s Paradox
The aggregate (pooled) relationship would suggest that policies raising income improve wellbeing substantially. But the within-person relationship reveals that once you account for who is rich versus poor, income gains do almost nothing for wellbeing.
The policy implication: A policy that increases everyone’s income by 10% does not produce the 3.0-point wellbeing gain that cross-sectional data suggests — it produces the 0.4-point gain that within-person data reveals. Ignoring nesting leads to catastrophically wrong policy predictions.


18.3.5 Connection to Module 2: The CMC/GMC Fix
The CMC/GMC decomposition is precisely the solution:
m_full <- lmer(satisfaction ~ income_cmc + income_mean + (1 | person),
               data = df_panel_sp)
This model recovers both the within-person effect (income_cmc \(\approx 0.4\)) and the between-person effect (income_mean \(\approx 3.0\)) simultaneously, making the paradox explicit and the decomposition clean. This is exactly what Module 2 Parts 1 and 2 covered.






ImportantThe Panel Data Rule



In any longitudinal analysis, run both pooled OLS and a within-person model (fixed effects or CMC mixed model). If the coefficients differ substantially, you are in Simpson’s Paradox territory. Report both. Explain which one answers your research question.

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
section: 18.4 Context 3: Within-Subjects Experiments — The Stimulus Confound
text:
18.4 Context 3: Within-Subjects Experiments — The Stimulus Confound

18.4.1 Memory, Emotional Valence, and Concrete Words
Simpson’s Paradox can appear even in experiments when stimuli are not properly counterbalanced — when certain conditions happen to receive stimuli that systematically differ on a key attribute.
The scenario: A researcher tests whether positive emotional content improves memory recall. 50 participants each study 30 stimuli: 15 positive-valence words and 15 neutral-valence words.
The flaw: Positive stimuli happen to be more concrete (higher imageability — easier to visualize: “puppy”, “sunset”, “garden”). Neutral stimuli are more abstract (“justice”, “theory”, “process”). Concreteness is a well-established predictor of memory — concrete words are better recalled regardless of valence.
The result: Pooled across participants, positive words are better recalled. But this is entirely because they are more concrete. Within each concreteness level, valence has NO effect on recall. This is Simpson’s Paradox at the stimulus level.


Code
n_par  <- 50
n_stim <- 30

par_effects  <- rnorm(n_par,  0, 5)
stim_effects <- rnorm(n_stim, 0, 4)
valence      <- rep(c(0, 1), times = n_stim / 2)   # 0 = neutral, 1 = positive
concreteness <- 3 + 2 * valence + rnorm(n_stim, 0, 1)  # confound: positive = more concrete

df_exp_sp <- expand_grid(participant = 1:n_par, stimulus = 1:n_stim) |>
  mutate(
    par_eff  = par_effects[participant],
    stim_eff = stim_effects[stimulus],
    val_s    = valence[stimulus],
    conc_s   = concreteness[stimulus],
    # True DGP: concreteness matters; valence has NO direct effect
    recall   = 50 + par_eff + stim_eff + 2 * conc_s + rnorm(n(), 0, 3),
    participant = factor(participant),
    stimulus    = factor(stimulus)
  )




18.4.2 Fitting the Wrong and Correct Models


Code
# Wrong model: ignores stimulus-level confound and stimulus random effects
m_wrong   <- lmer(recall ~ val_s + (1 | participant),
                  data = df_exp_sp)

# Correct model: controls for concreteness + crossed stimulus RE
m_correct <- lmer(recall ~ val_s + conc_s + (1 | participant) + (1 | stimulus),
                  data = df_exp_sp)

# Compare valence coefficients
tidy_wrong   <- broom.mixed::tidy(m_wrong,   effects = "fixed")
tidy_correct <- broom.mixed::tidy(m_correct, effects = "fixed")

bind_rows(
  tidy_wrong   |> filter(term == "val_s") |>
    mutate(model = "Wrong (no concreteness, no stimulus RE)"),
  tidy_correct |> filter(term == "val_s") |>
    mutate(model = "Correct (concreteness + stimulus RE)")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, statistic) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Valence coefficient: false positive vs. null result (statistic = t-value)")



Valence coefficient: false positive vs. null result (statistic = t-value)








model
estimate
std.error
statistic




Wrong (no concreteness, no stimulus RE)
1.813
0.304
5.958


Correct (concreteness + stimulus RE)
-1.946
2.015
-0.966





The wrong model finds a statistically significant positive valence effect. The correct model — which includes concreteness as a covariate and crossed stimulus random effects — shows that valence has no effect. The apparent memory advantage of positive words disappears entirely once the concreteness confound is controlled.


18.4.3 Visualizing the Stimulus Confound


Code
stimulus_summary <- df_exp_sp |>
  mutate(valence_label = ifelse(val_s == 1, "positive", "neutral")) |>
  group_by(stimulus, valence_label, conc_s) |>
  summarise(mean_recall = mean(recall), .groups = "drop")

p_valence_raw <- ggplot(stimulus_summary,
                        aes(x = valence_label, y = mean_recall,
                            color = valence_label)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, alpha = 0.6, size = 2) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "crossbar", width = 0.3, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("positive" = "#d73027", "neutral" = "#4393c3")) +
  labs(title = "Apparent effect: positive words recalled more",
       subtitle = "This looks like a valence effect...",
       x = "Valence", y = "Mean recall", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p_concreteness <- ggplot(stimulus_summary,
                         aes(x = conc_s, y = mean_recall,
                             color = valence_label)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("positive" = "#d73027", "neutral" = "#4393c3")) +
  labs(title = "True structure: concreteness drives recall",
       subtitle = "...but both lines have the same slope; valence just shifts concreteness",
       x = "Concreteness (imageability)", y = "Mean recall",
       color = "Valence") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_valence_raw | p_concreteness









The right panel is diagnostic: the two regression lines (positive and neutral) are nearly parallel — same slope for concreteness — and the gap between them narrows within each concreteness band. Positive words appear higher overall only because they occupy higher positions on the concreteness axis.


18.4.4 Why This Is Simpson’s Paradox
Within each concreteness level, positive and neutral stimuli have the same recall. But pooled across all stimuli, positive words look better because they happen to be more concrete. This is precisely the Simpson’s Paradox structure: the within-group relationship (null) and the aggregate relationship (positive) point in different directions.


18.4.5 The Fix

Design solution: Properly counterbalance stimuli across conditions — match positive and neutral words on concreteness, length, frequency, arousal, and other relevant attributes. This removes the confound at the data collection stage.
Analysis solution: Include crossed stimulus random effects (Module 1 Part 6) and known stimulus attributes as covariates. This partials out the stimulus-level confound after the fact — but requires that the confounding attribute was measured.

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
section: 18.5 Synthesis: The Diagnosis — Always Decompose Before You Report
text:
18.5 Synthesis: The Diagnosis — Always Decompose Before You Report
Simpson’s Paradox is not a special case. It is the predictable outcome whenever:

The predictor varies systematically across groups
The outcome varies systematically across groups
The group-level and unit-level relationships point in different directions


18.5.1 Practical Checklist
Cross-sectional nested data:

Compare the pooled coefficient to the within-cluster coefficient
If they differ in sign or by more than 50%, you have a Simpson’s Paradox
Report the within-cluster coefficient; explain the between-cluster confound

Panel data:

Always run both pooled OLS and a within-person model (FE or CMC)
Compare \(\hat{\beta}_W\) vs. \(\hat{\beta}_B\): if they differ substantially, decompose and report both
Ask: does the policy question target within-person change or between-person differences?

Within-subjects experiments:

Include crossed stimulus random effects in all models
Check whether stimuli are balanced on key attributes (frequency, concreteness, arousal, length) across conditions
Include item-level covariates to control post-hoc stimulus confounds







ImportantThe Universal Check



Before reporting any correlation or regression coefficient from nested data, ask: Does this relationship hold in the same direction within units as it does across units?
If not, you are reporting a Simpson’s Paradox. The CMC/GMC decomposition (Module 2) is the systematic fix.




18.5.2 Three-Panel Summary Visualization


Code
# Panel 1: Cross-sectional Simpson
p1 <- ggplot(students, aes(x = study, y = grade)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = program_label), alpha = 0.4, size = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "black", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_smooth(aes(color = program_label), method = "lm",
              se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "1. Cross-sectional nesting",
       subtitle = "Pooled: negative\nWithin program: positive",
       x = "Study hours", y = "Grade", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# Panel 2: Panel data Simpson (person-level means vs within-person)
p2 <- ggplot(person_means, aes(x = income_mean, y = satisfaction)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#d73027", size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#d73027", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "2. Panel data (Easterlin)",
       subtitle = paste0("Between persons: slope \u2248 ",
                         round(coef(m_between)["income_mean"], 1),
                         " (strong)\nWithin persons over time: slope \u2248 0.4 (weak)"),
       x = "Person-mean income (centered)", y = "Person-mean satisfaction") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

# Panel 3: Experiment Simpson
p3 <- ggplot(stimulus_summary,
             aes(x = conc_s, y = mean_recall, color = valence_label)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("positive" = "#d73027", "neutral" = "#4393c3")) +
  labs(title = "3. Within-subjects experiment",
       subtitle = "Marginal: positive recalled more\nWithin concreteness: no valence effect",
       x = "Concreteness", y = "Mean recall", color = "Valence") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p1 | p2 | p3









The table below summarizes the three contexts, their structural causes, and their remedies:










Context
Mechanism
Aggregate relationship
Within-unit relationship
Remedy




Cross-sectional nesting
Program difficulty confounds study–grade link
Negative (wrong)
Positive (correct)
Mixed model; within-group slope


Panel data
Between-person selection confounds within-person change
Strongly positive (misleading)
Near zero (causal)
CMC decomposition; FE model


Experiment
Stimulus attribute confounded with condition
Positive (false positive)
Null (correct)
Stimulus RE; covariate control

title: 18  Overview
text: Module 3: Inferential Threats & Identification

title: 18  Overview
text:
18.1 The “Independent” Variable
A variable only earns the label “independent” after you have done the work to establish that it is exogenously related to your outcome. Until then, it is just a predictor — one that may be deeply entangled with other forces that also shape the outcome.
Nested data creates special entanglement problems. When people are grouped into firms, schools, neighborhoods, or time periods, the things that determine their group membership are often the same things that affect their outcomes. A person does not end up in a high-autonomy firm at random. A student does not end up in a high-quality classroom at random. A country does not adopt a policy at random.
This module is about recognizing these entanglements — endogeneity — and learning the identification strategies that let us recover credible causal claims despite them.

title: 18  Overview
text:
18.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: Endogeneity — The three sources of endogeneity in social science data: omitted variable bias, simultaneity/reverse causality, and selection. What each looks like in the DGP, and how to recognize them.
Part 2: Instrumental Variables — When you cannot randomize, an instrument can do some of the work. What makes a valid instrument, the two-stage procedure, and real examples from consumer and organizational research.
Part 3: Selection Effects & Attrition — The many ways observations end up in your dataset non-randomly: representativeness failures, self-selection, survivorship, attrition in longitudinal studies, and researcher-driven exclusions.
Part 4: Regression Discontinuity Design — When treatment assignment is based on a threshold, the continuity of potential outcomes around that threshold gives you a local quasi-experiment. Assumptions, tests, and worked examples.
Part 5: Difference-in-Differences — When some units receive a treatment and others do not, and you have pre- and post-treatment observations, DiD recovers causal effects under the parallel trends assumption.
Part 6: Propensity Scores & Matching — When treatment is observational but you can condition on all relevant confounders, matching and weighting approaches can reduce bias.

title: 18  Overview
text:
18.3 The Through-Line: Identification
Every method in this module is an identification strategy — a way to isolate variation in your predictor that is (approximately) exogenous, and use only that variation to estimate the effect of interest.
The math of each strategy is not the hard part. The hard part is the judgment: Does your instrument really satisfy the exclusion restriction? Are your parallel trends really plausible? Can you really claim you have measured all confounders?
This module teaches both the tools and the judgment.

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text: 20 Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
section: 20.1 The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference
text:
20.1 The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference
Before introducing regression at all, consider the true object of scientific interest: the causal effect of X on Y for a specific unit \(i\).
Under the potential outcomes framework (Rubin, 1974), every unit has two potential outcomes:
\[
\underbrace{\tau_i}_{\substack{\text{causal effect} \\ \text{for unit } i}} = \underbrace{Y_i(1)}_{\substack{\text{outcome if} \\ \text{treated}}} - \underbrace{Y_i(0)}_{\substack{\text{outcome if} \\ \text{not treated}}}
\]
The fundamental problem: we can never observe both \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\) for the same unit at the same time. A person either received psychotherapy or they did not. A country either adopted a carbon tax or it did not. We observe one potential outcome and must infer the other.
Regression estimates the average treatment effect (ATE):
\[
\text{ATE} = E[\tau_i] = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)]
\]
For regression to estimate the ATE correctly, the observed difference in outcomes between treated and untreated units must equal the counterfactual difference between what would have happened to those same units under both conditions. This requires that who gets treated is independent of the potential outcomes.
When this independence fails — when who gets X is related to Y through pathways other than X itself — the regression coefficient is endogenous and does not estimate the causal effect.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.2 What is Endogeneity?
Endogeneity means your predictor X is correlated with the error term \(\varepsilon\) in your regression:
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i \quad \text{where } \text{Cov}(X_i, \varepsilon_i) \neq 0
\]
When this happens, \(\hat{\beta}_1\) is biased. It estimates not just the effect of X on Y but a blend of that effect and all the other pathways through which X and Y are connected.
There are four main sources.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.3 Source 1: Omitted Variable Bias (OVB)
A variable Z influences both X and Y, but Z is not in your model. The path Z → Y gets attributed to X because X and Z are correlated.

20.3.1 The Bias Formula
The expected value of the biased coefficient is:
\[
\underbrace{E[\hat{\beta}_1]}_{\substack{\text{what we} \\ \text{estimate}}} = \underbrace{\beta_1}_{\substack{\text{true} \\ \text{effect}}} + \underbrace{\delta_{ZY}}_{\substack{Z \text{'s effect} \\ \text{on } Y}} \cdot \underbrace{\tilde{\rho}_{XZ}}_{\substack{\text{correlation of} \\ X \text{ and } Z}}
\]
where \(\delta_{ZY}\) is the effect of Z on Y (holding X constant) and \(\tilde{\rho}_{XZ}\) is the correlation of Z with X after partialling out other covariates.
The bias formula has a critical practical use: even without data on Z, if you know the sign of both \(\delta_{ZY}\) and \(\tilde\rho_{XZ}\), you can predict the direction of the bias on your estimate.


Code
# Bias direction table
tibble(
  Example         = c("Ice cream → drowning", "Advertising → sales", "Mindfulness → stress"),
  Confounder_Z    = c("Summer heat", "Store quality", "Stable personality"),
  `Sign Z→X`      = c("+", "+", "+"),
  `Sign Z→Y`      = c("+", "+", "-"),
  `Bias direction` = c("Positive (↑ est.)", "Positive (↑ est.)", "Negative (↓ est.)")
) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "OVB bias direction: multiply signs to get bias direction")



OVB bias direction: multiply signs to get bias direction









Example
Confounder_Z
Sign Z→X
Sign Z→Y
Bias direction




Ice cream → drowning
Summer heat
+
+
Positive (↑ est.)


Advertising → sales
Store quality
+
+
Positive (↑ est.)


Mindfulness → stress
Stable personality
+
-
Negative (↓ est.)











NoteReading the Bias Table



Sign(Z→X) × Sign(Z→Y) = sign of the bias: - Both positive → positive bias (estimate is too large) - Both negative → positive bias (two negatives multiply to positive) - Mixed signs → negative bias (estimate is too small or reversed)
You can use this qualitatively even before collecting data: “If my estimate is already large, and the likely omitted confound would produce positive bias, my true effect is probably smaller than I think.”




20.3.2 Three Demonstrations


Code
n <- 500

# Example 1: Ice cream / drowning / heat
Z_heat    <- rnorm(n, 70, 10)
X_icecream <- 0.3 * Z_heat + rnorm(n, 0, 5)
Y_drowning <- 0.5 * Z_heat + rnorm(n, 0, 8)  # true X effect = 0

m_biased_1  <- lm(Y_drowning ~ X_icecream)
m_correct_1 <- lm(Y_drowning ~ X_icecream + Z_heat)

# Example 2: Advertising / sales / store quality
Z_quality   <- rnorm(n, 50, 10)
X_ads       <- 0.4 * Z_quality + rnorm(n, 0, 8)
Y_sales     <- 0.6 * Z_quality + 0.15 * X_ads + rnorm(n, 0, 10)
# true X effect = 0.15; quality inflates this upward

m_biased_2  <- lm(Y_sales ~ X_ads)
m_correct_2 <- lm(Y_sales ~ X_ads + Z_quality)

# Example 3: Mindfulness / stress / stable personality
Z_stability   <- rnorm(n, 50, 10)
X_mindfulness <- 0.5 * Z_stability + rnorm(n, 0, 6)
Y_stress      <- 80 - 0.4 * Z_stability - 2 * X_mindfulness + rnorm(n, 0, 8)
# true X effect = -2; stability also lowers stress AND increases mindfulness → downward bias

m_biased_3  <- lm(Y_stress ~ X_mindfulness)
m_correct_3 <- lm(Y_stress ~ X_mindfulness + Z_stability)

bind_rows(
  tidy(m_biased_1)  |> filter(term == "X_icecream")   |> mutate(example = "Ice cream (true=0)",    model = "Omits Z"),
  tidy(m_correct_1) |> filter(term == "X_icecream")   |> mutate(example = "Ice cream (true=0)",    model = "Includes Z"),
  tidy(m_biased_2)  |> filter(term == "X_ads")        |> mutate(example = "Advertising (true=0.15)", model = "Omits Z"),
  tidy(m_correct_2) |> filter(term == "X_ads")        |> mutate(example = "Advertising (true=0.15)", model = "Includes Z"),
  tidy(m_biased_3)  |> filter(term == "X_mindfulness")|> mutate(example = "Mindfulness (true=-2)",   model = "Omits Z"),
  tidy(m_correct_3) |> filter(term == "X_mindfulness")|> mutate(example = "Mindfulness (true=-2)",   model = "Includes Z")
) |>
  select(example, model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "OVB across three examples: omitting Z biases estimates in predictable directions")



OVB across three examples: omitting Z biases estimates in predictable directions


example
model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Ice cream (true=0)
Omits Z
0.543
0.067
0.000


Ice cream (true=0)
Includes Z
-0.052
0.070
0.457


Advertising (true=0.15)
Omits Z
0.398
0.052
0.000


Advertising (true=0.15)
Includes Z
0.130
0.053
0.014


Mindfulness (true=-2)
Omits Z
-2.302
0.048
0.000


Mindfulness (true=-2)
Includes Z
-1.947
0.060
0.000





In every case, adding the confounder pulls the estimate toward the true value. The bias direction matches the prediction from the sign table.






WarningAssumption: No Omitted Variables Correlated with Both X and Y



How to check: Draw a causal diagram. List every plausible cause of Y. Ask: is any of these also correlated with X? This requires substantive reasoning about the DGP — you cannot test it purely from data.
In nested data: OVB is especially insidious because the omitted variable often varies at a higher level. Studying whether neighborhood green space predicts depression while omitting neighborhood income biases your estimate; the omitted variable is a neighborhood-level characteristic that clustering creates.
What to do: Add the variable if measurable. Use fixed effects to absorb all stable unit-level confounders. Use IV, RDD, or DiD if the confound is unmeasurable (covered in Parts 2–6).
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.4 Source 2: Simultaneity / Reverse Causality
X causes Y, but Y also causes X. The causal arrow runs in both directions, creating a feedback loop that OLS cannot untangle.


Code
n_sim <- 300
set.seed(42)

# Social support → depression (negative), but depression → social withdrawal (negative)
# Simulate equilibrium: both processes are real
time_pts <- tibble(
  t    = 1:10,
  support = c(60, 55, 52, 50, 49, 48, 47, 47, 47, 47) + rnorm(10, 0, 2),
  depression = c(30, 35, 38, 40, 41, 42, 43, 43, 43, 43) + rnorm(10, 0, 2)
)

p_sim <- time_pts |>
  pivot_longer(-t) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = t, y = value, color = name, group = name)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#e63946", "#1e3a5f"),
                     labels = c("Depression", "Social Support")) +
  labs(title = "Simultaneity: support → depression AND depression → support",
       subtitle = "OLS on cross-sectional data picks up both arrows simultaneously",
       x = "Time", y = "Level", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

p_sim









Psychology example: Social support reduces depression — but depression also causes people to withdraw from social networks. A cross-sectional regression of depression on social support picks up both directions simultaneously and gives a confounded estimate.
Marketing example: Brand awareness drives sales, but sales revenue funds advertising that builds brand awareness. A cross-sectional regression cannot tell you whether awareness causes sales or sales cause awareness.






WarningAssumption: No Reverse Causality



How to check: Ask: could Y at time \(t\) plausibly influence X at time \(t\) or \(t+1\)? If yes, simultaneity is a concern.
What to do: - Use lagged predictors (\(X_{t-1}\) predicting \(Y_t\)) in panel data - Use instrumental variables (Part 2) to isolate the causal direction - Use cross-lagged panel models (Module 4, Part 6) which model both causal arrows explicitly
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.5 Source 3: Selection Effects
The observations in your dataset are not a random sample of the population. Who ends up in your data — and which treatment they receive — is systematically related to both X and Y.
Self-selection: People who choose to undergo therapy are systematically different from those who don’t — they may be more motivated to change, have better social support, or have less severe disorders. Comparing therapy-goers to non-therapy-goers conflates the effect of therapy with all these pre-existing differences.
Survivorship bias: You only observe firms that survived long enough to be in your dataset. Failed firms are absent. Conclusions about firm strategy drawn from survivors are biased because you are conditioning on survival.
Attrition in longitudinal studies: Participants who drop out of a study are often systematically different from completers. If the sickest patients drop out of a clinical trial, the observed treatment effect among completers overstates the population-level effect.
This source of endogeneity is covered in depth in Part 3.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.6 Source 4: Collider Bias
This source is frequently overlooked but is just as dangerous as the others. Conditioning on a collider — a variable caused by both X and Y — creates a spurious association between X and Y even when none exists.
A collider is a variable C where two causal arrows “collide”:
\[X \rightarrow C \leftarrow Y\]
When you condition on C (include it as a control, restrict your sample to a subset defined by C, or analyze a non-random sample selected on C), you open a spurious backdoor path between X and Y.


Code
n_col <- 1000

# True DGP: X and Y are uncorrelated
X_col <- rnorm(n_col)
Y_col <- rnorm(n_col)  # truly independent of X

# Collider C is caused by both X and Y
C_col <- X_col + Y_col + rnorm(n_col, 0, 0.5)

# Full sample: no correlation
r_full <- cor(X_col, Y_col)

# Conditioning on C: restrict to top half of C
high_C <- C_col > median(C_col)
r_cond <- cor(X_col[high_C], Y_col[high_C])

tibble(
  Sample           = c("Full sample (no conditioning)", "Restricted to high-C (conditioning)"),
  `Cor(X, Y)`      = c(round(r_full, 3), round(r_cond, 3)),
  Interpretation   = c("True: X and Y are unrelated",
                        "Spurious: conditioning on C creates negative correlation")
) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Collider bias: conditioning on C creates a spurious X-Y association")



Collider bias: conditioning on C creates a spurious X-Y association







Sample
Cor(X, Y)
Interpretation




Full sample (no conditioning)
0.005
True: X and Y are unrelated


Restricted to high-C (conditioning)
-0.372
Spurious: conditioning on C creates negative correlation





The publish-or-perish example: Prolificacy (X) and idea quality (Y) may be uncorrelated in the full population of researchers. But publication (C) is caused by both — prolific researchers with good ideas get published. If you study only published papers (conditioning on C), you’ll observe that prolificacy and idea quality are negatively correlated within the published set, even though they’re unrelated in the population.
Berkson’s paradox in hospitals: Disease A (X) and disease B (Y) are unrelated in the general population. But both can lead to hospitalization (C). If you study only hospitalized patients, A and B appear negatively correlated — because having A makes it less likely you also have B to explain your hospitalization.


Code
df_col <- tibble(X = X_col, Y = Y_col, C_high = high_C)

p_full <- ggplot(df_col, aes(x = X, y = Y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.15, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Full sample", subtitle = paste0("r = ", round(r_full, 3)),
       x = "X", y = "Y") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_cond <- ggplot(df_col |> filter(C_high), aes(x = X, y = Y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, color = "#2d6a4f") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Conditioned on high C", subtitle = paste0("r = ", round(r_cond, 3)),
       x = "X", y = "Y") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_full + p_cond















WarningAssumption: No Conditioning on Colliders



How to check: Draw a DAG. For every variable you are controlling for or selecting on, ask: is this caused by X, by Y, or by both? If caused by both, it is a collider — do not condition on it.
Common mistake: “Kitchen sink” regressions that add every available covariate. If any of those covariates is a collider, adding it creates bias where none existed.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
section: 20.7 Pearl’s Causal Framework: Three Types of Paths
text:
20.7 Pearl’s Causal Framework: Three Types of Paths
Judea Pearl’s causal graph (DAG) framework provides a systematic way to identify endogeneity. Every causal graph has three fundamental path structures:


Code
make_dag_panel <- function(nodes, edges_df, title, note = NULL) {
  p <- ggplot() +
    geom_point(data = nodes, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 18,
               shape = 21, fill = "#deebf7", color = "#1e3a5f", stroke = 1.5) +
    geom_text(data = nodes, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), size = 3.8,
              fontface = "bold", color = "#1e3a5f")
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(edges_df))) {
    p <- p + annotate("segment",
                      x    = edges_df$x1[i], xend = edges_df$x2[i],
                      y    = edges_df$y1[i], yend = edges_df$y2[i],
                      arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm")),
                      color = edges_df$color[i], linewidth = 1.2)
  }
  p + labs(title = title, subtitle = note) +
    xlim(0.3, 3.7) + ylim(0.4, 2.6) + theme_void(base_size = 11) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9, color = "gray40"))
}

nodes_fork <- tibble(label = c("Z", "X", "Y"), x = c(2, 1, 3), y = c(2, 1, 1))
edges_fork <- tibble(x1=c(1.8,2.2), x2=c(1.2,2.8), y1=c(1.9,1.9), y2=c(1.1,1.1),
                     color = c("#e63946","#e63946"))
p_fork <- make_dag_panel(nodes_fork, edges_fork, "Fork (Confounder)",
  "Z → X and Z → Y\nCreates spurious X-Y correlation\nFix: control for Z")

nodes_chain <- tibble(label = c("X", "M", "Y"), x = c(1, 2, 3), y = c(1, 1, 1))
edges_chain <- tibble(x1=c(1.2,2.2), x2=c(1.8,2.8), y1=c(1,1), y2=c(1,1),
                      color = c("#1e3a5f","#1e3a5f"))
p_chain <- make_dag_panel(nodes_chain, edges_chain, "Chain (Mediator)",
  "X → M → Y\nM is on the causal path\nFix: do NOT control for M")

nodes_coll <- tibble(label = c("X", "C", "Y"), x = c(1, 2, 3), y = c(1, 1, 1))
edges_coll <- tibble(x1=c(1.2,2.8), x2=c(1.8,2.2), y1=c(1,1), y2=c(1,1),
                     color = c("#1e3a5f","#1e3a5f"))
p_coll <- make_dag_panel(nodes_coll, edges_coll, "Collider",
  "X → C ← Y\nCondition on C = open backdoor\nFix: do NOT control for C")

p_fork + p_chain + p_coll


















Path Type
Structure
What it means
What to do




Fork
Z → X and Z → Y
Z confounds X-Y relationship
Block by controlling for Z


Chain
X → M → Y
M mediates X’s effect on Y
Do NOT block (it carries the effect)


Collider
X → C ← Y
C is caused by both X and Y
Do NOT block (conditioning opens a backdoor)



The goal of every identification strategy in the rest of this module is to block all backdoor paths (forks) without blocking causal paths (chains) and without conditioning on colliders.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.8 The Backdoor Criterion
A set of variables S satisfies Pearl’s backdoor criterion for estimating the effect of X on Y if:

S blocks every path between X and Y that has an arrow into X (i.e., all backdoor paths)
S does not include any descendant of X on a causal path to Y (no colliders, no mediators on the X→Y path)

When you can find such a set S from measured variables, controlling for S gives you the causal effect. When you cannot — because the confounders are unmeasured — you need the identification strategies in Parts 2–6.






ImportantBefore Running Any Regression



Draw a causal diagram. Every arrow is a hypothesis about the DGP. Then ask:

Are there any forks (confounders) not in my model? → OVB
Could the outcome affect the predictor? → Simultaneity
Is my sample selected on something related to both X and Y? → Selection
Am I controlling for anything caused by X or Y? → Collider bias

Answering these four questions before looking at your results will save you from the most common errors in applied regression.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text: 21 Instrumental Variables

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
section: 21.1 When Endogeneity Cannot Be Removed by Fixed Effects
text:
21.1 When Endogeneity Cannot Be Removed by Fixed Effects
Module 1’s fixed effects model removes time-invariant confounders by exploiting within-unit variation over time. But fixed effects are not a universal cure for endogeneity. Two failure modes are especially common.
