In longitudinal panel research, the endogeneity can be time-varying: firms that are on an upward productivity trajectory are exactly the firms that invest in training programs. Firm fixed effects absorb stable firm quality, but they cannot absorb a time-varying quality shock that simultaneously drives training adoption and productivity. Even after demeaning, the estimate remains confounded.
In experiments, randomization solves endogeneity for assignment — but not always for treatment receipt. Participants assigned to a wellness intervention may never attend a session. Patients randomized to a drug may not take their pills. When compliance is imperfect, the simple comparison of assigned groups underestimates the effect of actually receiving the treatment.
In both cases, an instrument is a variable that provides exogenous variation in treatment uptake — variation that is not driven by the confounders affecting outcomes.

Longitudinal context: Distance from the firm’s headquarters to the nearest accredited training center provides exogenous variation in training adoption. Firms close to a training center adopt at higher rates not because of their productivity trajectory but because of geography. Distance does not directly affect productivity — only training adoption does.
Experimental context: Random assignment to an encouragement condition (a structured protocol with reminders) provides exogenous variation in actual program completion. Whether you were randomized to encouragement does not directly reduce stress — completing the sessions does.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.2 The Three IV Assumptions






WarningAssumption 1: Relevance



What it means: The instrument Z must actually cause the endogenous treatment X to change. An instrument that does not predict treatment is useless — it produces inflated standard errors and, when weak, can bias estimates in unpredictable directions.
How to check: Regress X on Z (the “first stage”). The F-statistic on Z must exceed 10. Below that threshold, the instrument is “weak.”
Longitudinal context: Distance to the nearest training center must meaningfully predict whether firms adopt the training program. Firms closer to a center should adopt at higher rates.
Experimental context: Being randomized to the encouragement condition must meaningfully predict actual program completion. If the encouragement protocol is so feeble that it does not increase uptake, it cannot serve as an instrument.








WarningAssumption 2: Exclusion Restriction



What it means: Z affects Y only through X — not through any other pathway. This is the core untestable assumption. No statistical test can verify it; it requires theoretical argument and domain knowledge.
How to scrutinize: Trace every conceivable path from Z to Y that bypasses X. Each such path is a potential violation.
Longitudinal context: Distance to a training center must not directly affect firm productivity. It should matter only because it affects whether the firm sends employees to training. A potential violation: firms near training centers may also be in denser urban areas with richer labor markets and better infrastructure — advantages that raise productivity regardless of training adoption.
Experimental context: Random assignment to the encouragement condition must not affect stress outcomes except through whether participants completed the mindfulness sessions. A potential violation: receiving reminder emails might itself reduce stress by making participants feel supported, even if they never attend a session — a direct path from Z to Y that bypasses D.








WarningAssumption 3: Independence (Exogeneity of the Instrument)



What it means: Z is as-good-as-randomly assigned — it is not caused by the same confounders that affect X and Y.
How to check: Verify that Z is balanced across observable pre-treatment characteristics. In a natural experiment, check whether the instrument predicts baseline covariates.
Longitudinal context: Distance from headquarters to a training center should not be correlated with firm quality or productivity drivers. Firms did not systematically choose to locate near training centers because of their underlying productivity potential. In practice: check whether baseline productivity and firm size predict distance.
Experimental context: In a proper encouragement design, Z is genuinely randomized — independence holds by construction. The practical threat is post-randomization contamination (e.g., differential attrition by encouragement condition) or a failed randomization procedure.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
section: 21.3 Section 3: Longitudinal IV — Training Programs and Firm Productivity
text:
21.3 Section 3: Longitudinal IV — Training Programs and Firm Productivity

21.3.1 The Setup
200 firms are observed over 6 years. The research question is whether adopting a formal employee training program increases firm productivity. The identification problem: high-performing firms are more likely to adopt training. They have the budget, the HR infrastructure, and the organizational culture that supports it. A naive OLS regression will overestimate the training effect.
Even adding firm fixed effects does not fully resolve this: if firms experiencing a positive time-varying productivity shock respond by investing in training, within-firm variation in training adoption is still confounded by this time-varying shock.
The instrument is geographic distance (in km) from the firm’s headquarters to the nearest accredited training center.


Code
n_firms <- 200
n_years <- 6

firm_quality <- rnorm(n_firms, 0, 5)   # unobserved time-invariant firm quality
distance     <- runif(n_firms, 10, 200) # km to nearest training center

df_panel <- expand_grid(firm = 1:n_firms, year = 1:n_years) |>
  mutate(
    fq       = firm_quality[firm],
    dist     = distance[firm],
    # High-quality firms and nearby firms adopt training more
    p_train  = plogis(-0.5 + 0.3 * (fq / sd(firm_quality)) - 0.005 * dist +
                        rnorm(n(), 0, 0.3)),
    training     = rbinom(n(), 1, p_train),
    # True causal effect of training = 4; firm quality also boosts productivity
    productivity = 50 + 4 * training + 0.8 * fq + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    firm     = factor(firm),
    year     = factor(year)
  )




21.3.2 First Stage: Instrument Relevance


Code
m_first <- lm(training ~ dist, data = df_panel)
f_stat  <- summary(m_first)$fstatistic[1]
cat("First-stage F-statistic:", round(f_stat, 1), "\n")


First-stage F-statistic: 17.8 


Code
cat("(Rule of thumb: F > 10 indicates a relevant instrument)\n")


(Rule of thumb: F > 10 indicates a relevant instrument)


Distance is a strong negative predictor of training adoption (closer firms adopt at higher rates). F > 10 confirms the instrument is not weak.


21.3.3 OLS vs. IV


Code
# Naive OLS: inflated by firm quality confound
m_ols <- lm(productivity ~ training, data = df_panel)

# IV via Two-Stage Least Squares: uses only distance-driven variation in training
m_iv  <- ivreg(productivity ~ training | dist, data = df_panel)

bind_rows(
  tidy(m_ols) |> filter(term == "training") |>
    mutate(model = "OLS (confounded — true = 4)"),
  tidy(m_iv)  |> filter(term == "training") |>
    mutate(model = "IV / TSLS (recovers true effect)")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Panel IV: effect of training on firm productivity (true effect = 4)")



Panel IV: effect of training on firm productivity (true effect = 4)


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




OLS (confounded — true = 4)
4.648
0.354
0.000


IV / TSLS (recovers true effect)
8.769
3.090
0.005





OLS overestimates the training effect because firm quality simultaneously drives adoption and productivity. The IV estimate isolates only the exogenous, geography-driven variation in training, recovering the true causal effect of approximately 4 units.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
section: 21.4 Section 4: Two-Stage Least Squares — The Mechanics
text:
21.4 Section 4: Two-Stage Least Squares — The Mechanics
TSLS makes the IV logic explicit in two regression steps.
Stage 1: Regress the endogenous treatment X on the instrument Z (and any controls W):
\[\hat{X}_i = \hat{\gamma}_0 + \hat{\gamma}_1 Z_i + \hat{\gamma}_2 W_i\]
This produces fitted values \(\hat{X}\) that contain only the variation in X that is driven by Z — the “clean,” exogenous part of treatment uptake.
Stage 2: Regress Y on \(\hat{X}\) (and controls W):
\[Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \hat{X}_i + \beta_2 W_i + \varepsilon_i\]
Because \(\hat{X}\) contains no endogenous variation, \(\hat{\beta}_1\) is a consistent causal estimate.


Code
# Manual TSLS to show the mechanics
# Stage 1: predict training from distance
stage1    <- lm(training ~ dist, data = df_panel)
df_panel  <- df_panel |> mutate(training_hat = fitted(stage1))

# Stage 2: regress productivity on predicted training
stage2    <- lm(productivity ~ training_hat, data = df_panel)

cat("Manual TSLS (Stage 2 coefficient on training_hat):",
    round(coef(stage2)["training_hat"], 3), "\n")


Manual TSLS (Stage 2 coefficient on training_hat): 8.769 


Code
cat("ivreg() coefficient (should match):",
    round(coef(m_iv)["training"], 3), "\n")


ivreg() coefficient (should match): 8.769 








NoteNote: Standard Errors in Manual TSLS



The coefficients from manual TSLS match ivreg(), but the standard errors do not. The second-stage regression uses \(\hat{X}\) (a generated regressor), so residuals are computed against the wrong quantity. Always use ivreg() or an equivalent dedicated IV function to obtain correct standard errors.



21.4.1 Connection to Module 1 Fixed Effects
Panel fixed effects absorb time-invariant firm-level confounders. But if training adoption responds to time-varying firm shocks, fixed effects alone are insufficient. Adding IV on top of FE handles both:
\[\underbrace{\text{FE removes}}_{\substack{\text{time-invariant} \\ \text{confounders}}} \quad + \quad \underbrace{\text{IV removes}}_{\substack{\text{time-varying} \\ \text{endogeneity}}}\]


Code
# Panel FE-IV: year fixed effects as controls; distance as instrument
m_fe_iv <- ivreg(productivity ~ training + year | dist + year, data = df_panel)
cat("FE-IV estimate (training):", round(coef(m_fe_iv)["training"], 3), "\n")


FE-IV estimate (training): 8.769 


By including year fixed effects in both stages, we account for common time trends while using distance to instrument training adoption within each year.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
section: 21.5 Section 5: Experimental IV — Encouragement Designs and LATE
text:
21.5 Section 5: Experimental IV — Encouragement Designs and LATE
In experiments, the instrument is the random assignment Z itself. When all participants comply, the simple comparison of assigned groups gives an unbiased causal estimate. But when compliance is imperfect, Z becomes an instrument for actual treatment receipt D.

21.5.1 The Wald Estimator
\[\text{LATE} = \frac{\underbrace{E[Y \mid Z=1] - E[Y \mid Z=0]}_{\text{Intent-to-treat effect (ITT)}}}{\underbrace{E[D \mid Z=1] - E[D \mid Z=0]}_{\text{Compliance rate (first stage)}}}\]
The Intent-to-treat (ITT) effect — the effect of assignment, regardless of compliance — is an unbiased estimate of the causal effect of being assigned to treatment. Dividing by the compliance rate scales the ITT up to recover the LATE: the causal effect of actually receiving treatment, among those who comply.


21.5.2 Compliance Classes
When Z = random assignment and D = actual treatment receipt, participants fall into four latent types:









Type
D if Z = 0
D if Z = 1
Description




Complier
0
1
Takes treatment iff assigned — IV identifies this group


Always-taker
1
1
Takes treatment regardless — uninformative


Never-taker
0
0
Never takes treatment — uninformative


Defier
1
0
Does the opposite of assignment — assumed absent (monotonicity)



Only compliers contribute to the IV estimate. LATE is the effect for compliers only.


21.5.3 Simulation: Online Mindfulness Intervention
160 participants are randomized to encouragement (n = 80) or control (n = 80). Of those encouraged, 70% actually complete the mindfulness sessions; none in the control group can access the program. The true causal effect of completing the program is a 10-point stress reduction.


Code
n <- 160
Z <- rep(c(0, 1), each = 80) # random assignment to encouragement

# One-sided noncompliance: encouraged participants comply at 70%
D <- ifelse(Z == 1, rbinom(80, 1, 0.70), 0)

# True effect of completing the program = -10 points on stress scale
stress  <- 60 - 10 * D + rnorm(n, 0, 8)
df_exp  <- tibble(Z = factor(Z), D = factor(D), stress, id = 1:n)

# Three estimators
m_itt  <- lm(stress ~ Z, data = df_exp)         # ITT: effect of assignment
m_at   <- lm(stress ~ D, data = df_exp)         # As-treated: biased by selection
m_late <- ivreg(stress ~ D | Z, data = df_exp)  # LATE: IV estimate

bind_rows(
  tidy(m_itt)  |> filter(term == "Z1") |>
    mutate(model = "ITT — effect of assignment (diluted, unbiased)"),
  tidy(m_at)   |> filter(term == "D1") |>
    mutate(model = "As-treated — biased by self-selection"),
  tidy(m_late) |> filter(term == "D1") |>
    mutate(model = "IV / LATE — true effect ≈ −10")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Encouragement design: three estimators compared (true effect = −10)")



Encouragement design: three estimators compared (true effect = −10)








model
estimate
std.error
p.value




ITT — effect of assignment (diluted, unbiased)
-7.605
1.330
0


As-treated — biased by self-selection
-10.106
1.319
0


IV / LATE — true effect ≈ −10
-11.267
1.853
0







Code
# Manual Wald estimator: confirms IV equals ITT / compliance rate
itt_effect <- mean(stress[Z == 1]) - mean(stress[Z == 0])
compliance <- mean(D[Z == 1] == 1) - mean(D[Z == 0] == 1)
wald_late  <- itt_effect / compliance

cat("ITT (numerator)       :", round(itt_effect, 3), "\n")


ITT (numerator)       : -7.605 


Code
cat("Compliance (denominator):", round(compliance, 3), "\n")


Compliance (denominator): 0.675 


Code
cat("Wald LATE             :", round(wald_late, 3), "\n")


Wald LATE             : -11.267 


Code
cat("ivreg() LATE          :", round(coef(m_late)["D1"], 3), "\n")


ivreg() LATE          : -11.267 


The ITT is approximately −7: diluted because only 70% of encouraged participants completed the program. The as-treated estimate is biased upward in absolute value — completers are likely more motivated than the average participant, so they would have had lower stress regardless. The IV/LATE recovers approximately −10, the true causal effect for compliers.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.6 Section 6: The LATE Interpretation
The IV estimand is always a LATE — the causal effect for compliers only. This has concrete implications in both research contexts.
What LATE means in the longitudinal case: The estimate applies specifically to firms that adopted training because they happened to be geographically close to a training center. Firms that would have adopted regardless (always-takers) or would never have adopted regardless (never-takers) are not identified. If the firms near the margin of adoption are smaller, younger, or less resourced than the typical firm in your sample, the LATE may not generalize to the broader firm population.
What LATE means in the experimental case: The estimate applies to participants who completed the mindfulness program because they were encouraged — not to always-takers who would have completed it on their own initiative, and not to never-takers who would not complete it even when encouraged. If compliers are more ambivalent or less intrinsically motivated than always-takers, the LATE may understate how much the program helps self-selected completers, while overstating how much it would help never-takers if somehow compelled to participate.
Generalizability: LATE is a valid causal estimate, but it is local. Policy conclusions drawn from LATE assume that the complier subpopulation is relevant to the policy question. When it is not — when you need to know the ATE for everyone — IV alone is insufficient.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.7 Section 7: Finding Good Instruments
The exclusion restriction is the bottleneck. Common instrument classes in social science:









Instrument class
Longitudinal example
Experimental example
Why valid




Geographic variation
Distance to training center
Site randomization in multi-site trials
Not chosen by individual unit


Natural experiment
Weather shock on operations
Random lottery for waitlisted participants
Random by nature or design


Regulatory threshold
Age cutoff for program eligibility
PHQ-9 screening cutoff (RDD–IV)
Arbitrary administrative cutoff


Time-based shock
Policy change, sudden regulation
DST, seasonal variation in study timing
Exogenous timing


Peer / administrative assignment
Random roommate assignment
Experimenter assignment in multi-experimenter designs
Administrative randomization



The mathematics of TSLS are straightforward. Finding a credible instrument — one whose exclusion restriction can be defended from theory and whose relevance can be demonstrated empirically — requires deep domain knowledge and careful thinking about the data-generating process. A weak but credible instrument is preferable to a strong instrument with a plausible exclusion-restriction violation. The first produces inflated standard errors; the second produces biased point estimates.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text: 22 Selection Effects & Attrition

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
section: 22.1 When Your Sample is Not Your Population
text:
22.1 When Your Sample is Not Your Population
Even with a perfectly specified model, if your observations are not representative of the population you care about, your conclusions may be wrong. Selection effects are the family of processes by which observations non-randomly enter, remain in, or leave your dataset.
Selection is especially dangerous in nested and longitudinal data because the same processes that structure the data — time, group membership, experimental condition — also drive who stays in it. The threats that follow apply across research contexts, but the way they manifest differs.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.2 Type 1: Representativeness Bias
Your sampling frame does not cover the full population you want to generalize to.
In longitudinal research: Panel databases draw from specific registers (e.g., publicly traded firms, firms above an employment threshold). Private, small, or young firms are systematically absent. Trends in the observed panel may not generalize to the full firm population.
In experimental research: Convenience samples from university subject pools (WEIRD populations — Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic) over-represent young, highly educated, and cognitively flexible participants. Reactions to scarcity, authority, or social norms that are measured in the lab may not generalize to broader populations.






WarningAssumption: Sampling Frame Covers the Population of Interest



How to check: Compare your sample’s distributions on key variables to known population statistics. Are any groups systematically over- or under-represented?
What to do: Acknowledge the limitation explicitly. Use stratified sampling or post-stratification weights if population distributions are known. Consider whether your research question and theoretical claims are appropriately scoped to your sampling frame.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.3 Type 2: Self-Selection and Non-Response Bias
Participants choose whether to enter your study based on factors related to your variables of interest.
The landmark example: the 1936 Literary Digest poll predicted Landon would defeat Roosevelt by a landslide, based on 2.4 million responses. Their sampling frame (telephone directories and car registration lists) over-represented wealthy Republicans. Roosevelt won in a historic landslide.
In longitudinal research: Firms that agree to participate in a panel survey are often larger, more stable, and more administratively capable than firms that decline. If firm size and stability predict your outcome, effect estimates from the panel will not generalize to the true firm population.
In experimental research: Participants who volunteer for a study on “decision-making and well-being” may be more introspective, more compliant, and more psychologically attuned to the constructs you measure. They are not a random draw from the population of people to whom your theory applies.


Code
n_total    <- 1000
health_con <- rnorm(n_total, 50, 10)  # latent health consciousness (unobserved)

# Health-conscious people are more likely to volunteer for a health study
p_participate <- plogis(-2 + 0.06 * health_con)
participated  <- rbinom(n_total, 1, p_participate)

# Outcome: actual health score, partly caused by health consciousness
health_score <- 40 + 0.5 * health_con + rnorm(n_total, 0, 8)

tibble(
  Group = c("Full population", "Self-selected sample"),
  N     = c(n_total, sum(participated)),
  Mean_health = c(mean(health_score),
                  mean(health_score[participated == 1]))
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
    caption = "Self-selection inflates estimated health level in the study sample")



Self-selection inflates estimated health level in the study sample


Group
N
Mean_health




Full population
1000
65.05


Self-selected sample
702
65.87





The self-selected sample substantially overestimates the population average health score because health-conscious people both participate more and are healthier.


22.3.1 Demand Characteristics in Nested Experimental Designs
Self-selection is not only about who enters the study — it also operates within the study as a behavioral process. Demand characteristics are a form of selection into behavior: participants selectively display responses that confirm the hypothesis they have inferred from the study design, the experimenter’s demeanor, or prior experimental experience.
In a standard between-subjects design, demand characteristics inflate both conditions equally and partially cancel out. But in within-subjects (repeated-measures) designs, participants can observe their own behavior across conditions, infer the hypothesis more easily, and respond accordingly. This is especially pernicious because it can masquerade as a real treatment effect.
How demand characteristics create bias in within-subjects designs:
Some participants are systematically more susceptible to demand — those higher in agreeableness, more experienced with experiments, or more motivated to please the experimenter. These participants inflate their responses in the treatment condition more than in the control condition. This creates a pattern that looks like a treatment × participant interaction in the data: susceptible participants show larger “effects” not because the treatment worked better for them, but because they responded more to perceived expectations.
This inflated variance is absorbed by the random slope for condition, but it also biases the fixed effect estimate of the treatment itself — pulling the overall effect in the direction of the demand hypothesis.


Code
n_par          <- 80
n_trials_per   <- 6
susceptible    <- c(rep(FALSE, 60), rep(TRUE, 20))  # 25% demand-susceptible

df_demand <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_par,
  condition   = c("control", "scarcity"),
  trial       = 1:n_trials_per
) |>
  mutate(
    demand_sus  = susceptible[participant],
    # True scarcity effect = -3 (scarcity reduces willingness to pay via threat)
    # Demand-susceptible participants inflate scarcity condition scores by +8
    demand_bias = ifelse(demand_sus & condition == "scarcity", 8, 0),
    dq          = 65 + (-3) * (condition == "scarcity") + demand_bias +
                    rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    participant = factor(participant),
    condition   = factor(condition, levels = c("control", "scarcity"))
  )

# Pooled model: mixes susceptible and non-susceptible participants
m_pooled <- lmer(dq ~ condition + (1 | participant), data = df_demand,
                 REML = FALSE)

# Split by susceptibility to reveal the true effect vs. demand inflation
m_non_sus <- lmer(dq ~ condition + (1 | participant),
                  data = df_demand |> filter(!demand_sus), REML = FALSE)
m_sus     <- lmer(dq ~ condition + (1 | participant),
                  data = df_demand |> filter(demand_sus),  REML = FALSE)

bind_rows(
  tidy(m_pooled,  effects = "fixed") |> filter(term == "conditionscarcity") |>
    mutate(group = "Pooled (all participants)"),
  tidy(m_non_sus, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term == "conditionscarcity") |>
    mutate(group = "Non-susceptible participants (true effect ≈ −3)"),
  tidy(m_sus,     effects = "fixed") |> filter(term == "conditionscarcity") |>
    mutate(group = "Demand-susceptible participants (inflated)")
) |>
  select(group, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Demand characteristics attenuate the pooled scarcity effect toward zero")



Demand characteristics attenuate the pooled scarcity effect toward zero








group
estimate
std.error
p.value




Pooled (all participants)
-0.912
0.346
0.009


Non-susceptible participants (true effect ≈ −3)
-2.685
0.381
0.000


Demand-susceptible participants (inflated)
4.406
0.662
0.000





The pooled estimate is attenuated toward zero: the 25% of participants who inflate their scarcity-condition responses mask the true negative effect among the other 75%. The estimate for non-susceptible participants cleanly recovers the true effect of −3. The susceptible participants show near-zero or positive effects — the demand inflation swamps the true effect for them.
Practical remedies: Within-subjects designs employ deception (concealing the true hypothesis), filler tasks (obscuring the critical conditions), post-hoc suspicion checks (screening out participants who guessed the hypothesis), and implicit measures (which are harder to consciously manipulate). In the mixed model framework, demand characteristics manifest as systematic participant × condition interactions. This is why you should always examine the random slope variance for condition: a large random slope variance in a within-subjects design is a warning sign that participants are responding heterogeneously in ways that may reflect demand rather than true effect heterogeneity.



22.3.2 Hawthorne Effects and Experimenter Effects in Multi-Site Designs
Participants change behavior when they know they are being observed. This Hawthorne effect is a between-experimenter confound in multi-experimenter or multi-site studies: some experimenters elicit stronger Hawthorne effects because of their demeanor, perceived authority, or enthusiasm for the study.
Practical implication for nested experimental data: Experimenter is a level-2 clustering variable. If some experimenters consistently elicit stronger Hawthorne effects, their participants will show elevated treatment effects regardless of the actual manipulation. Adding experimenter random intercepts (covered in Module 5, Part 4) absorbs experimenter-level Hawthorne effects. If experimenter is not modeled, its variance inflates the treatment effect standard error and potentially biases the point estimate.
The same logic applies in multi-site longitudinal studies: the site or observer conducting the repeated assessments is a clustering variable. Observer drift (systematic change in measurement practice over time) and observer effects (participants performing differently for different assessors) are level-2 and cross-level confounds, not nuisance.






WarningAssumption: Observer Effects Are Negligible or Modeled



How to check: Test whether experimenter / site / assessor identity predicts outcomes after controlling for treatment. A significant experimenter effect signals that Hawthorne-type confounds are operating.
What to do: Include experimenter or site as a random effect. Pre-train experimenters to a behavioral protocol to minimize between-experimenter variation. In longitudinal studies, keep the same assessor across waves where possible.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.4 Type 3: Survivorship Bias
You only observe units that survived some selection filter correlated with your outcome.
The canonical example: World War II analysts studied bullet-hole patterns on returning planes to determine where to add armor. Abraham Wald pointed out that the relevant evidence came from planes that did not return. The areas with no bullet holes on returning planes were the critical spots — hits there were fatal. The returning planes were a survivor-selected sample.
In longitudinal research: Studying predictors of firm innovation using currently existing firms excludes all firms that failed. If early failure is systematically related to your predictors (e.g., low R&D investment), estimates of those predictors are biased in the surviving sample.
In experimental research: Analyzing only participants who completed all sessions of a multi-session experiment excludes those who dropped out. If dropout is related to the treatment (e.g., participants who find the task aversive are more likely to withdraw), the completed-cases analysis represents a survivorship-selected sample — typically the more motivated, more compliant, or less affected participants.






WarningAssumption: No Survivorship Bias



How to check: Ask whether your outcome is systematically related to the probability of appearing in your dataset. If yes, you likely have survivorship bias.
What to do: Obtain historical data including non-survivors. In longitudinal research, use survival analysis (Module 6, Part 3) to model time-to-exit explicitly. In experiments, report and analyze dropout patterns and compare dropouts to completers on baseline characteristics.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
section: 22.5 Type 4: Attrition Bias in Longitudinal Data
text:
22.5 Type 4: Attrition Bias in Longitudinal Data
Participants drop out of your study non-randomly over time. This is one of the most serious threats to longitudinal research — and a direct consequence of the nested structure of time.
In longitudinal panel research: High-depression individuals may withdraw from a well-being panel because participation is burdensome. High-performing firms may exit the panel because they no longer need advisory services. In both cases, later waves of the panel over-represent lower-symptom or lower-performing units.
In experimental repeated-measures research: Participants who find the experimental task aversive, boring, or demanding are more likely to withdraw after the first session. If task aversiveness is related to the treatment (e.g., a high-cognitive-load manipulation is more exhausting for lower-capacity participants), dropout is informatively related to both condition and outcome.


Code
n_people <- 200
n_waves  <- 5

baseline_dep <- rnorm(n_people, 50, 10)
treatment    <- rbinom(n_people, 1, 0.5)

df_long <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, wave = 1:n_waves) |>
  mutate(
    baseline   = baseline_dep[person],
    treat      = treatment[person],
    depression = baseline - 5 * treat * (wave / n_waves) + rnorm(n(), 0, 4)
  )

# People with higher depression are more likely to drop out
df_long <- df_long |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(
    drop_risk   = plogis(-3 + 0.04 * mean(depression)),
    dropped_out = cummax(rbinom(n(), 1, drop_risk)),
    observed    = dropped_out == 0 | wave == 1
  ) |>
  ungroup()

complete_cases <- df_long |>
  group_by(person) |> filter(all(observed)) |> ungroup()

tibble(
  Sample   = c("All (Wave 1)", "Completers only"),
  N        = c(n_distinct(df_long$person[df_long$wave == 1]),
               n_distinct(complete_cases$person)),
  Mean_baseline_depression = c(
    mean(baseline_dep),
    mean(unique(complete_cases$baseline))
  )
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
    caption = "Attrition: completers have lower baseline depression than the full sample")



Attrition: completers have lower baseline depression than the full sample


Sample
N
Mean_baseline_depression




All (Wave 1)
200
49.90


Completers only
43
45.95







Code
# True treatment effect = -5 (per unit of wave / n_waves)
m_completers <- lm(depression ~ treat + wave + baseline, data = complete_cases)
m_all        <- lm(depression ~ treat + wave + baseline,
                   data = df_long |> filter(observed))

bind_rows(
  tidy(m_completers) |> filter(term == "treat") |>
    mutate(model = "Complete cases only (attrition bias)"),
  tidy(m_all) |> filter(term == "treat") |>
    mutate(model = "All observed data (less biased)")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Attrition biases the treatment effect estimate (true ≈ −5 at final wave)")



Attrition biases the treatment effect estimate (true ≈ −5 at final wave)


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Complete cases only (attrition bias)
-1.799
0.550
0.001


All observed data (less biased)
-1.651
0.356
0.000











WarningAssumption: Dropout Mechanism is Ignorable



MCAR (Missing Completely At Random): Dropout is unrelated to any variable — observed or unobserved. Very rare in practice.
MAR (Missing At Random): Dropout depends only on observed variables. If MAR holds, mixed-effects models using all available data (not just completers) give valid estimates. This is a reasonable assumption when you have rich baseline data.
MNAR (Missing Not At Random): Dropout depends on the unobserved outcome itself (e.g., people drop out because their depression worsened, not because of any measured variable). Requires sensitivity analysis or selection models (Heckman correction).
How to check: Compare baseline characteristics of completers vs. dropouts. If they differ substantially on predictors of the outcome, the dropout mechanism is non-ignorable and complete-cases analysis is biased.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.6 Type 5: Exclusion Bias
The researcher removes observations non-randomly — typically based on criteria correlated with the outcome.
In longitudinal research: Removing firm-year observations where productivity was “unusually low” because the firm was going through a restructuring is a form of exclusion bias if restructuring itself is the phenomenon you are studying. Excluding outlier years based on outcome values selectively removes exactly the high-variance observations that may be most informative.
In experimental research: Removing participants who “clearly did not understand the task” based on their responses — after you have seen the data — is exclusion bias if the misunderstanding or non-compliance rate differs systematically by condition. Post-hoc exclusions that benefit the hypothesis are a particularly consequential form of researcher degrees of freedom.
The key principle: Any data deletion that is non-random introduces selection bias. This includes:

Removing “outliers” based on outcome values
Excluding protocol violators after seeing results
Dropping units that seem “too different” from the main pattern
Selectively trimming conditions that inflate the standard error







TipPre-Registration as a Defense



Pre-registering exclusion criteria before data collection prevents post-hoc rationalization of exclusions that benefit your hypothesis. It does not eliminate the statistical bias if exclusions are necessary, but it prevents the researcher from inadvertently introducing bias by making exclusion decisions under the influence of the results. Pre-registered exclusion criteria are also far more credible to reviewers and readers than post-hoc criteria.

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text: 23 Regression Discontinuity Design

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.1 The Threshold as a Quasi-Experiment
Regression Discontinuity Design (RDD) exploits a rule: treatment is assigned when a continuous running variable crosses a known threshold. Observations just below and just above the threshold are nearly identical in every respect except treatment status — because small differences in the running variable near the cutoff are essentially random noise in the data-generating process.
The key insight: Right at the threshold, treatment is as-good-as-randomly assigned. The jump in outcomes at the cutoff is the causal effect of treatment for units near the threshold.
RDD appears in two distinct forms across the research contexts this course covers. In longitudinal research, the running variable is often time itself (Interrupted Time Series) or a cumulative score that triggers a policy intervention. In experimental research, the running variable is often a continuous eligibility screening score, and the cutoff determines who qualifies for a treatment arm.

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
section: 23.2 Part A: RDD in Longitudinal Research
text:
23.2 Part A: RDD in Longitudinal Research

23.2.1 Sharp RDD: Test Scores and Earnings
The canonical cross-sectional RDD uses a test score cutoff to assign college admission. Applicants above the cutoff are admitted; those below are not. Applicants just below and just above an arbitrary threshold are otherwise nearly identical.






WarningAssumption: Continuity of Potential Outcomes at the Threshold



What it means: In the absence of treatment, the expected outcome must be a continuous function of the running variable at the cutoff. The only reason outcomes should jump at the threshold is the treatment itself.
Why it fails:

Sorting/manipulation: If people can manipulate their running variable to cross the threshold (retaking tests, strategic score reporting), those just above and just below are no longer comparable.
Other policies at the same cutoff: If a second treatment also activates at exactly the same threshold, you cannot separate the effects.
Anticipation effects: If people change behavior in anticipation of crossing the threshold (e.g., firms change investment plans because they know a regulatory threshold is approaching).

How to check: McCrary density test — a spike in the density of the running variable just above the cutoff indicates manipulation/sorting.




Code
n <- 1000
# Running variable: test score, centered at cutoff = 0
test_score <- rnorm(n, 0, 1)
admitted   <- as.integer(test_score >= 0)
# True causal effect of admission on earnings = 8
earnings   <- 50 + 5 * test_score + 8 * admitted + rnorm(n, 0, 5)

df_rdd <- tibble(test_score, admitted, earnings)

ggplot(df_rdd, aes(x = test_score, y = earnings, color = factor(admitted))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ poly(x, 2), se = TRUE,
              linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black",
             linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f"),
                     labels = c("Not admitted", "Admitted")) +
  labs(title = "RDD: earnings vs. test score",
       subtitle = "Jump at the dashed cutoff = causal effect of admission",
       x = "Test Score (centered at cutoff)", y = "Earnings",
       color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











Code
rdd_result <- rdrobust(y = df_rdd$earnings, x = df_rdd$test_score, c = 0)
summary(rdd_result)


Sharp RD estimates using local polynomial regression.

Number of Obs.                 1000
BW type                       mserd
Kernel                   Triangular
VCE method                       NN

Number of Obs.                  515          485
Eff. Number of Obs.             286          278
Order est. (p)                    1            1
Order bias  (q)                   2            2
BW est. (h)                   0.774        0.774
BW bias (b)                   1.331        1.331
rho (h/b)                     0.581        0.581
Unique Obs.                     515          485

=====================================================================
                   Point    Robust Inference
                Estimate         z     P>|z|      [ 95% C.I. ]       
---------------------------------------------------------------------
     RD Effect     9.057    10.078     0.000     [7.492 , 11.110]    
=====================================================================


rdrobust uses local polynomial regression with an automatically selected optimal bandwidth. The estimated jump at the cutoff recovers the true causal effect of approximately 8 earnings units.


23.2.2 The Density Test


Code
ggplot(df_rdd, aes(x = test_score)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "#1e3a5f", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.2,
             linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Density test: check for manipulation at the cutoff",
       subtitle = "A spike just right of the dashed line = sorting violation",
       x = "Test Score (centered at cutoff)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









The distribution is smooth through the cutoff — no evidence of sorting. In real test-score data, a spike just above the cutoff would indicate that applicants near the threshold are retaking tests or that administrators are rounding scores upward.


23.2.3 Falsification Tests
Run the RDD on pre-treatment covariates (age, family income, gender). These variables cannot be caused by the treatment. A jump at the threshold in a covariate means something else is discontinuous there — a serious threat to validity.


Code
df_rdd <- df_rdd |> mutate(age = 18 + rnorm(n, 0, 1))
fake   <- rdrobust(y = df_rdd$age, x = df_rdd$test_score, c = 0)
cat("Jump in age at cutoff (should be ~0):", round(fake$coef[1], 3),
    "| p =", round(fake$pv[1], 3), "\n")


Jump in age at cutoff (should be ~0): -0.02 | p = 0.915 




23.2.4 Bandwidth Sensitivity
The estimate should be stable as you vary the bandwidth around the default. Dramatic changes signal dependence on functional form assumptions.


Code
bws  <- seq(0.3, 1.5, by = 0.2)
ests <- sapply(bws, \(bw)
  rdrobust(y = df_rdd$earnings, x = df_rdd$test_score, c = 0, h = bw)$coef[1])

tibble(Bandwidth = bws, Estimate = ests) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Bandwidth, y = Estimate)) +
  geom_line(color = "#1e3a5f", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 8, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  labs(title = "Bandwidth sensitivity (red dashed = true effect = 8)",
       x = "Bandwidth", y = "Estimated Discontinuity") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)












23.2.5 Interrupted Time Series as Temporal RDD
A special form of RDD uses time as the running variable. When a policy takes effect at a known date, observations before that date serve as “control” and observations after as “treated.” The continuity assumption becomes: absent the policy, the pre-existing time trend would have continued smoothly through the policy date.
This design is called an Interrupted Time Series (ITS) and is especially valuable in longitudinal panel data where a policy is applied to all units simultaneously.
The ITS model separates three distinct quantities:
\[Y_t = \underbrace{\beta_0 + \beta_1 t}_{\text{pre-policy trend}} + \underbrace{\beta_2 D_t}_{\text{level jump at policy date}} + \underbrace{\beta_3 (t - c) D_t}_{\text{post-policy slope change}} + \varepsilon_t\]

\(\beta_1\): the pre-policy trend (the counterfactual slope — what would have continued)
\(\beta_2\): the immediate level jump at the policy date (the “intercept shift”)
\(\beta_3\): the change in slope after the policy (whether the policy altered the trajectory)



Code
n_stores <- 30
n_months <- 30
store_eff <- rnorm(n_stores, 0, 6)  # store-level random intercepts

df_its <- expand_grid(store = 1:n_stores, month = 1:n_months) |>
  mutate(
    post    = as.integer(month >= 16),          # policy starts at month 16
    t_post  = pmax(month - 15, 0),              # time since policy, 0 pre-policy
    store_u = store_eff[store],
    # True: level jump = +5, slope change = +0.3/month
    sales   = 60 + 0.8 * month + 5 * post + 0.3 * t_post + store_u +
                rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    store   = factor(store)
  )

m_its <- lmer(sales ~ month + post + t_post + (1 | store), data = df_its,
              REML = FALSE)
summary(m_its)$coefficients |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  rownames_to_column("term") |>
  filter(term %in% c("month", "post", "t_post")) |>
  rename(estimate = Estimate, std.error = `Std. Error`, t = `t value`) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 3))) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "ITS model: pre-trend, level jump (post), and slope change (t_post)")



ITS model: pre-trend, level jump (post), and slope change (t_post)


term
estimate
std.error
t




month
0.819
0.055
14.940


post
4.952
0.672
7.371


t_post
0.293
0.077
3.781







Code
# Aggregate to store-average for plotting
df_its_agg <- df_its |>
  group_by(month, post) |>
  summarise(sales = mean(sales), .groups = "drop")

# Predicted line from model (using population-level prediction, no store RE)
coefs <- fixef(m_its)
df_pred <- tibble(month = 1:30) |>
  mutate(
    post   = as.integer(month >= 16),
    t_post = pmax(month - 15, 0),
    pred   = coefs["(Intercept)"] + coefs["month"] * month +
               coefs["post"] * post + coefs["t_post"] * t_post
  )

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = df_its_agg, aes(x = month, y = sales, color = factor(post)),
             size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_line(data = df_pred, aes(x = month, y = pred), linewidth = 1.2,
            color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 15.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946",
             linewidth = 1.1) +
  annotate("text", x = 16.5, y = 72, label = expression(beta[2] ~ "(level jump)"),
           hjust = 0, color = "#e63946", size = 4) +
  annotate("text", x = 22, y = 84, label = expression(beta[3] ~ "(slope change)"),
           hjust = 0, color = "#555555", size = 4) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f"),
                     labels = c("Pre-policy", "Post-policy")) +
  labs(title = "Interrupted Time Series: pricing policy at month 16",
       x = "Month", y = "Mean Sales", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









Interpretation:

\(\hat{\beta}_2 \approx 5\): The policy caused an immediate, step-change increase in sales at implementation.
\(\hat{\beta}_3 \approx 0.3\): The policy also increased the monthly sales growth rate by 0.3 units per month compared to the pre-policy trend.
\(\hat{\beta}_1 \approx 0.8\): The counterfactual — in the absence of the policy, sales would have continued rising by 0.8 units per month.

The ITS design cannot rule out other events that happened exactly at month 16. The strongest threats are contemporaneous shocks (another policy, a market event) that coincide precisely with the policy date.
The density-test equivalent for ITS: Check for unusual clustering of observations just before the policy implementation date. Firms that anticipate a pricing policy may accelerate purchases or delay inventory, creating a temporary spike in the outcome in periods 14–15. Plot the distribution of the outcome in the 3–4 periods immediately before implementation and compare to the pre-trend expectation.

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
section: 23.3 Part B: RDD in Experimental Research
text:
23.3 Part B: RDD in Experimental Research
RDD logic appears in experiments when eligibility for treatment is determined by crossing a continuous screening threshold. Unlike the observational RDD — where the cutoff is an exogenous administrative rule — the experimental RDD uses the cutoff to create a natural comparison group within a clinical or intervention study.

23.3.1 Clinical Trial with PHQ-9 Screening Cutoff
In a depression intervention trial, participants who score ≥ 15 on the PHQ-9 depression screening instrument are eligible for the treatment arm; those scoring below 15 are placed on a waitlist (active control). This creates a regression discontinuity: participants just above and just below the cutoff of 15 are nearly identical in all respects except treatment assignment.
The PHQ-9 RDD is a sharp RDD if the cutoff is strictly enforced (everyone scoring ≥ 15 receives treatment; no one below 15 does). It becomes a fuzzy RDD if clinician judgment overrides the score — some below-15 participants receive treatment based on clinical discretion, and some above-15 participants decline. In the fuzzy case, the cutoff is an instrument for treatment receipt (not a deterministic assignment rule), and the estimand is a LATE — just as in the IV framework.






WarningAssumption: Sharp vs. Fuzzy Cutoff



Sharp RDD: Treatment is deterministically assigned by the cutoff — everyone above the threshold is treated, no one below is. The discontinuity directly identifies the treatment effect for units near the cutoff.
Fuzzy RDD: The cutoff changes the probability of treatment receipt — some above-threshold participants do not receive treatment (refusal, clinical override), some below-threshold participants do (clinician discretion). Fuzzy RDD gives a LATE via IV: the cutoff serves as an instrument for treatment receipt. Use rdrobust(..., fuzzy = treatment_received) for fuzzy designs.
The key diagnostic: check the first stage — does crossing the cutoff produce a large, discontinuous jump in the probability of treatment receipt?




Code
n     <- 600
phq9  <- round(runif(n, 0, 27))  # PHQ-9 scores from 0 to 27
treated <- as.integer(phq9 >= 15)

# True causal effect for participants near threshold = 6 points improvement
# on Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HDRS; lower = less depressed)
hdrs <- 12 - 0.15 * phq9 + 6 * treated + rnorm(n, 0, 4)

df_clinical <- tibble(phq9, treated, hdrs)

rdd_clinical <- rdrobust(y = df_clinical$hdrs, x = df_clinical$phq9, c = 15)
summary(rdd_clinical)


Sharp RD estimates using local polynomial regression.

Number of Obs.                  600
BW type                       mserd
Kernel                   Triangular
VCE method                       NN

Number of Obs.                  335          265
Eff. Number of Obs.              77          107
Order est. (p)                    1            1
Order bias  (q)                   2            2
BW est. (h)                   4.211        4.211
BW bias (b)                   8.002        8.002
rho (h/b)                     0.526        0.526
Unique Obs.                      15           13

=====================================================================
                   Point    Robust Inference
                Estimate         z     P>|z|      [ 95% C.I. ]       
---------------------------------------------------------------------
     RD Effect     7.820     5.283     0.000     [5.292 , 11.535]    
=====================================================================




Code
ggplot(df_clinical, aes(x = phq9, y = hdrs, color = factor(treated))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 1.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, se = TRUE, linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 15, linetype = "dashed", color = "black",
             linewidth = 1.1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f"),
                     labels = c("Waitlist (PHQ-9 < 15)",
                                "Treated (PHQ-9 ≥ 15)")) +
  labs(title = "RDD: depression intervention at PHQ-9 cutoff = 15",
       subtitle = "Jump at the cutoff = causal effect of treatment for participants near 15",
       x = "PHQ-9 Screening Score", y = "HDRS Outcome Score",
       color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











23.3.2 Density Test: Did Participants Game Their PHQ-9 Score?
If participants believe that scoring ≥ 15 gives them access to a desirable treatment, they might inflate their self-reported PHQ-9 responses to qualify. A spike in the density just above 15 indicates this gaming behavior — a sorting violation that makes those just above and just below the threshold non-comparable.


Code
ggplot(df_clinical, aes(x = phq9)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 28, fill = "#1e3a5f", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 15, color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.2,
             linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Density test: PHQ-9 score distribution",
       subtitle = "A spike just above 15 (red dashed) = participants inflating scores to qualify",
       x = "PHQ-9 Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









In this simulation there is no spike — the distribution is smooth through the cutoff, consistent with the continuity assumption.


23.3.3 Falsification Test: No Jump in Pre-Treatment Covariates
Run the RDD on age — a pre-treatment covariate that cannot be caused by depression treatment eligibility. A discontinuity in age at PHQ-9 = 15 would indicate that something other than depression severity drives the threshold, undermining the identification assumption.


Code
df_clinical <- df_clinical |>
  mutate(age = 35 + rnorm(n, 0, 10))

fake_clinical <- rdrobust(y = df_clinical$age, x = df_clinical$phq9, c = 15)
cat("Jump in age at PHQ-9 = 15 (should be ~0):",
    round(fake_clinical$coef[1], 3),
    "| p =", round(fake_clinical$pv[1], 3), "\n")


Jump in age at PHQ-9 = 15 (should be ~0): 1.535 | p = 0.763 




23.3.4 The LATE Interpretation for Clinical RDD
Like all RDD estimates, this is a Local Average Treatment Effect for participants near the PHQ-9 = 15 cutoff. The treatment effect for severely depressed patients (PHQ-9 > 25) may be very different — and RDD cannot speak to it, because those patients are far from the threshold. Similarly, participants with PHQ-9 scores of 5–10 (mild symptoms) are far below the cutoff; the treatment effect there is entirely unidentified by this design.
The clinical implication: The RDD estimate is most credible precisely where the clinical evidence is most contested — the moderate-severity range near the threshold. It cannot adjudicate whether the treatment would work better for the most severely depressed, who are already likely to receive treatment through other channels.

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.4 Summary: RDD Across Contexts









Feature
Cross-sectional / Observational RDD
Interrupted Time Series
Experimental Screening RDD




Running variable
Continuous score (test, age, income)
Time (months, quarters)
Continuous screening instrument


Threshold
Administrative cutoff
Policy date
Eligibility cutoff


Treatment
Program / policy eligibility
Pre vs. post policy
Treatment arm entry


Density test
Spike in score near cutoff
Unusual clustering before policy date
Spike in screening score near cutoff


Continuity assumption
No sorting on unobservables
No confounding events at policy date
No gaming of screening score


LATE for…
Units near score threshold
All units at the policy date
Participants near screening cutoff



RDD is most credible when the threshold is truly arbitrary from the unit’s perspective, when the running variable cannot be precisely manipulated, and when the bandwidth is narrow enough that treated and control units are genuinely comparable. The narrower the bandwidth, the more credible the local randomization — but also the smaller the sample and the larger the standard errors.

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text: 24 Difference-in-Differences

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.1 The Core Idea
A policy rolls out: some firms must adopt a new management practice; others are unaffected. You have productivity data from before and after. Can you estimate the effect?
The naive approach — compare treated and control firms after the rollout — is biased if they were already different. The Difference-in-Differences (DiD) approach subtracts the control group’s change from the treated group’s change:
\[\hat{\tau}_{DiD} = \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{T,\text{post}} - \bar{Y}_{T,\text{pre}})}_{\text{treated group change}} - \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{C,\text{post}} - \bar{Y}_{C,\text{pre}})}_{\text{control group change}}\]
The control group’s change serves as the counterfactual: what would have happened to treated units if they had not been treated?
DiD appears in two distinct research contexts, and the same estimator underlies both:

Longitudinal panel data: firms, employees, or hospitals tracked across calendar time, with a policy or shock affecting some units.
Repeated-measures experimental data: participants assigned to treatment vs. control and measured both before and after an intervention.

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.2 Part A: Panel DiD

24.2.1 Setup and Simulation


Code
n_firms <- 80
n_waves <- 6  # waves 1–3: pre; 4–6: post

firm_treat <- c(rep(1, 40), rep(0, 40))
firm_base  <- rnorm(n_firms, 50, 8)

df_did <- expand_grid(firm = 1:n_firms, wave = 1:n_waves) |>
  mutate(
    treat        = firm_treat[firm],
    post         = as.integer(wave > 3),
    baseline     = firm_base[firm],
    trend        = (wave - 3.5) * 1.5,
    te           = treat * post * 10,
    productivity = baseline + trend + te + rnorm(n(), 0, 4)
  )




24.2.2 The Core Assumption: Parallel Trends






WarningAssumption: Parallel Trends



What it means: In the absence of treatment, treated and control units would have followed the same trend over time. They can start at different levels — DiD handles that. But their trajectories must be parallel.
How to check:

Visual inspection: Plot pre-treatment trends for both groups — they should look parallel.
Formal pre-trend test: Regress the outcome on treat × wave using only pre-treatment data. A significant interaction signals non-parallel trends.
Event study plot: Estimate separate treatment effects for each period. Pre-period coefficients should hover near zero.

Why it fails: Treated and control units may have been on different trajectories before treatment — perhaps because units that selected into treatment were already changing faster. No statistical fix can rescue DiD if parallel trends are implausible given the selection process.




Code
group_means <- df_did |>
  group_by(wave, treat) |>
  summarise(mean_prod = mean(productivity), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(group = ifelse(treat == 1, "Treated", "Control"))

ggplot(group_means, aes(x = wave, y = mean_prod, color = group, linetype = group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 3.5, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray40") +
  annotate("text", x = 3.65, y = max(group_means$mean_prod),
           label = "Treatment onset", hjust = 0, color = "gray40", size = 3.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f")) +
  labs(
    title = "DiD: parallel pre-trends, divergence post-treatment",
    x = "Wave", y = "Productivity", color = NULL, linetype = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











24.2.3 Regression Implementation: Two-Way Fixed Effects
The regression implementation of DiD is the Two-Way Fixed Effects (TWFE) model:
\[Y_{it} = \underbrace{\alpha_i}_{\text{unit FE}} + \underbrace{\lambda_t}_{\text{time FE}} + \underbrace{\delta \cdot D_{it}}_{\text{treatment effect}} + \varepsilon_{it}\]
Unit fixed effects \(\alpha_i\) absorb all time-invariant differences between firms (e.g., size, industry). Time fixed effects \(\lambda_t\) absorb all unit-invariant temporal trends (e.g., business cycles). The treatment indicator \(D_{it}\) captures the DiD estimate after removing both sources of variation.
In the 2×2 case (one treated group, one pre and one post period), treat:post in an OLS interaction model is algebraically identical to the DiD estimator above. With multiple periods and multiple units, the TWFE formulation generalises naturally.


Code
# OLS with unit and time fixed effects
m_did_fe <- lm(productivity ~ treat * post + factor(firm) + factor(wave),
               data = df_did)

# The treat:post coefficient IS the DiD estimate
tidy(m_did_fe) |>
  filter(term == "treat:post") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "`treat:post` = DiD estimate via TWFE (true effect = 10)")



treat:post = DiD estimate via TWFE (true effect = 10)


term
estimate
std.error
statistic
p.value




treat:post
10.364
0.726
14.282
0





The same model via plm makes the fixed-effects structure explicit:


Code
library(plm)
df_panel <- df_did |> mutate(did_treat = treat * post)

m_plm <- plm(productivity ~ did_treat,
             data   = df_panel,
             index  = c("firm", "wave"),
             model  = "within",
             effect = "twoways")
summary(m_plm)








WarningConnection to Module 1, Part 1 (Unit Fixed Effects)



Adding time fixed effects to a unit-FE panel model is DiD when treatment varies over time. The DiD design is the observational analog of a within-subjects experimental design with a control group: the within-unit pre-to-post change is the “within-subjects” component, and subtracting the control group’s change removes confounding time trends.




24.2.4 Clustered Standard Errors
In DiD with panel data, residuals are correlated within units across waves — the same firm’s errors co-vary over time. Ignoring this inflates precision (underestimates standard errors). Cluster at the unit level:


Code
m_did_basic <- lm(productivity ~ treat * post + baseline, data = df_did)

# Cluster-robust standard errors at the firm level
coeftest(m_did_basic, vcov = vcovCL(m_did_basic, cluster = ~firm)) |>
  tidy() |>
  filter(term == "treat:post") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "DiD with cluster-robust SEs (clustered by firm)")



DiD with cluster-robust SEs (clustered by firm)

m_pre <- lm(productivity ~ treat * factor(wave) + baseline,
            data = df_did |> filter(post == 0))

tidy(m_pre) |>
  filter(str_detect(term, "treat:")) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Pre-period treat × wave interactions — should be near zero")



Pre-period treat × wave interactions — should be near zero

24.2.6 Event Study Plot
An event study estimates a separate treatment effect for each period (omitting one reference pre-period). Pre-period coefficients should not differ significantly from zero — that would indicate differential pre-trends. Post-period coefficients trace out the dynamic treatment effect.


Code
m_ev  <- lm(productivity ~ treat * factor(wave) + baseline, data = df_did)
ev_df <- tidy(m_ev, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(str_detect(term, "treat:factor")) |>
  mutate(
    wave = as.integer(str_extract(term, "[0-9]+")),
    pre  = wave <= 3
  )

ggplot(ev_df, aes(x = wave, y = estimate, color = pre)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 3.5, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray40") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), width = 0.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("#1e3a5f", "#aec6e8"),
    labels = c("Post-treatment", "Pre-treatment (should ≈ 0)")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Event study: pre-period near zero, post-period shows effect",
    x = "Wave", y = "Estimated Treatment Effect", color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











24.2.7 A Note on Staggered Adoption
When different units adopt treatment at different calendar times, the standard TWFE estimator becomes a weighted average of all pairwise DiD comparisons — including “forbidden comparisons” where already-treated units serve as controls for later-treated units. If treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or over time, this weighting can produce a biased and even sign-reversed estimate.






WarningStaggered DiD: A Module 4/5 Preview



Callaway & Sant’Anna (2021) and related estimators address this by computing group-time average treatment effects \(ATT(g, t)\) for each cohort \(g\) at each post-treatment period \(t\), then aggregating. If you have staggered adoption, check whether the did or staggered packages are appropriate before relying on plain TWFE. See Roth et al. (2023) for a comprehensive review of the staggered DiD literature.

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.3 Part B: Experimental DiD

24.3.1 DiD Logic in Pre-Post Controlled Experiments
DiD logic appears directly in experimental designs when you have both a between-subjects factor (treatment vs. control assignment) and a within-subjects time factor (pre vs. post measurement). This is the standard pre-post controlled experimental design — and it is a DiD.
\[\hat{\tau}_{DiD} = \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{T,\text{post}} - \bar{Y}_{T,\text{pre}})}_{\text{treatment group change}} - \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{C,\text{post}} - \bar{Y}_{C,\text{pre}})}_{\text{control group change}}\]
In a randomized experiment, pre-test differences are zero in expectation due to random assignment. The DiD estimate therefore equals the ATT. Adding the pre-test measurement makes the estimate more efficient (reduced variance compared to post-only comparison) and corrects for chance pre-randomization imbalances — especially important in smaller samples.


24.3.2 The Experimental DiD as a Mixed Model
Pre-post experimental data is nested: measurements (L1) within participants (L2) within conditions (between-subjects). The correct model includes a random intercept for participant to account for the within-person correlation across time points:
\[Y_{ij} = \gamma_{00} + \underbrace{\gamma_{10} \cdot \text{time}_{ij}}_{\text{pre-to-post change}} + \underbrace{\gamma_{01} \cdot \text{cond}_j}_{\text{group difference}} + \underbrace{\gamma_{11} \cdot \text{time}_{ij} \times \text{cond}_j}_{\text{DiD estimate}} + u_{0j} + \varepsilon_{ij}\]
The interaction \(\gamma_{11}\) is the DiD estimand. The random effect \(u_{0j}\) absorbs between-person variance in the outcome level, just as unit fixed effects do in the panel setting.


24.3.3 Why Use DiD Instead of Post-Only Comparison?
In a randomized experiment, a simple post-test comparison gives an unbiased estimate of the treatment effect. But DiD is more efficient because:

It reduces variance by removing stable individual differences (absorbed by the participant random intercept).
It corrects for chance pre-randomization imbalances — important in small samples.
With three or more waves, it allows testing pre-trend patterns (the experimental analog of an event study).

The random intercept for participant is the experimental equivalent of unit fixed effects in the panel TWFE model. Both absorb time-invariant individual heterogeneity; the DiD interaction term in both cases identifies the treatment effect net of that heterogeneity.


24.3.4 Worked Example: Mindfulness Intervention
120 participants (60 intervention, 60 control) are measured at baseline and at an 8-week follow-up. The outcome is perceived stress (PSS, 0–40 scale; higher = more stressed). The true DiD effect is −8: the intervention reduces stress 8 points more than the control condition.


Code
n_per <- 60

df_mindful <- tibble(
  participant = rep(1:(n_per * 2), each = 2),
  condition   = rep(c("control", "intervention"), each = n_per * 2),
  wave        = rep(c("pre", "post"), times = n_per * 2),
  u0          = rep(rnorm(n_per * 2, 0, 5), each = 2)
) |>
  mutate(
    time_num  = as.integer(wave == "post"),
    treat_num = as.integer(condition == "intervention"),
    stress    = 25 + u0 - 2 * time_num - 8 * time_num * treat_num + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    participant = factor(participant),
    wave        = factor(wave, levels = c("pre", "post")),
    condition   = factor(condition)
  )




Code
# The DiD mixed model: time × condition interaction = DiD estimate
m_exp_did <- lmer(stress ~ wave * condition + (1 | participant),
                  data = df_mindful)

# wavepost:conditionintervention ≈ -8
tidy(m_exp_did, effects = "fixed") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "`wavepost:conditionintervention` = DiD estimate (true = -8)")



wavepost:conditionintervention = DiD estimate (true = -8)

Code
# (a) Group means plot
group_means_exp <- df_mindful |>
  group_by(wave, condition) |>
  summarise(mean_stress = mean(stress), se = sd(stress)/sqrt(n()), .groups = "drop")

p_means <- ggplot(group_means_exp,
                  aes(x = wave, y = mean_stress, color = condition, group = condition)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_stress - se, ymax = mean_stress + se), width = 0.1) +
  annotate("segment",
           x = 2, xend = 2,
           y = group_means_exp$mean_stress[group_means_exp$wave == "post" &
                                            group_means_exp$condition == "control"],
           yend = group_means_exp$mean_stress[group_means_exp$wave == "post" &
                                               group_means_exp$condition == "intervention"],
           arrow = arrow(ends = "both", length = unit(0.1, "inches")),
           color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = 2.08, y = mean(c(
    group_means_exp$mean_stress[group_means_exp$wave == "post" &
                                  group_means_exp$condition == "control"],
    group_means_exp$mean_stress[group_means_exp$wave == "post" &
                                  group_means_exp$condition == "intervention"]
  )), label = "DiD", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f")) +
  labs(
    title = "(a) Group means",
    subtitle = "Annotation shows the DiD estimate at post",
    x = "Wave", y = "Mean Perceived Stress", color = "Condition"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

# (b) Individual trajectories (sample of 30 per group)
set.seed(42)
sampled_ids <- df_mindful |>
  distinct(participant, condition) |>
  group_by(condition) |>
  slice_sample(n = 30) |>
  pull(participant)

p_indiv <- df_mindful |>
  filter(participant %in% sampled_ids) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = wave, y = stress, group = participant, color = condition)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.5) +
  stat_summary(aes(group = condition), fun = mean,
               geom = "line", linewidth = 1.5, linetype = "solid") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f")) +
  labs(
    title = "(b) Individual trajectories",
    subtitle = "Thin lines = participants; thick = group mean",
    x = "Wave", y = "Perceived Stress", color = "Condition"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_means + p_indiv











24.3.5 Comparing Estimation Strategies
Three common analytic strategies for pre-post experimental data differ in efficiency and assumptions:


Code
# (a) Post-test only comparison — ignores pre-test; unbiased but less efficient
m_post_only <- lm(stress ~ condition,
                  data = df_mindful |> filter(wave == "post"))

# (b) ANCOVA: pre-test as covariate, outcome is post-test score
df_wide <- df_mindful |>
  select(participant, condition, wave, stress) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = wave, values_from = stress) |>
  rename(stress_pre = pre, stress_post = post)

m_ancova <- lm(stress_post ~ condition + stress_pre, data = df_wide)

# Collect treatment effect estimates
bind_rows(
  tidy(m_post_only) |> filter(term == "conditionintervention") |>
    mutate(model = "Post-only"),
  tidy(m_ancova) |> filter(term == "conditionintervention") |>
    mutate(model = "ANCOVA"),
  tidy(m_exp_did, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(str_detect(term, "wave.*condition|condition.*wave")) |>
    mutate(model = "Mixed DiD")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Comparison of estimation strategies (true effect = -8)")



Comparison of estimation strategies (true effect = -8)


model

std.error
p.value




Post-only

Mixed DiD
