ANCOVA and the mixed DiD model are numerically equivalent when the model is correctly specified (linear pre-post relationship, equal slopes across groups). ANCOVA is often slightly more efficient in the two-wave case; the mixed DiD generalises naturally to three or more waves. The post-only comparison is unbiased but uses less information, yielding wider confidence intervals.


24.3.6 Experimental Event Study: Multiple Waves
With three or more measurement waves, you can estimate separate time-point effects — the experimental analog of the panel event study. Pre-intervention point estimates should hover near zero (confirming randomization was successful), while post-intervention estimates trace the trajectory of the effect.


Code
# 4 waves: pre, mid-intervention (4 wk), post (8 wk), follow-up (6 mo)
df_long <- tibble(
  participant = rep(1:(n_per * 2), each = 4),
  condition   = rep(c("control", "intervention"), each = n_per * 2 * 2),
  wave        = rep(c("pre", "mid", "post", "followup"), times = n_per * 2),
  u0          = rep(rnorm(n_per * 2, 0, 5), each = 4)
) |>
  mutate(
    wave_num  = case_when(wave == "pre" ~ 0, wave == "mid" ~ 1,
                          wave == "post" ~ 2, wave == "followup" ~ 3),
    treat_num = as.integer(condition == "intervention"),
    true_did  = case_when(wave == "pre" ~ 0, wave == "mid" ~ -4,
                          wave == "post" ~ -8, wave == "followup" ~ -5),
    stress    = 25 + u0 - 2 * wave_num + true_did * treat_num + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    wave      = factor(wave, levels = c("pre", "mid", "post", "followup")),
    participant = factor(participant),
    condition   = factor(condition)
  )

m_ev_exp <- lmer(stress ~ wave * condition + (1 | participant), data = df_long)

ev_exp <- tidy(m_ev_exp, effects = "fixed", conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(str_detect(term, "wave.*condition")) |>
  mutate(wave = str_extract(term, "mid|post|followup"),
         wave = factor(wave, levels = c("mid", "post", "followup")))

ggplot(ev_exp, aes(x = wave, y = estimate)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), width = 0.2,
                color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  labs(
    title = "Experimental event study: differential effect at each post-wave",
    subtitle = "Pre-period reference = 0 by construction (wave 'pre' is omitted category)",
    x = "Wave (relative to pre)", y = "DiD Estimate (vs. pre)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









The pre-period reference wave is omitted by construction and equals zero. A coefficient near zero at “mid” and significantly negative at “post” would confirm the expected pattern: the effect builds during the intervention and partially persists at follow-up.

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.4 Shared Assumptions






WarningAssumption: SUTVA — No Spillovers



What it means: Treatment of unit A should not affect the outcomes of unit B. If treated firms compete with control firms and treatment improves treated performance at control firms’ expense, the control trend is contaminated.
In within-subjects experimental designs, SUTVA violation is called carryover effects: exposure to condition A changes responses to condition B. If participants learn that resources are scarce in the scarcity condition, this knowledge may carry over to the control condition, reducing the contrast. Carryover effects also arise when the effects of an intervention are physiological or psychological and persist beyond the measurement window.
How to manage (experimental): Randomize condition order across participants; use filler tasks or rest periods between conditions; consider between-subjects designs when carryover is likely to be severe. Including condition order as a covariate also allows you to test for and estimate carryover.
How to check (panel): Test whether control units near treated units trend differently from distant controls.








WarningAssumption: Stable Composition



What it means: The composition of treated and control groups should not change differentially after treatment. If the policy causes the most productive employees to leave treated firms, the post-treatment group is a different population — you are comparing different people, not the same people in different states.
In experiments, differential attrition at follow-up can produce the same bias. If high-stress participants in the intervention drop out before the post-test, the remaining sample is not representative of the randomized group. The treatment effect estimated on completers may differ from the true intent-to-treat effect.
How to check: Test whether observable baseline characteristics predict group × attrition status. If they do, consider inverse probability weighting to correct for non-random dropout (see Part 6).

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
text: 25 Propensity Scores & Matching

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
text:
25.1 The Problem PSM Solves
You want to estimate whether participating in a corporate wellness program improves employee health, using observational HR data. Employees self-selected into the program — healthier, more motivated employees are more likely to participate. A naive comparison of participants vs. non-participants will overestimate the benefit.
Propensity score methods reduce this bias by creating a comparison group similar to the treated group on all observed confounders. They work by estimating the probability that each unit received treatment — the propensity score — then matching or weighting on that score.
\[e(X) = P(\text{Treatment} = 1 \mid X)\]
Key result (Rosenbaum & Rubin, 1983): conditioning on \(e(X)\) is equivalent to conditioning on all of \(X\) — you do not need to match on each covariate separately.
Propensity score methods appear in both settings covered in this course:

Longitudinal/panel data: to address non-random treatment adoption across units and time.
Experimental data: to correct for non-random attrition at follow-up, self-selected sub-treatment usage, or nonlinear pre-test relationships.

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
section: 25.2 Part A: Propensity Scores in Panel / Longitudinal Data
text:
25.2 Part A: Propensity Scores in Panel / Longitudinal Data

25.2.1 Simulation: Employee Wellness Program


Code
n <- 600
age          <- rnorm(n, 40, 10)
health_aware <- rnorm(n, 50, 12)
job_stress   <- rnorm(n, 45, 8)

# Treatment probability driven by confounders
p_treat   <- plogis(-4 + 0.05 * age + 0.06 * health_aware - 0.03 * job_stress)
treatment <- rbinom(n, 1, p_treat)

# True ATE = 5
health_score <- 40 + 0.3 * age + 0.4 * health_aware - 0.2 * job_stress +
                5 * treatment + rnorm(n, 0, 6)

df_ps <- tibble(age, health_aware, job_stress, treatment, health_score)

m_naive <- lm(health_score ~ treatment, data = df_ps)
cat("Naive estimate:", round(coef(m_naive)["treatment"], 2),
    "  |  True effect = 5\n")


Naive estimate: 9.4   |  True effect = 5




25.2.2 Step 1: Estimate Propensity Scores and Check Overlap


Code
ps_model <- glm(treatment ~ age + health_aware + job_stress,
                family = binomial, data = df_ps)
df_ps <- df_ps |> mutate(ps = fitted(ps_model))

ggplot(df_ps, aes(x = ps, fill = factor(treatment))) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.6, bins = 30, position = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f"),
                    labels = c("Control", "Treated")) +
  labs(
    title = "Overlap: propensity score distributions",
    subtitle = "Both groups must cover the same PS range for valid comparison",
    x = "Propensity Score", y = "Count", fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)















WarningAssumption 1: Overlap (Common Support)



What it means: Every unit must have a propensity score strictly between 0 and 1. Units with PS near 0 or 1 have no valid comparison units — causal inference is not possible for them.
How to check: Plot the PS distributions for treated and control groups. Trim units outside the common support region if overlap is poor at the extremes.








WarningAssumption 2: No Unmeasured Confounders (Ignorability)



What it means: Given observed covariates, treatment assignment is independent of potential outcomes. There are no unobserved variables that both predict treatment and affect the outcome.
This is the critical untestable assumption. Propensity score methods can only control for variables you measured. Any unobserved confounder still biases the estimate. Conduct a sensitivity analysis after matching to quantify your exposure to this risk.




25.2.3 Step 2: Nearest-Neighbor Matching and Balance Check


Code
m_out <- matchit(treatment ~ age + health_aware + job_stress,
                 data = df_ps, method = "nearest", ratio = 1)

summary(m_out)$sum.matched |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Balance after nearest-neighbor matching (std. diff. should be < 0.10)")

25.2.4 Step 3: PSM Treatment Effect Estimate


Code
matched_data <- match.data(m_out)

m_psm <- lm(health_score ~ treatment + age + health_aware + job_stress,
            data = matched_data, weights = weights)

tidy(m_psm) |> filter(term == "treatment") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "PSM estimate of ATT (true effect = 5)")



PSM estimate of ATT (true effect = 5)

25.2.5 Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW)
PSM discards unmatched units, which loses information. IPW uses all units by reweighting observations so that the weighted sample looks like a random sample from the full population:
\[w_i = \frac{D_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)} + \frac{1-D_i}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\]
Treated units are upweighted when their treatment probability was low (they are “surprising” treated units); control units are upweighted when their treatment probability was high (they are “surprising” controls). After reweighting, covariates should be balanced between groups.


Code
df_ps <- df_ps |>
  mutate(
    ipw = ifelse(treatment == 1,
                 1 / fitted(ps_model),
                 1 / (1 - fitted(ps_model)))
  )

m_ipw <- lm(health_score ~ treatment + age + health_aware + job_stress,
            data = df_ps, weights = ipw)

tidy(m_ipw) |> filter(term == "treatment") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "IPW estimate (true effect = 5)")



IPW estimate (true effect = 5)


term
estimate
std.error
statistic
p.value




treatment
5.548
0.495
11.213
0





IPW is generally preferred over PSM in panel data settings where you need all time points for each unit. Dropping unmatched observations in PSM can create unbalanced panels — some units contribute to some waves but not others — which complicates longitudinal estimation.


25.2.6 Dynamic Treatment in Panel Data
In panel data, treatment adoption timing varies across units. The propensity score should account for when treatment was adopted — not just whether it was adopted.
At each wave, among units not yet treated, compute the probability of adopting treatment in this wave. Match recently-adopting units to not-yet-treated units with similar adoption probabilities at the time of adoption.


Code
# Time-varying propensity score: probability of adopting in wave w,
# conditional on not yet having adopted
ps_wave <- glm(
  adopted_this_wave ~ lag_outcome + age + job_stress,
  family = binomial,
  data   = df_panel |> filter(wave == w, !previously_treated)
)


This approach is the foundation of marginal structural models, which chain together wave-specific IPW weights to estimate the effect of a treatment history rather than a single binary treatment.


25.2.7 Doubly Robust Estimation
Doubly robust combines a propensity score model with an outcome model. The estimate is consistent if either model is correctly specified — providing an extra layer of protection against misspecification.


Code
# Doubly robust: IPW weights + regression adjustment on covariates
m_dr <- lm(health_score ~ treatment + age + health_aware + job_stress,
           data = df_ps, weights = ipw)

tidy(m_dr) |> filter(term == "treatment") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Doubly robust estimate (true effect = 5)")



Doubly robust estimate (true effect = 5)


term

std.error

p.value

Code
# Side-by-side comparison of all four approaches
bind_rows(
  tidy(m_naive) |> filter(term == "treatment") |> mutate(method = "Naive OLS"),
  tidy(m_psm)   |> filter(term == "treatment") |> mutate(method = "PSM (ATT)"),
  tidy(m_ipw)   |> filter(term == "treatment") |> mutate(method = "IPW (ATE)"),
  tidy(m_dr)    |> filter(term == "treatment") |> mutate(method = "Doubly Robust")
) |>
  select(method, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Comparison: naive OLS vs. PSM vs. IPW vs. doubly robust (true = 5)")



Comparison: naive OLS vs. PSM vs. IPW vs. doubly robust (true = 5)

25.2.8 Rosenbaum Sensitivity Analysis
After PSM, the critical question is: how large would an unmeasured confounder need to be to overturn the conclusion? The sensitivity parameter \(\Gamma\) (gamma) quantifies this.

\(\Gamma = 1\): no hidden bias assumed — two units with the same observed covariates have the same probability of treatment.
\(\Gamma = 2\): an unmeasured confounder could double the odds of treatment for otherwise-identical units.

A small \(\Gamma\) threshold (say, \(\Gamma = 1.2\)) means the conclusion is fragile; a large threshold (say, \(\Gamma = 3\)) means the conclusion is robust to substantial unobserved confounding. The rbounds package implements Rosenbaum bounds for continuous and binary outcomes. Report the \(\Gamma\) threshold at which the p-value crosses 0.05.

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
section: 25.3 Part B: Propensity Scores in Experimental Research
text:
25.3 Part B: Propensity Scores in Experimental Research
Random assignment guarantees unbiased intent-to-treat estimates at baseline. But three situations arise in practice where propensity score methods are needed even within an experiment.

25.3.1 Context A: IPW for Non-Random Attrition
This is the most important experimental application of IPW. Random assignment guarantees an unbiased ITT estimate at baseline — but when participants drop out at follow-up and dropout is related to the outcome, the remaining sample is no longer a random subsample. The naive estimate among completers is biased.
IPW corrects for this by upweighting participants who were similar to drop-outs (by observed baseline characteristics) but happened to remain. The attrition propensity score is modelled as a function of baseline covariates and treatment assignment; each completer is then weighted by the inverse of their probability of remaining.


Code
n_att <- 200
set.seed(42)

treatment_att   <- rep(c(0, 1), each = 100)
baseline_anxiety <- rnorm(n_att, 50, 10)

# High anxiety + being in control group → higher dropout
p_remain <- plogis(2 - 0.04 * baseline_anxiety + 0.4 * treatment_att)
remained  <- rbinom(n_att, 1, p_remain)

# True treatment effect = -5
outcome <- 40 - 5 * treatment_att + 0.3 * baseline_anxiety + rnorm(n_att, 0, 6)

df_att <- tibble(treatment = treatment_att, baseline_anxiety, remained, outcome)

cat("Overall retention rate:", round(mean(df_att$remained) * 100, 1), "%\n")


Overall retention rate: 58 %


Code
cat("Control retention:     ", round(mean(df_att$remained[df_att$treatment == 0]) * 100, 1), "%\n")


Control retention:      56 %


Code
cat("Treatment retention:   ", round(mean(df_att$remained[df_att$treatment == 1]) * 100, 1), "%\n")


Treatment retention:    60 %




Code
# Attrition propensity model
ps_att    <- glm(remained ~ baseline_anxiety + treatment,
                 family = binomial, data = df_att)
df_att$ipw_att <- 1 / fitted(ps_att)

# Complete the analysis on retained participants
df_complete <- df_att |> filter(remained == 1)

m_naive_att <- lm(outcome ~ treatment, data = df_complete)
m_ipw_att   <- lm(outcome ~ treatment, data = df_complete,
                  weights = df_att$ipw_att[df_att$remained == 1])

bind_rows(
  tidy(m_naive_att) |> filter(term == "treatment") |> mutate(method = "Naive (completers only)"),
  tidy(m_ipw_att)   |> filter(term == "treatment") |> mutate(method = "IPW-corrected")
) |>
  select(method, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  mutate(true_effect = -5) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Attrition bias: naive vs. IPW-corrected (true effect = -5)")



Attrition bias: naive vs. IPW-corrected (true effect = -5)


method
estimate
std.error
p.value
true_effect




Naive (completers only)
-6.374
1.072
0
-5


IPW-corrected
-6.618
1.063
0
-5











TipReporting Attrition IPW



Report both the naive (completer-only) and IPW-corrected estimates. They answer different questions:

The naive estimate describes the treatment effect among completers — potentially a biased, self-selected subsample.
The IPW estimate approximates the intent-to-treat effect for the full randomized sample, including those who would have dropped out.

A large discrepancy between the two signals that differential attrition is materially affecting your conclusions.




Code
# Visualize: who drops out by treatment group and anxiety level
p_dropout <- ggplot(df_att, aes(x = baseline_anxiety,
                                 y = remained,
                                 color = factor(treatment))) +
  geom_jitter(height = 0.03, alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "glm",
              method.args = list(family = binomial),
              se = TRUE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f"),
                     labels = c("Control", "Treatment")) +
  labs(
    title = "Retention probability by baseline anxiety and treatment",
    subtitle = "High-anxiety controls are most likely to drop out — biasing naive estimate upward",
    x = "Baseline Anxiety", y = "P(Retained at follow-up)", color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

p_dropout











25.3.2 Context B: PSM for Self-Selected Sub-Treatments
In field experiments, random assignment gives participants access to an intervention, but actual usage is voluntary. Comparing high-users to non-users within the treatment arm of an experiment is an observational comparison — high-users likely differ systematically from non-users on motivation, baseline severity, and other factors.
PSM on baseline covariates makes this comparison more credible by creating a matched non-user group similar to high-users on observed characteristics. This provides an estimate of the effect of intensive engagement, net of observable self-selection.


Code
# Within the intervention arm: match high-users to low-users
# on pre-randomization covariates
m_sub <- matchit(
  high_user ~ pre_score + age + motivation + baseline_severity,
  data   = intervention_arm,
  method = "nearest",
  ratio  = 1
)

# Estimate effect of high engagement vs. low engagement
m_usage <- lm(outcome ~ high_user + pre_score + age + motivation,
              data = match.data(m_sub), weights = weights)


This design is sometimes called a broken randomization analysis or a within-arm observational study. The critical caveat: ignorability holds only if all determinants of usage that also affect outcomes are measured and included in the PSM model. Unmeasured motivation or severity remains a threat.


25.3.3 Context C: Matching on Pre-Test Score
When the goal is to compare the change from pre to post (rather than just the post-test score), matching on the pre-test score before estimating treatment effects can be more robust than ANCOVA when the pre-test — outcome relationship is nonlinear.
ANCOVA assumes a linear, homogeneous (equal across groups) relationship between the pre-test and post-test. When this assumption is violated — for instance, when very high or very low pre-test scorers change at a different rate — matching on the pre-test creates a comparison group that is directly comparable at the pre-test level, allowing nonparametric estimation of the change. This is sometimes called pre-test matching or coarsened exact matching on the pre-test.

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
text:
25.4 Reporting Checklist for PS Analyses






TipPS Reporting Checklist




Specify all covariates included in the PS model and justify their inclusion (pre-treatment only; no mediators)
Report overlap check (PS histogram by treatment group; note any violations of common support)
Report balance statistics before AND after matching/weighting (standardised mean difference table; target SMD < 0.10)
Specify estimand: ATE, ATT, or ATC? And why does it match the research question?
Report sensitivity analysis (Rosenbaum \(\Gamma\) or E-value) to quantify vulnerability to unmeasured confounding
For experimental attrition IPW: report both the naive (completer-only) and IPW-corrected estimates and note any discrepancy
For doubly robust estimation: note which model (PS or outcome) is the primary specification and which provides the robustness guarantee

title: 25  Overview
text: Module 4: Longitudinal Data & Change

title: 25  Overview
section: 25.1 Time is the Ultimate Nesting Variable
text:
25.1 Time is the Ultimate Nesting Variable
Every person, firm, country, or organism exists within a stream of time. When you measure them repeatedly, your observations are nested within that stream. Each measurement is a snapshot of an ongoing trajectory — the DGP is a time-varying process, and your data is a discrete sample from it.
This creates two profound challenges for analysis:

Observations within a person are correlated. Your mood at 9am is related to your mood at 10am. A firm’s revenue this quarter predicts its revenue next quarter. Treating these as independent observations — as standard regression does — violates independence and inflates Type I error.
Change itself is the phenomenon. In many longitudinal studies, the question is not just “what is the average level?” but “how does this change over time, and does it change differently for different people?” A model that treats all observations as exchangeable — ignoring that some come first and some come later — cannot answer this question.

Module 4 builds the toolkit for modeling change over time correctly.

title: 25  Overview
text:
25.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models — The repeated measures ANOVA that most psychologists learned makes a specific assumption about the covariance structure of repeated observations (compound symmetry/sphericity). We show concretely what this assumption means, why it almost always fails, and how mixed-effects models relax it.
Part 2: Covariance Structures — The covariance matrix of your repeated observations is not just a nuisance to be controlled — it is a window into the DGP. We catalog the main structures (compound symmetry, AR(1), unstructured, Toeplitz), show what each implies about how measurements relate over time, and explain how to choose among them.
Part 3: Growth Curve Models — When the question is how outcomes change over time and whether trajectories differ across people, growth curve models are the right tool. We build from simple linear growth to random slopes, nonlinear trajectories, and predictors of growth.
Part 4: Latent Growth Curve Models in SEM — The same growth curve model can be estimated in a structural equation modeling (SEM) framework. We show the connection, explain when the SEM approach adds value (modeling measurement error, multiple growth processes), and demonstrate the equivalence using lavaan.
Part 5: Autoregressive Structures — When today’s outcome predicts tomorrow’s, the covariance structure is autoregressive. We explain AR(1) models, how to specify them in nlme, and the assumptions of stationarity.
Part 6: Cross-Lagged Panel Models — When you want to understand the dynamic relationship between two variables over time — does A cause B, does B cause A, or do they mutually reinforce each other — cross-lagged panel models (and their improved version, the RI-CLPM) provide the framework.

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
text: 27 From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
crumbs: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
text:
27.1 The Standard Psychology Approach
Repeated measures ANOVA (RM-ANOVA) has been the default tool for within-subject designs in psychology for decades. It is familiar, built into every statistics textbook, and available in SPSS, JASP, and R. And it makes an assumption about your data that is almost always wrong.
That assumption is sphericity.
crumbs: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
text:
27.2 What is Sphericity?
Sphericity is a constraint on the variance-covariance matrix of your repeated measurements. Specifically, it requires that the variances of all pairwise differences between time points are equal.
Imagine you measure anxiety at three time points: before a speech, immediately after, and one week later. Sphericity requires that: - Var(Time1 − Time2) = Var(Time1 − Time3) = Var(Time2 − Time3)
In plain language: the variability in how much people change is the same regardless of which two time points you compare. This is equivalent to assuming compound symmetry — that all time points correlate equally with each other.


Code
# Visualize compound symmetry vs. a realistic AR(1) structure
library(Matrix)

# Compound symmetry: equal variances, equal covariances
CS <- matrix(c(1.0, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5,
               0.5, 1.0, 0.5, 0.5,
               0.5, 0.5, 1.0, 0.5,
               0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 1.0), 4, 4)

# AR(1): closer time points are more correlated
rho <- 0.7
AR1 <- outer(1:4, 1:4, FUN = function(i, j) rho^abs(i - j))

# Unstructured: no constraints
US <- matrix(c(1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.3,
               0.8, 1.0, 0.7, 0.5,
               0.6, 0.7, 1.0, 0.8,
               0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0), 4, 4)

plot_cov <- function(M, title) {
  as.data.frame(M) |>
    rownames_to_column("row") |>
    pivot_longer(-row, names_to = "col", values_to = "corr") |>
    mutate(row = factor(row, levels = rev(unique(row))),
           col = factor(col)) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = col, y = row, fill = corr)) +
    geom_tile(color = "white") +
    geom_text(aes(label = round(corr, 1)), size = 4) +
    scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#f7fbff", mid = "#6baed6", high = "#08306b",
                         midpoint = 0.5, limits = c(0, 1)) +
    labs(title = title, x = "Time point", y = "Time point", fill = "Corr") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
}

library(patchwork)
plot_cov(CS, "Compound Symmetry\n(sphericity assumption)") +
plot_cov(AR1, "AR(1)\n(more realistic)") +
plot_cov(US, "Unstructured\n(no constraints)")









The compound symmetry structure (left) forces all off-diagonal entries to be equal. The AR(1) structure (middle) lets correlations decay with time lag — closer time points are more correlated. The unstructured matrix (right) lets each correlation be whatever the data say.
Real longitudinal data almost always looks more like AR(1) or unstructured than compound symmetry.
crumbs: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
section: 27.3 What Happens When Sphericity is Violated?
text:
27.3 What Happens When Sphericity is Violated?
When sphericity fails, RM-ANOVA F-tests are anti-conservative — Type I error rates are inflated. You reject the null hypothesis more often than your alpha level would suggest.


Code
# Simulate data that violates sphericity: correlations decay with lag
simulate_repeated <- function(n = 50, n_times = 4, spherical = TRUE) {
  if (spherical) {
    # Compound symmetry (sphericity holds)
    Sigma <- CS * 4  # multiply by variance
  } else {
    # AR(1) (sphericity violated)
    Sigma <- AR1 * 4
  }

  # Simulate null data (no treatment effect) and check Type I error
  MASS::mvrnorm(n, mu = rep(0, n_times), Sigma = Sigma)
}

# Under the null (no effect), how often does RM-ANOVA reject?
n_sims <- 1000
type1_spherical <- numeric(n_sims)
type1_violated  <- numeric(n_sims)

for (i in 1:n_sims) {
  y_sph <- simulate_repeated(spherical = TRUE)
  y_vio <- simulate_repeated(spherical = FALSE)

  # Reshape and run RM-ANOVA
  extract_pval <- function(y_mat) {
    df_aov <- data.frame(
      Y      = c(y_mat),
      time   = factor(rep(1:4, each = 50)),
      person = factor(rep(1:50, 4))
    )
    aov_fit <- aov(Y ~ time + Error(person/time), data = df_aov)
    s <- summary(aov_fit)
    # Extract p-value from the within-person time effect
    tryCatch(s[["Error: person:time"]][[1]][["Pr(>F)"]][1],
             error = function(e) NA_real_)
  }
  pval_sph <- extract_pval(y_sph)
  pval_vio <- extract_pval(y_vio)

  type1_spherical[i] <- ifelse(is.null(pval_sph), NA, pval_sph < 0.05)
  type1_violated[i]  <- ifelse(is.null(pval_vio), NA, pval_vio < 0.05)
}

tibble(
  Condition = c("Sphericity holds (CS)", "Sphericity violated (AR1)"),
  `Type I Error Rate` = c(mean(type1_spherical, na.rm = TRUE),
                           mean(type1_violated,  na.rm = TRUE))
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Type I error under sphericity vs. violation (nominal alpha = 0.05)")



Type I error under sphericity vs. violation (nominal alpha = 0.05)


Condition
Type I Error Rate




Sphericity holds (CS)
0.038


Sphericity violated (AR1)
0.062











WarningAssumption: Sphericity (RM-ANOVA Only)



What it means: All pairwise variance differences among time points are equal. Equivalent to compound symmetry in the covariance matrix.
Mauchly’s test: The standard test for sphericity in RM-ANOVA. If Mauchly’s W is significant (p < 0.05), sphericity is violated.
The problem with Mauchly’s test: It is sensitive to sample size (underpowered in small samples; overpowered in large ones). It also tests an exact assumption — the question is not whether sphericity is exactly true but whether the violation is large enough to matter.
Corrections within RM-ANOVA: Greenhouse-Geisser or Huynh-Feldt corrections adjust the degrees of freedom. These are better than nothing but still assume a specific covariance structure.
The better solution: Switch to mixed-effects models, which do not assume sphericity and let you specify the appropriate covariance structure for your data.
crumbs: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
text:
27.4 Mixed-Effects Models: No Sphericity Needed
A mixed-effects model makes no assumption about the overall shape of the covariance matrix (in its most general form). The random effects structure and the residual covariance structure together determine what the model implies about how measurements co-vary.


Code
# Simulate anxiety study: 3 time points, AR(1) correlation structure
n_people <- 60
n_times  <- 3
Sigma_ar <- AR1[1:3, 1:3] * 4  # true covariance is AR(1)

# No treatment effect (null data)
anx_data <- MASS::mvrnorm(n_people, mu = c(50, 50, 50), Sigma = Sigma_ar) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  setNames(c("time1", "time2", "time3")) |>
  mutate(person = 1:n_people) |>
  pivot_longer(-person, names_to = "time", values_to = "anxiety") |>
  mutate(
    time_num = as.integer(str_extract(time, "[0-9]")),
    person   = factor(person)
  )

# RM-ANOVA
m_anova <- aov(anxiety ~ factor(time_num) + Error(person / factor(time_num)),
               data = anx_data)

# Mixed-effects model with AR(1) covariance
m_mlm <- lme(anxiety ~ factor(time_num),
             random = ~1 | person,
             correlation = corAR1(),
             data = anx_data)

cat("RM-ANOVA p-value for time:", round(summary(m_anova)$`Error: person:factor(time_num)`[[1]]["factor(time_num)", "Pr(>F)"], 4), "\n")


RM-ANOVA p-value for time: 0.1692 


Code
cat("MLM p-value for time:", round(anova(m_mlm)["factor(time_num)", "p-value"], 4), "\n")


MLM p-value for time: 0.2214 


When the true covariance structure is AR(1) (not compound symmetry), the mixed-effects model gives more accurate p-values because it uses the correct covariance model. RM-ANOVA’s p-value may be misleading.
crumbs: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
section: 27.5 The Key Advantages of Mixed Models Over RM-ANOVA
text:
27.5 The Key Advantages of Mixed Models Over RM-ANOVA








Feature
RM-ANOVA
Mixed-Effects Model




Handles missing data?
No — requires complete cases
Yes — uses all available data


Assumes sphericity?
Yes
No


Allows continuous time?
No
Yes


Models individual trajectories?
No
Yes (random slopes)


Handles unbalanced designs?
Poorly
Gracefully


Can include level-2 predictors?
No
Yes



The only reason to prefer RM-ANOVA is familiarity. Mixed-effects models strictly dominate for any longitudinal design.
crumbs: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models

title: 27  Covariance Structures
text: 28 Covariance Structures

title: 27  Covariance Structures
section: 28.1 The Covariance Matrix is a Scientific Statement
text:
28.1 The Covariance Matrix is a Scientific Statement
When you measure the same person at multiple time points, those measurements co-vary. How they co-vary is not a statistical nuisance — it is a substantive statement about the DGP. The choice of covariance structure encodes an assumption about how the underlying process operates over time.
The covariance structure specifies two things: variances (how variable are observations at each time point?) and covariances (how correlated are observations at different time points?).

title: 27  Covariance Structures
text:
28.2 The Main Structures

28.2.1 1. Compound Symmetry (CS)
All variances are equal. All covariances are equal regardless of lag.
\[\Sigma_{CS} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2 & \sigma^2\rho & \sigma^2\rho \\ \sigma^2\rho & \sigma^2 & \sigma^2\rho \\ \sigma^2\rho & \sigma^2\rho & \sigma^2 \end{pmatrix}\]
When it makes sense: When there is no reason to think observations closer in time are more similar than distant observations. Very rare in practice.
When it fails: Almost always. Time 1 and Time 2 are almost always more correlated than Time 1 and Time 6.


28.2.2 2. Autoregressive Order 1 — AR(1)
Correlations decay exponentially with time lag. Observations one step apart correlate \(\rho\); two steps apart, \(\rho^2\); \(k\) steps apart, \(\rho^k\).
\[\Sigma_{AR1} = \sigma^2\begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho & \rho^2 \\ \rho & 1 & \rho \\ \rho^2 & \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}\]
When it makes sense: For many psychological, physiological, and economic time series where the past predicts the present and the influence fades over time. Very commonly appropriate.


28.2.3 3. Unstructured (UN)
No constraints. Every variance and covariance is estimated freely.
When it makes sense: When you have no prior beliefs about the pattern and enough data to estimate all parameters.
When it fails: With many time points or small samples, the unstructured model is overparameterized and produces unstable estimates.


28.2.4 4. Toeplitz
Like AR(1) but relaxes the constraint that correlations must follow an exponential decay — each lag has its own correlation, but the correlation only depends on the lag (not the absolute time point). A flexible middle ground.

title: 27  Covariance Structures
text:
28.3 Visualizing the Structures


Code
make_cov_plot <- function(Sigma, title) {
  n <- nrow(Sigma)
  # Convert to correlation
  D <- diag(1 / sqrt(diag(Sigma)))
  Corr <- D %*% Sigma %*% D

  data.frame(Corr) |>
    setNames(paste0("T", 1:n)) |>
    mutate(row = paste0("T", 1:n)) |>
    pivot_longer(-row, names_to = "col", values_to = "r") |>
    mutate(row = factor(row, levels = rev(paste0("T", 1:n))),
           col = factor(col, levels = paste0("T", 1:n))) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = col, y = row, fill = r)) +
    geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 1) +
    geom_text(aes(label = round(r, 2)), size = 3.5) +
    scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#f7fbff", mid = "#4292c6", high = "#08306b",
                         midpoint = 0.5, limits = c(-0.1, 1)) +
    labs(title = title, x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
}

n <- 5
rho <- 0.6

CS   <- matrix(rho, n, n); diag(CS) <- 1; CS <- CS * 4
AR1  <- outer(0:(n-1), 0:(n-1), function(i, j) rho^abs(i-j)) * 4
Toep <- outer(0:(n-1), 0:(n-1), function(i, j) {
  c(1, 0.7, 0.5, 0.3, 0.2)[abs(i-j)+1]
}) * 4
UN <- matrix(c(4.0, 2.4, 1.8, 1.4, 1.0,
               2.4, 4.2, 2.2, 1.6, 1.1,
               1.8, 2.2, 4.5, 2.4, 1.5,
               1.4, 1.6, 2.4, 4.8, 2.2,
               1.0, 1.1, 1.5, 2.2, 5.0), n, n)

library(patchwork)
(make_cov_plot(CS,   "Compound Symmetry") +
 make_cov_plot(AR1,  "AR(1)")) /
(make_cov_plot(Toep, "Toeplitz") +
 make_cov_plot(UN,   "Unstructured"))

title: 27  Covariance Structures
text:
28.4 Specifying Covariance Structures in R
lme4 does not support custom covariance structures beyond the random effects structure. For explicit covariance modeling, use nlme:


Code
# Simulate data with AR(1) structure
n_people <- 50
n_times  <- 5
rho_true <- 0.7

Y_ar1 <- MASS::mvrnorm(n_people, rep(50, n_times),
                        outer(0:(n_times-1), 0:(n_times-1),
                              function(i,j) rho_true^abs(i-j)) * 9)

df_long <- as.data.frame(Y_ar1) |>
  setNames(paste0("t", 1:n_times)) |>
  mutate(person = 1:n_people) |>
  pivot_longer(-person, names_to = "time", values_to = "Y") |>
  mutate(time_num = as.integer(str_extract(time, "[0-9]")),
         person   = factor(person))

# Fit four competing models
m_cs  <- gls(Y ~ factor(time_num), correlation = corCompSymm(form = ~1 | person), data = df_long)
m_ar1 <- gls(Y ~ factor(time_num), correlation = corAR1(form = ~time_num | person), data = df_long)
m_un  <- gls(Y ~ factor(time_num), correlation = corSymm(form = ~time_num | person),
             weights = varIdent(form = ~1 | factor(time_num)), data = df_long)

# Compare AIC
tibble(
  Model = c("Compound Symmetry", "AR(1)", "Unstructured"),
  AIC   = c(AIC(m_cs), AIC(m_ar1), AIC(m_un)),
  BIC   = c(BIC(m_cs), BIC(m_ar1), BIC(m_un))
) |>
  arrange(AIC) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 1,
    caption = "Model comparison: AR(1) fits better than CS when data has AR structure")



Model comparison: AR(1) fits better than CS when data has AR structure


Model
AIC
BIC




AR(1)
1132.2
1156.7


Unstructured
1143.6
1213.6


Compound Symmetry
1209.9
1234.4

title: 27  Covariance Structures
section: 28.5 Assumptions and How to Check Each Structure
text:
28.5 Assumptions and How to Check Each Structure






WarningAR(1) Assumption: Stationarity



What it means: The mean and variance of observations are constant over time, and the correlation between observations depends only on the time lag, not on the absolute time point.
How to check: Plot the mean and variance of your outcome at each time point. If either is trending systematically, stationarity is violated. A detrended model (with time as a fixed effect) may restore stationarity of the residuals.








WarningUnstructured Assumption: Sufficient Sample Size



What it means: The unstructured model estimates \(T(T+1)/2\) covariance parameters (for \(T\) time points). With 5 time points, that is 15 parameters just for the covariance structure.
How to check: Compare the number of observations to the number of covariance parameters. As a rough guide, you want at least 10× as many observations as parameters.

title: 27  Covariance Structures
text:
28.6 Model Selection Strategy
Use AIC/BIC to compare competing covariance structures:

Start with the AR(1) model as a reasonable default
Compare to compound symmetry (more constrained) and Toeplitz (more flexible)
Try unstructured if sample size allows
Choose the model with the lowest AIC, subject to convergence and interpretability

title: 28  Growth Curve Models
text: 29 Growth Curve Models

title: 28  Growth Curve Models
text:
29.1 Trajectories as the Target
Standard repeated measures models ask: “Is the mean outcome different across time points?” Growth curve models ask something richer: “How does each unit’s trajectory unfold over time, and what explains variation in those trajectories?”
The shift in framing is fundamental. We are not trying to estimate one average change — we are trying to recover the distribution of individual change processes that generated the data. Each person has their own DGP; growth curve models estimate the parameters of that distribution.

title: 28  Growth Curve Models
text:
29.2 The Unconditional Growth Model
Start with no predictors. Just time and people.


Code
n_people <- 60
n_times  <- 6  # weeks

# Each person has a random baseline (intercept) and random rate of change (slope)
person_intercepts <- rnorm(n_people, 50, 8)
person_slopes     <- rnorm(n_people, -2, 1.5)  # average decline of 2/week

df_growth <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, week = 0:(n_times-1)) |>
  mutate(
    stress = person_intercepts[person] +
             person_slopes[person] * week +
             rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    person = factor(person)
  )

# Plot individual trajectories
sample_people <- sample(levels(df_growth$person), 12)

ggplot(df_growth |> filter(person %in% sample_people),
       aes(x = week, y = stress, group = person, color = person)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.7) + geom_point(size = 1.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(data = df_growth, aes(group = NULL, color = NULL),
              method = "lm", color = "black", linewidth = 1.5, se = TRUE) +
  scale_color_viridis_d() +
  labs(title = "Growth curve: individual stress trajectories over 6 weeks",
       subtitle = "Colored lines = 12 individuals; black line = average trajectory",
       x = "Week", y = "Stress (0-100)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")











Code
# Unconditional growth model
m_growth <- lmer(stress ~ week + (week | person), data = df_growth)
summary(m_growth)

title: 28  Growth Curve Models
text:
29.3 Adding Predictors of Growth
The power of growth curve models comes from asking: what predicts the trajectory? We can add level-2 predictors to explain why some people’s intercepts and slopes differ from others.


Code
# Add a predictor: treatment condition
df_growth <- df_growth |>
  mutate(treatment = rep(rbinom(n_people, 1, 0.5), each = n_times))

# Treatment might affect both initial level and rate of change
m_growth_pred <- lmer(stress ~ week * treatment + (week | person),
                      data = df_growth)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_growth_pred, effects = "fixed") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Growth model with treatment: interaction = different slopes by condition")

title: 28  Growth Curve Models
text:
29.4 Nonlinear Growth
Some processes do not change linearly. Symptoms might improve rapidly at first, then plateau. Performance curves often show diminishing returns. The growth curve framework accommodates nonlinearity.


Code
# Quadratic growth
df_growth_q <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, week = 0:8) |>
  mutate(
    person_int    = rep(rnorm(n_people, 50, 8), each = 9),
    person_lin    = rep(rnorm(n_people, -5, 2), each = 9),
    person_quad   = rep(rnorm(n_people, 0.5, 0.3), each = 9),
    stress        = person_int + person_lin * week + person_quad * week^2 + rnorm(n(), 0, 3),
    person        = factor(person)
  )

# Fit with quadratic time
m_quad <- lmer(stress ~ week + I(week^2) + (week | person), data = df_growth_q)

# Plot average predicted trajectory
df_growth_q <- df_growth_q |>
  mutate(fitted_stress = fitted(m_quad))

df_growth_q |>
  group_by(week) |>
  summarise(mean_obs = mean(stress), mean_fit = mean(fitted_stress)) |>
  pivot_longer(-week) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = week, y = value, color = name, linetype = name)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f"),
                     labels = c("Fitted (quadratic)", "Observed")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("solid", "dashed"),
                        labels = c("Fitted (quadratic)", "Observed")) +
  labs(title = "Quadratic growth: curve captures the deceleration",
       x = "Week", y = "Mean Stress", color = NULL, linetype = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)















WarningAssumption: The Growth Function is Correctly Specified



What it means: Linear growth assumes constant rate of change. Quadratic growth assumes a parabolic trajectory. If the true trajectory is neither — e.g., a logarithmic curve or piecewise linear — your model will be misspecified.
How to check: Plot the observed means by time point. Does the pattern look linear? Is there a clear deceleration or acceleration that requires a curve? Use AIC to compare linear vs. quadratic models.








WarningAssumption: Random Slope for Time is Correctly Specified



What it means: Allowing only a random intercept (but not slope) for time assumes everyone changes at exactly the same rate. This is almost always wrong for developmental or therapeutic outcomes.
How to check: Compare the random-intercept-only model to the random-slope model using a likelihood ratio test. Plot individual trajectories — if they clearly fan out or diverge, a random slope is needed.

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
text: 30 Latent Growth Curve Models

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
section: 30.1 Two Languages for the Same Model
text:
30.1 Two Languages for the Same Model
The growth curve model from Part 3 and the Latent Growth Curve Model (LGC) from structural equation modeling are mathematically equivalent. They answer the same questions using different parameterizations and different software.
Understanding both languages is valuable because: 1. Many psychology researchers encounter LGC models in SEM training (Mplus, lavaan) before they encounter MLM-based growth models 2. The SEM framing makes certain things explicit that are implicit in MLM — particularly the role of measurement error and the ability to model multiple growth processes simultaneously 3. Reviewing both frames deepens understanding of what the model is actually doing

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
text:
30.2 The SEM Framing
In the latent growth curve model, the trajectory is represented by two latent variables: - Intercept factor (η_i): The latent initial level for each person - Slope factor (η_s): The latent rate of change for each person
Observed scores at each time point are modeled as: \[Y_{ti} = 1 \cdot \eta_i + \lambda_t \cdot \eta_s + \varepsilon_{ti}\]
where \(\lambda_t\) are factor loadings that encode the time metric (e.g., \(\lambda_1 = 0, \lambda_2 = 1, \lambda_3 = 2\) for linear growth), and \(\varepsilon_{ti}\) are measurement residuals.
The intercept factor has loadings of 1.0 at all time points. The slope factor has loadings equal to the time coding.

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
section: 30.3 Fitting an LGC Model in lavaan
text:
30.3 Fitting an LGC Model in lavaan


Code
# Simulate data
n <- 150
intercepts <- rnorm(n, 50, 8)
slopes     <- rnorm(n, -2, 1.5)

Y <- sapply(0:4, function(t) intercepts + slopes * t + rnorm(n, 0, 4))
colnames(Y) <- paste0("y", 1:5)
df_lgc <- as.data.frame(Y)




Code
# Latent growth curve model in lavaan
lgc_model <- '
  # Intercept factor (loadings all fixed to 1)
  intercept =~ 1*y1 + 1*y2 + 1*y3 + 1*y4 + 1*y5

  # Slope factor (loadings = time coding: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4)
  slope     =~ 0*y1 + 1*y2 + 2*y3 + 3*y4 + 4*y5

  # Means of latent factors (the fixed effects)
  intercept ~ 1
  slope     ~ 1

  # Variances of latent factors (random effects variance)
  intercept ~~ intercept
  slope     ~~ slope

  # Covariance between intercept and slope
  intercept ~~ slope

  # Residual variances (constrained equal across time for simplicity)
  y1 ~~ a*y1
  y2 ~~ a*y2
  y3 ~~ a*y3
  y4 ~~ a*y4
  y5 ~~ a*y5
'

m_lgc <- growth(lgc_model, data = df_lgc)
summary(m_lgc, fit.measures = TRUE)

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
text:
30.4 The Equivalence with MLM


Code
# Fit equivalent MLM
df_long_lgc <- df_lgc |>
  mutate(id = 1:n) |>
  pivot_longer(-id, names_to = "wave", values_to = "Y") |>
  mutate(time = as.integer(str_extract(wave, "[0-9]")) - 1,
         id   = factor(id))

m_mlm <- lmer(Y ~ time + (time | id), data = df_long_lgc)

# Compare estimates
tibble(
  Parameter        = c("Mean intercept", "Mean slope", "Var(intercept)", "Var(slope)"),
  `MLM estimate`   = c(fixef(m_mlm),
                        as.data.frame(VarCorr(m_mlm))$vcov[1:2]),
  `LGC estimate`   = c(
    lavInspect(m_lgc, "mean.lv")[c("intercept", "slope")],
    diag(lavInspect(m_lgc, "cov.lv"))[c("intercept", "slope")]
  )
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "MLM and LGC give equivalent results")



MLM and LGC give equivalent results

The estimates match. The difference is in the language and the software ecosystem — not the math.

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
section: 30.5 When the SEM Framing Adds Value
text:
30.5 When the SEM Framing Adds Value






NoteAdvantage 1: Modeling Measurement Error



In the SEM framework, each observed score \(Y_{ti}\) has a residual \(\varepsilon_{ti}\) that can be interpreted as measurement error — the gap between the true latent trajectory and the imperfect observed score. This is explicit in the LGC model.
In standard MLM, the residual is treated as a mix of measurement error and genuine within-person fluctuation. If your outcome is a measured construct (scale scores, ratings, test scores), the SEM framing forces you to be explicit about measurement.








NoteAdvantage 2: Multiple Growth Processes



With SEM, you can model two interrelated growth curves simultaneously — for instance, how anxiety and depression co-evolve over therapy. The covariance between the two intercept factors and between the two slope factors captures how initial levels and rates of change co-relate.
This is difficult to do cleanly in standard MLM.

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
text:
30.6 Assumptions of LGC Models






WarningAssumption: Correctly Specified Factor Loadings



What it means: The factor loadings for the slope factor encode the time metric. Linear loadings (0, 1, 2, 3…) assume linear growth. If growth is nonlinear, you can free some loadings to be estimated from the data — but the intercept must still be defined by fixing at least two loadings.
How to check: Test alternative loading patterns (free vs. fixed) and compare model fit using CFI, RMSEA, and SRMR.








WarningAssumption: Multivariate Normality



What it means: SEM estimation (default ML) assumes the observed variables are multivariate normal. Severely skewed outcomes or floor/ceiling effects can bias estimates.
How to check: Check univariate skewness and kurtosis. Use robust ML estimators (MLR in lavaan) if normality is questionable.

title: 30  Autoregressive Structures
text: 31 Autoregressive Structures

title: 30  Autoregressive Structures
text:
31.1 The Memory of Time
Some outcomes carry their history with them. Mood at 9am predicts mood at 10am. Anxiety on Monday affects anxiety on Tuesday. GDP in quarter 1 predicts GDP in quarter 2. This temporal dependency — the “memory” of the process — is not just nuisance variance. It is a feature of the DGP that your model must account for.
When you ignore serial dependence and treat residuals as independent, your standard errors are biased and your inference is wrong. More importantly, you are misrepresenting the process: you are claiming the outcome at each time point arises fresh, unconnected from what came before.

title: 30  Autoregressive Structures
text:
31.2 What AR(1) Means
In an autoregressive order 1 (AR(1)) process, each observation is a linear function of the immediately preceding observation plus random noise:
\[Y_t = \phi \cdot Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_\varepsilon)\]
The parameter \(\phi\) is the autoregressive coefficient — how strongly the past predicts the present. When \(\phi = 0\), observations are independent. When \(\phi = 0.9\), yesterday’s value strongly predicts today’s.


Code
# Simulate AR(1) process for one person over 30 days
phi   <- 0.75
sigma <- 3
n_t   <- 30

Y <- numeric(n_t)
Y[1] <- rnorm(1, 50, 5)
for (t in 2:n_t) Y[t] <- phi * Y[t-1] + rnorm(1, 0, sigma)

p1 <- tibble(t = 1:n_t, Y = Y) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = t, y = Y)) +
  geom_line(color = "#1e3a5f", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2, color = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = paste0("AR(1) process: φ = ", phi),
       subtitle = "Each value depends on the previous",
       x = "Day", y = "Outcome") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

# ACF plot
acf_vals <- acf(Y, plot = FALSE, lag.max = 10)
p2 <- tibble(lag = 0:10, acf = acf_vals$acf[,1,1]) |>
  filter(lag > 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = lag, y = acf)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-1, 1) * 1.96 / sqrt(n_t),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  labs(title = "Autocorrelation function (ACF)",
       subtitle = "Should decay exponentially for AR(1)",
       x = "Lag", y = "ACF") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

library(patchwork)
p1 + p2

title: 30  Autoregressive Structures
30  Autoregressive Structures
text:
31.4 Specifying AR(1) in nlme


Code
# Correct model: AR(1) residual structure
m_ar1 <- lme(Y ~ day,
             random      = ~1 | person,
             correlation = corAR1(form = ~day | person),
             data        = df_ar)

# Compare AIC
tibble(
  Model = c("Naive (no AR)", "AR(1)"),
  AIC   = c(AIC(m_naive), AIC(m_ar1)),
  BIC   = c(BIC(m_naive), BIC(m_ar1))
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 1, caption = "AR(1) substantially improves model fit")

title: 30  Autoregressive Structures
text:
31.5 The Assumptions of AR(1) Models






WarningAssumption 1: Stationarity



What it means: The mean and variance of the process are constant over time, and the autocorrelation depends only on the lag, not the absolute time point. An AR(1) process is stationary when \(|\phi| < 1\).
How to check: Plot the mean and variance of your outcome at each time point. Systematic trends violate stationarity. Fit a detrended model (including time as a fixed effect) and check whether residuals are stationary.
What to do if violated: Include time trends as fixed effects. Consider differencing the outcome (analyzing change scores rather than raw scores).








WarningAssumption 2: Only Lag-1 Dependence



What it means: AR(1) assumes that \(Y_{t-2}\) predicts \(Y_t\) only through \(Y_{t-1}\) — once you condition on yesterday, the day before yesterday adds no additional predictive power.
How to check: Examine the partial autocorrelation function (PACF). If the PACF shows significant values at lag 2 or beyond, you need AR(2) or a higher-order model.


Code
pacf_vals <- pacf(Y, plot = FALSE, lag.max = 10)
tibble(lag = 1:10, pacf = pacf_vals$acf[,1,1]) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = lag, y = pacf)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-1, 1) * 1.96 / sqrt(n_t),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  labs(title = "Partial ACF: only lag 1 significant = AR(1) is sufficient",
       x = "Lag", y = "Partial ACF") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: 31  Cross-Lagged Panel Models
text: 32 Cross-Lagged Panel Models

title: 31  Cross-Lagged Panel Models
text:
32.1 Two Variables, Evolving Together
Suppose you want to understand the relationship between anxiety and depression over time. Does anxiety precede and predict depression? Does depression feed back into anxiety? Or do they mutually reinforce each other?
To answer this, you need data on both variables at multiple time points, and you need a model that can disentangle the temporal ordering of their relationship. The cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) is the standard tool for this question.

title: 31  Cross-Lagged Panel Models
text:
32.2 The Classic CLPM
In a two-wave, two-variable CLPM with variables A (anxiety) and D (depression):
\[A_{t+1} = a_{AA} \cdot A_t + a_{DA} \cdot D_t + \varepsilon_{A,t+1}\] \[D_{t+1} = a_{DD} \cdot D_t + a_{AD} \cdot A_t + \varepsilon_{D,t+1}\]
Four parameters: - \(a_{AA}\), \(a_{DD}\): autoregressive effects — stability of each variable over time - \(a_{DA}\): cross-lagged effect of D at time \(t\) on A at time \(t+1\) - \(a_{AD}\): cross-lagged effect of A at time \(t\) on D at time \(t+1\)


Code
# Simulate 3-wave panel data
n <- 200
n_waves <- 3

# True DGP: anxiety -> depression (not the reverse)
A <- matrix(NA, n, n_waves)
D <- matrix(NA, n, n_waves)

A[, 1] <- rnorm(n, 50, 8)
D[, 1] <- rnorm(n, 45, 7)

for (t in 2:n_waves) {
  A[, t] <- 0.6 * A[, t-1] + 0.1 * D[, t-1] + rnorm(n, 0, 5)  # small D->A effect
  D[, t] <- 0.6 * D[, t-1] + 0.3 * A[, t-1] + rnorm(n, 0, 5)  # strong A->D effect
}

df_clpm <- as.data.frame(cbind(A, D)) |>
  setNames(c(paste0("A", 1:3), paste0("D", 1:3)))




Code
# Classic CLPM in lavaan
clpm_model <- '
  # Autoregressive effects
  A2 ~ a_AA * A1
  A3 ~ a_AA * A2
  D2 ~ a_DD * D1
  D3 ~ a_DD * D2

  # Cross-lagged effects
  A2 ~ a_DA * D1
  A3 ~ a_DA * D2
  D2 ~ a_AD * A1
  D3 ~ a_AD * A2

  # Residual covariances (within same wave)
  A2 ~~ D2
  A3 ~~ D3

  # Variance of wave 1
  A1 ~~ A1
  D1 ~~ D1
  A1 ~~ D1
'

m_clpm <- sem(clpm_model, data = df_clpm)
parameterEstimates(m_clpm) |>
  filter(op == "~") |>
  select(lhs, rhs, label, est, se, pvalue) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "CLPM: cross-lagged path from A→D is strong; D→A is weak")

title: 31  Cross-Lagged Panel Models
text:
32.3 The Problem with Classic CLPM
The classic CLPM conflates within-person dynamics with between-person differences. Consider: people who are chronically anxious tend to also be chronically depressed (stable between-person correlation). The cross-lagged paths in the classic CLPM pick up both this stable between-person covariation and the genuine within-person dynamic — they cannot be separated.
This means even if there is no within-person causal relationship between anxiety and depression (i.e., changes in anxiety within a person do not cause changes in depression), the classic CLPM can show significant “cross-lagged” effects simply because chronically anxious people are also chronically depressed.

title: 31  Cross-Lagged Panel Models
text:
32.4 The Random Intercept CLPM (RI-CLPM)
Hamaker, Kuiper, & Grasman (2015) proposed the Random Intercept CLPM, which solves this by separating two sources of variation:

Between-person: Stable trait levels (random intercepts) that explain why some people are chronically higher on A or D
Within-person: Fluctuations around each person’s stable level, and the cross-lagged dynamics among those fluctuations



Code
ri_clpm_model <- '
  # Random intercepts (stable between-person levels)
  RI_A =~ 1*A1 + 1*A2 + 1*A3
  RI_D =~ 1*D1 + 1*D2 + 1*D3

  # Within-person components (deviations from stable level)
  wA1 =~ 1*A1; wA2 =~ 1*A2; wA3 =~ 1*A3
  wD1 =~ 1*D1; wD2 =~ 1*D2; wD3 =~ 1*D3

  # Constrain observed variances to 0 (all captured by RI + within)
  A1 ~~ 0*A1; A2 ~~ 0*A2; A3 ~~ 0*A3
  D1 ~~ 0*D1; D2 ~~ 0*D2; D3 ~~ 0*D3

  # Within-person cross-lagged dynamics
  wA2 ~ a_AA*wA1 + a_DA*wD1
  wA3 ~ a_AA*wA2 + a_DA*wD2
  wD2 ~ a_DD*wD1 + a_AD*wA1
  wD3 ~ a_DD*wD2 + a_AD*wA2

  # Random intercept variances and covariance
  RI_A ~~ RI_A; RI_D ~~ RI_D; RI_A ~~ RI_D

  # Within-person residual variances
  wA1 ~~ wA1; wD1 ~~ wD1
  wA2 ~~ wA2; wD2 ~~ wD2
  wA3 ~~ wA3; wD3 ~~ wD3
  wA2 ~~ wD2; wA3 ~~ wD3

  # Mean structure
  RI_A ~ 1; RI_D ~ 1
  wA1 ~ 0; wD1 ~ 0
'

m_ri_clpm <- sem(ri_clpm_model, data = df_clpm, estimator = "ML")

parameterEstimates(m_ri_clpm) |>
  filter(op == "~", lhs %in% c("wA2","wA3","wD2","wD3")) |>
  select(lhs, rhs, label, est, se, pvalue) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "RI-CLPM: within-person cross-lagged effects (controls for stable between-person differences)")

title: 31  Cross-Lagged Panel Models
text:
32.5 Comparing CLPM vs. RI-CLPM



Feature
Classic CLPM
RI-CLPM




Controls for stable between-person differences?
No
Yes


Cross-lagged = within-person dynamics?
No (confounded)
Yes


More parameters?
No
Yes (random intercepts)


Better for causal within-person questions?
No
Yes









WarningAssumption: Stationarity of Cross-Lagged Effects



What it means: The CLPM (and RI-CLPM) typically constrains cross-lagged effects to be equal across waves. This assumes the dynamics do not change over the study period.
How to check: Fit an unconstrained model (different path coefficients at each wave) and compare fit to the constrained model.








WarningAssumption: No Omitted Dynamics



What it means: Both models assume all relevant variables are included. An omitted third variable that drives both A and D could produce spurious cross-lagged effects.
How to check: Think carefully about what else changes over time that could explain both variables. Include plausible third variables as additional nodes.

title: 32  Binary and Count Outcomes Over Time
text: 33 Binary and Count Outcomes Over Time
crumbs: 32  Binary and Count Outcomes Over Time

title: 32  Binary and Count Outcomes Over Time
section: 33.1 When the DGP Does Not Generate Continuous Data
text:
33.1 When the DGP Does Not Generate Continuous Data
Every model we have used in this module so far assumes something about your outcome: that the true data-generating process (DGP) produces values that, after accounting for predictors and random effects, are normally distributed. That assumption is perfectly reasonable for outcomes like stress scores, reaction times, or cognitive performance — values that can in principle be any real number.
But many longitudinal outcomes in psychology and medicine are not continuous. They are binary — did the person have a depressive episode this week? Did they relapse? Did they complete the task? Or they are counts — how many panic attacks occurred this week? How many drinks did someone consume on Monday? How many hospital visits did a patient have this year?
When the DGP generates binary or count data, a linear mixed model imposes the wrong structure on that DGP. Predicted probabilities can fall outside [0, 1]. Residuals cannot be normally distributed. Parameter estimates become biased. The model is not just inefficient — it is misspecified.
Generalized Linear Mixed Models (GLMMs) extend the mixed model framework to non-normal outcomes. They combine:

A random effects structure (exactly as in lmer) to handle nesting and repeated measures
A link function that transforms the linear predictor to the appropriate scale
A distributional family that matches the DGP

The longitudinal setting makes GLMMs especially important, because tracking people over time almost guarantees you will encounter binary events and count outcomes alongside the usual continuous measures.
crumbs: 32  Binary and Count Outcomes Over Time

title: 32  Binary and Count Outcomes Over Time
section: 33.2 Part 1: Binary Longitudinal Outcomes — The Logistic Mixed Model
text:
33.2 Part 1: Binary Longitudinal Outcomes — The Logistic Mixed Model

33.2.1 The Model
For a binary outcome \(Y_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}\) measured at occasion \(i\) for person \(j\), the logistic mixed model is:
\[\text{logit}\bigl(P(Y_{ij} = 1)\bigr) = \underbrace{\beta_0}_{\substack{\text{average log-odds} \\ \text{at baseline}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{person's deviation} \\ \text{from average}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1 \cdot \text{week}_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{change in log-odds} \\ \text{per week}}} + \underbrace{\beta_2 \cdot \text{treatment}_j}_{\substack{\text{treatment effect} \\ \text{on log-odds scale}}}\]
where:

\(\text{logit}(p) = \log\frac{p}{1-p}\) is the log-odds of the event
\(u_{0j} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^2)\) is a person-level random intercept — capturing the fact that some people are generally more likely to have an episode, regardless of treatment or time
The fixed effects \(\beta_1\) and \(\beta_2\) operate on the log-odds scale, so we exponentiate them to get odds ratios

This is exactly the linear mixed model, but with a logit link function wrapping the left-hand side.


33.2.2 Simulating a Longitudinal Intervention Study
We simulate 60 participants tracked over 6 weeks. Half are randomly assigned to a treatment that halves the odds of having a depressive episode each week. The outcome is binary: whether the participant had at least one depressive episode that week.


Code
n_people    <- 60
n_weeks     <- 6
treatment   <- rep(c(0, 1), each = n_people / 2)

# Person-level random intercepts (on log-odds scale)
u0 <- rnorm(n_people, 0, 0.8)

df_binary <- expand_grid(
  person = 1:n_people,
  week   = 0:(n_weeks - 1)
) |>
  mutate(
    treatment = treatment[person],
    u0        = u0[person],
    # True log-odds: intercept + time trend + treatment effect + random intercept
    log_odds  = -0.5 + u0 - 0.1 * week - 0.7 * treatment,
    prob      = plogis(log_odds),
    episode   = rbinom(n(), 1, prob),
    person    = factor(person),
    treatment = factor(treatment, labels = c("Control", "Treatment"))
  )

# Quick look at observed proportions by week and treatment
df_binary |>
  group_by(week, treatment) |>
  summarise(prop_episode = mean(episode), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = week, y = prop_episode, color = treatment, group = treatment)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#1e3a5f", "#e63946")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Proportion with depressive episode each week",
    subtitle = "Observed proportions by treatment group",
    x = "Week", y = "Proportion with episode", color = "Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











33.2.3 Fitting the Logistic Mixed Model


Code
m_binary <- glmer(
  episode ~ week * treatment + (1 | person),
  data   = df_binary,
  family = binomial
)

summary(m_binary)

  Approximation) [glmerMod]

33.2.4 Reading the Output
Fixed effects come out on the log-odds scale. To interpret them as odds ratios, exponentiate:


Code
broom.mixed::tidy(m_binary, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Fixed effects as odds ratios (exponentiated log-odds)")



Fixed effects as odds ratios (exponentiated log-odds)


term
estimate
conf.low
conf.high
p.value




(Intercept)
0.789
0.438
1.422
0.431


week
0.837
0.693
1.012
0.066


treatmentTreatment
0.510
0.215
1.206
0.125


week:treatmentTreatment
1.111
0.844
1.462
0.455





The treatment odds ratio tells you: among people at the same week and with the same random intercept, how does the odds of an episode in the treatment group compare to the control group? If OR = 0.50, treatment halves the odds — which is what we simulated.


33.2.5 Marginal Predicted Probabilities
Odds ratios describe the model’s internal logic, but audiences (and your future self) think in probabilities, not log-odds. ggeffects::ggpredict() computes marginal predicted probabilities — averaging over the random effects distribution rather than conditioning on a specific individual.


Code
preds_binary <- ggpredict(m_binary, terms = c("week", "treatment"), type = "fixed")

plot(preds_binary) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#1e3a5f", "#e63946")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c("#1e3a5f", "#e63946")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Marginal predicted probability of a depressive episode",
    subtitle = "Averaging over individual differences (marginal effect)",
    x = "Week", y = "Predicted probability", color = "Group", fill = "Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











33.2.6 Marginal vs. Conditional Effects
This distinction matters more in GLMMs than in linear models, because the logit link is nonlinear.

A conditional effect asks: “For a specific person (with a specific \(u_{0j}\)), what is the predicted probability?” It conditions on knowing that person’s random intercept.
A marginal effect asks: “For an average person drawn from this population, what is the predicted probability?” It averages over the distribution of random effects.

The conditional effect is the right answer for individual-level prediction — predicting whether this patient will have an episode. The marginal effect is the right answer for policy-level inference — estimating the population-average impact of a treatment.
ggpredict(..., type = "fixed") gives marginal effects. ggpredict(..., type = "random") gives predictions for an average individual (random effects set to zero). Neither is wrong — they answer different questions.






Warning



Assumption: Correct link function
Logistic regression assumes the log-odds of the outcome are a linear function of the predictors. For bounded probabilities near 0 or 1, this is usually reasonable. The probit link is an alternative if you have theoretical reasons to prefer it (probit assumes a latent Gaussian threshold; logistic assumes a logistic latent distribution). In practice, they rarely differ much.








Warning



Assumption: No overdispersion in binomial outcomes
Binomial outcomes (0/1 per trial) cannot be overdispersed in the strict statistical sense — by definition, a Bernoulli draw has variance \(p(1-p)\). However, if your outcome is a proportion based on multiple trials (e.g., 3 out of 5 tasks completed), overdispersion can occur. Check it with Pearson \(\chi^2\) / df. For strict 0/1 data, this is not a concern.








Warning



Assumption: Normality of random effects on the log-odds scale
The random intercepts \(u_{0j}\) are assumed to follow a normal distribution on the log-odds scale — not on the probability scale. This means a person with \(u_{0j} = 2\) is simply a person with systematically higher log-odds. The distribution of random effects is not directly observable, but you can inspect BLUPs (Best Linear Unbiased Predictors) for gross departures from normality.
crumbs: 32  Binary and Count Outcomes Over Time

title: 32  Binary and Count Outcomes Over Time
section: 33.3 Part 2: Count Longitudinal Outcomes — Poisson and Negative Binomial
text:
33.3 Part 2: Count Longitudinal Outcomes — Poisson and Negative Binomial

33.3.1 Why Counts Need Different Models
Count outcomes (0, 1, 2, 3, …) have two properties that break standard regression assumptions:

Non-negativity. Counts cannot be negative. A linear model can happily predict −2 hospitalizations.
Mean-variance relationship. For a Poisson process, the variance equals the mean. Higher-count people are intrinsically more variable. A linear model assumes constant variance.

The Poisson mixed model handles both:
\[\log\bigl(\mu_{ij}\bigr) = \underbrace{\beta_0}_{\substack{\text{log expected count} \\ \text{at baseline}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{person's log-rate} \\ \text{deviation}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1 \cdot \text{visit}_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{change in log-count} \\ \text{per visit}}} + \underbrace{\beta_2 \cdot \text{treatment}_j}_{\substack{\text{treatment effect} \\ \text{on log-count scale}}}\]
The log link ensures predicted counts are always positive. Exponentiating fixed effects gives rate ratios (also called incidence rate ratios).


33.3.2 Simulating Longitudinal Count Data
We simulate 80 patients tracked over 8 clinic visits. The count outcome is the number of pain flares per week between visits.


Code
n_patients  <- 80
n_visits    <- 8
treatment_c <- rep(c(0, 1), each = n_patients / 2)

u0_count <- rnorm(n_patients, 0, 0.5)

df_count <- expand_grid(
  patient = 1:n_patients,
  visit   = 1:n_visits
) |>
  mutate(
    treatment = treatment_c[patient],
    u0        = u0_count[patient],
    log_mu    = 1.8 + u0 - 0.08 * visit - 0.5 * treatment,
    mu        = exp(log_mu),
    flares    = rpois(n(), mu),
    patient   = factor(patient),
    treatment = factor(treatment, labels = c("Control", "Treatment"))
  )

# Observed mean counts by visit and treatment
df_count |>
  group_by(visit, treatment) |>
  summarise(mean_flares = mean(flares), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = visit, y = mean_flares, color = treatment, group = treatment)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#1e3a5f", "#e63946")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Mean pain flares per week, by clinic visit",
    subtitle = "Observed means by treatment group",
    x = "Visit number", y = "Mean weekly flares", color = "Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











33.3.3 Fitting the Poisson Mixed Model


Code
m_poisson <- glmmTMB(
  flares ~ visit + treatment + (1 | patient),
  data   = df_count,
  family = poisson
)

summary(m_poisson)


 Family: poisson  ( log )
Formula:          flares ~ visit + treatment + (1 | patient)
Data: df_count

      AIC       BIC    logLik -2*log(L)  df.resid 
   2706.8    2724.6   -1349.4    2698.8       636 

Random effects:

Conditional model:
 Groups  Name        Variance Std.Dev.
 patient (Intercept) 0.2215   0.4706  
Number of obs: 640, groups:  patient, 80

Conditional model:
                    Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)         1.792426   0.087202  20.555  < 2e-16 ***
visit              -0.084425   0.008726  -9.675  < 2e-16 ***
treatmentTreatment -0.337426   0.113964  -2.961  0.00307 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1




33.3.4 Checking for Overdispersion
The Poisson model assumes variance = mean. If your data are more variable than this (which is common in practice — people differ in unmeasured ways), the model will underestimate standard errors and produce overconfident p-values. This is called overdispersion.
A simple check: compute the Pearson statistic divided by the residual degrees of freedom. If this ratio is substantially greater than 1 (a common rule of thumb is > 1.5), overdispersion is present.


Code
# Pearson statistic / residual df
pearson_resid <- residuals(m_poisson, type = "pearson")
dispersion_ratio <- sum(pearson_resid^2) / df.residual(m_poisson)

cat("Dispersion ratio (Pearson χ² / df):", round(dispersion_ratio, 3), "\n")


Dispersion ratio (Pearson χ² / df): 0.824 


Code
cat("Interpretation: values near 1.0 indicate no overdispersion;\n")


Interpretation: values near 1.0 indicate no overdispersion;


Code
cat("values > 1.5 suggest the negative binomial model is preferred.\n")


values > 1.5 suggest the negative binomial model is preferred.




33.3.5 Negative Binomial Model
The negative binomial adds a dispersion parameter (\(\theta\)) that allows variance to exceed the mean:
\[\text{Var}(Y) = \mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\theta}\]
When \(\theta \to \infty\), the negative binomial reduces to Poisson. Smaller \(\theta\) means more overdispersion (more between-person variance in counts beyond what the Poisson allows).


Code
m_negbin <- glmmTMB(
  flares ~ visit + treatment + (1 | patient),
  data   = df_count,
  family = nbinom2
)

summary(m_negbin)

Formula:          flares ~ visit + treatment + (1 | patient)
Data: df_count

Number of obs: 640, groups:  patient, 80

Dispersion parameter for nbinom2 family (): 8.82e+06

(Intercept)         1.792426   0.087202  20.555  < 2e-16 ***
visit              -0.084425   0.008726  -9.675  < 2e-16 ***
treatmentTreatment -0.337426   0.113964  -2.961  0.00307 ** 
---

  knitr::kable(caption = "Model comparison: Poisson vs. Negative Binomial")



Model comparison: Poisson vs. Negative Binomial
