33.3.6 Predicted Count Trajectories


Code
preds_count <- ggpredict(m_negbin, terms = c("visit", "treatment"), type = "fixed")

plot(preds_count) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#1e3a5f", "#e63946")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c("#1e3a5f", "#e63946")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted pain flares per week over clinic visits",
    subtitle = "Marginal predictions from negative binomial mixed model",
    x = "Visit", y = "Predicted flares", color = "Group", fill = "Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











33.3.7 What About Zero-Inflation?
In many longitudinal count outcomes, there are far more zeros than a Poisson or negative binomial distribution would predict. For example, in a study of alcohol use, many people on many days consume zero drinks. A standard count model will underfit these zeros and overfit the positive counts.
glmmTMB handles this cleanly with a zero-inflation component:
# Zero-inflated negative binomial (not run — for illustration)
m_zinb <- glmmTMB(
  flares ~ visit + treatment + (1 | patient),
  ziformula = ~1,        # intercept-only zero-inflation model
  family    = nbinom2,
  data      = df_count
)
The zero-inflation formula ziformula models the probability that an observation comes from the “always zero” process (e.g., someone who never drinks) rather than the count-generating process. You can make this a function of predictors too.






Warning



Assumption: Correct distributional family
Start with Poisson. Check the dispersion ratio. If > 1.5, switch to negative binomial (nbinom2). If you have many more zeros than either model predicts (compare prop_zero in your data to mean(predict(..., type="response") == 0)), add zero-inflation. The model should match the DGP that generated your outcome.








Warning



Assumption: Log link is appropriate
The log link assumes that predictor effects are multiplicative on the count scale (each unit increase in X multiplies the expected count by \(e^\beta\)). This is usually sensible for rate outcomes. If you have strong theoretical reasons to expect additive effects on the count scale, the identity link is possible but numerically unstable with count data.
crumbs: 32  Binary and Count Outcomes Over Time

title: 32  Binary and Count Outcomes Over Time
section: 33.4 Putting It Together: GLMMs and the DGP
text:
33.4 Putting It Together: GLMMs and the DGP
The key insight is that GLMMs are not a special case of mixed models — they are a more faithful representation of the DGP when your outcome is binary or a count. Forcing binary or count data into a linear mixed model is equivalent to saying “I know the DGP generates counts, but I will pretend it generates continuous normal data.” The parameter estimates you get from that misspecified model are biased and not meaningful.
GLMMs respect the DGP:










Outcome type
DGP generates
Appropriate model
Link function
Exponentiate for




0/1 event
Binary Bernoulli trials
glmer(..., family = binomial)
logit
Odds ratios


Counts (no overdispersion)
Poisson process
glmmTMB(..., family = poisson)
log
Rate ratios


Counts (overdispersed)
Negative binomial process
glmmTMB(..., family = nbinom2)
log
Rate ratios


Counts (excess zeros)
Zero-inflated process
glmmTMB(..., ziformula = ~1, family = nbinom2)
log
Rate ratios



In every case, the random effects structure stays exactly as it would be in a linear mixed model: participants are nested within themselves across time, and the random intercept (or random slope, if you have one) captures stable individual differences in the outcome on the transformed scale.
crumbs: 32  Binary and Count Outcomes Over Time

title: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events
text: 34 Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events
crumbs: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events

title: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events
text:
34.1 When the Outcome Is Time-to-Event
Many longitudinal outcomes of interest are not scores that change over time — they are events: relapse, remission, dropout, divorce, promotion, death. The scientific question is not “how did the score change?” but “how long until the event happened, and what predicts the rate at which it happens?”
Survival analysis (also called event history analysis or time-to-event analysis) is the appropriate framework for these outcomes. It addresses three features that distinguish event data from ordinary repeated measures:

Censoring. Not every person experiences the event during the study window. Someone who has not relapsed by the end of the study contributed real information — they survived at least that long — but you do not know when (or whether) they eventually relapsed. Discarding them would introduce selection bias. Treating them as non-events would distort the rate estimates.
Non-constant hazard. The risk of an event is not constant over time. Depression relapse is highest in the first months after remission. Divorce peaks around the 7-year mark. Survival models estimate a hazard function — the instantaneous rate of the event at each time point, conditional on having survived to that point.
Nesting. When participants are nested in clinics, teams, or therapists, standard survival models assume independence that is violated. Frailty models extend survival analysis to handle clustering, exactly as random intercepts extend regression.
crumbs: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events

title: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events
section: 34.2 The Hazard Function and Survival Function
text:
34.2 The Hazard Function and Survival Function
The two central quantities in survival analysis are mathematically related:
The survival function \(S(t)\) gives the probability of surviving (not experiencing the event) past time \(t\):
\[S(t) = P(T > t)\]
The hazard function \(h(t)\) gives the instantaneous rate of the event at time \(t\), given survival to that point:
\[h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t}\]
They are linked by:
\[S(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t h(u)\, du\right)\]
A high hazard at time \(t\) means the event is likely to occur around that time for people who have survived. Survival curves fall steeply when hazards are high.
crumbs: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events

title: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events
text:
34.3 Step 1: The Kaplan-Meier Estimator
The Kaplan-Meier (KM) estimator is the standard nonparametric estimate of the survival function. It makes no assumptions about the shape of the hazard function and handles censoring correctly.

34.3.1 Simulating Time-to-Relapse Data
We simulate 120 patients in a depression remission study. Half receive a maintenance therapy; half receive standard care. We track time-to-relapse (in weeks) over a 52-week follow-up. Patients who have not relapsed by week 52 are censored.


Code
n_patients <- 120
treatment  <- rep(c(0, 1), each = n_patients / 2)

# True relapse times: exponential with treatment-dependent rate
set.seed(2024)
rate_control  <- 0.025  # average relapse ~40 weeks
rate_treatment <- 0.012  # treatment roughly halves the rate

relapse_time <- ifelse(
  treatment == 0,
  rexp(n_patients, rate = rate_control),
  rexp(n_patients, rate = rate_treatment)
)

# Censoring at week 52
follow_up  <- 52
event_time <- pmin(relapse_time, follow_up)
event      <- as.integer(relapse_time <= follow_up)

df_surv <- tibble(
  patient   = 1:n_patients,
  time      = event_time,
  event     = event,
  treatment = factor(treatment, labels = c("Standard care", "Maintenance therapy"))
)

# Quick summary
df_surv |>
  group_by(treatment) |>
  summarise(
    n_events  = sum(event),
    n_censored = sum(1 - event),
    median_time = median(time[event == 1]),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Event summary by treatment group")



Event summary by treatment group


treatment
n_events
n_censored
median_time




Standard care
44
16
20.04866


Maintenance therapy
34

27.30527







34.3.2 Fitting and Plotting the Kaplan-Meier Curve


Code
km_fit <- survfit(
  Surv(time, event) ~ treatment,
  data = df_surv
)

# Base R version for reproducibility
plot_data <- broom::tidy(km_fit)

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = time, y = estimate, color = strata, fill = strata)) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 0.15, color = NA) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("#1e3a5f", "#e63946"),
    labels = c("Standard care", "Maintenance therapy")
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("#1e3a5f", "#e63946"),
    labels = c("Standard care", "Maintenance therapy")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title    = "Kaplan-Meier survival curves: time to depression relapse",
    subtitle = "Shaded bands = 95% confidence intervals. Steps at each event time.",
    x = "Weeks since remission",
    y = "Probability of remaining relapse-free",
    color = "Group", fill = "Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











34.3.3 Reading the KM Curve
The KM curve starts at \(S(0) = 1\) and steps down at each event time. The median survival time is the time at which the curve crosses 0.50 — half the group has relapsed by that point.
Censored observations appear as tick marks on the curve (here suppressed for clarity). Patients who are censored contribute their partial information: they reduce the “at risk” count at later times without contributing a step down in the survival curve.
crumbs: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events

title: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events
text:
34.4 Step 2: The Log-Rank Test
The log-rank test compares survival curves between groups without assuming a parametric shape. It tests \(H_0: S_1(t) = S_2(t)\) for all \(t\).


Code
logrank_test <- survdiff(Surv(time, event) ~ treatment, data = df_surv)
logrank_test


Call:
survdiff(formula = Surv(time, event) ~ treatment, data = df_surv)

                               N Observed Expected (O-E)^2/E (O-E)^2/V
treatment=Standard care       60       44     34.2      2.79      5.01
treatment=Maintenance therapy 60       34     43.8      2.18      5.01

 Chisq= 5  on 1 degrees of freedom, p= 0.03 


The log-rank test is most powerful when the hazard ratio between groups is approximately constant over time (proportional hazards assumption). If the curves cross — one group has higher early hazard, the other higher late hazard — the log-rank test loses power and may be misleading.
crumbs: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events

title: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events
section: 34.5 Step 3: The Cox Proportional Hazards Model
text:
34.5 Step 3: The Cox Proportional Hazards Model
The Cox model is the standard regression tool for survival outcomes. It models the hazard as a function of covariates without specifying the shape of the baseline hazard:
\[h(t \mid X_j) = \underbrace{h_0(t)}_{\substack{\text{baseline hazard} \\ \text{(unspecified)}}} \cdot \underbrace{\exp(\beta_1 X_{1j} + \beta_2 X_{2j} + \cdots)}_{\substack{\text{multiplicative predictor} \\ \text{effect on hazard rate}}}\]

\(h_0(t)\) is the baseline hazard — the hazard for someone with all covariates at zero. Its shape is left completely unspecified (this is why Cox is called “semi-parametric”).
Each \(\exp(\beta_k)\) is a hazard ratio: the multiplicative factor by which the hazard changes for a one-unit increase in \(X_k\), holding other covariates constant.
A hazard ratio of 0.5 means the event rate is halved at every time point.


34.5.1 Fitting the Cox Model


Code
cox_model <- coxph(
  Surv(time, event) ~ treatment,
  data = df_surv
)

broom::tidy(cox_model, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Cox model: hazard ratios. HR < 1 means lower hazard (better prognosis) in treatment group."
  )



Cox model: hazard ratios. HR < 1 means lower hazard (better prognosis) in treatment group.


term
estimate
conf.low
conf.high
p.value




treatmentMaintenance therapy
0.602
0.384
0.943
0.027





The treatment hazard ratio tells you: at any given week, what is the ratio of the relapse rate in the maintenance therapy group to the standard care group? A hazard ratio of ~0.5 means the maintenance therapy cuts the instantaneous relapse rate in half at every time point — which is what we simulated.


34.5.2 The Proportional Hazards Assumption
The Cox model assumes the hazard ratio is constant over time — the curves diverge proportionally. If the treatment effect fades over time (hazard ratio drifts toward 1.0 at later weeks), the model is misspecified.


Code
cox_test <- cox.zph(cox_model)
cox_test


          chisq df   p
treatment 0.273  1 0.6
GLOBAL    0.273  1 0.6


Code
# Schoenfeld residual plot
ggplot(
  data.frame(
    time     = cox_test$time,
    residual = cox_test$y[, 1]
  ),
  aes(x = time, y = residual)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "#e63946", se = TRUE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title    = "Schoenfeld residuals: testing proportional hazards",
    subtitle = "A flat loess line supports proportional hazards; a trend suggests time-varying effect",
    x = "Time", y = "Schoenfeld residual"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









A non-significant global test (cox.zph) and a flat Schoenfeld residual plot both support the proportional hazards assumption. If the assumption is violated, you can add time-by-covariate interaction terms (tt() in the survival package) or stratify by the violating variable.
crumbs: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events

title: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events
section: 34.6 Step 4: Frailty Models — Handling Clustered Survival Data
text:
34.6 Step 4: Frailty Models — Handling Clustered Survival Data
When participants are nested in groups (patients within hospitals, employees within teams), standard Cox models assume independence between observations. This assumption is violated in clustered data. The analogue of a random intercept in survival analysis is a frailty — a latent multiplicative factor that shifts the hazard up or down for everyone in a cluster.
The frailty model:
\[h_{ij}(t) = h_0(t) \cdot \exp(\beta X_{ij}) \cdot \underbrace{Z_j}_{\substack{\text{cluster frailty} \\ Z_j \sim \text{Gamma}(1, \theta)}}\]
\(Z_j\) is the frailty for cluster \(j\). If \(Z_j > 1\), all patients in that cluster have higher hazard (frailty variance \(\theta\) captures how much clusters differ).

34.6.1 Simulating Clustered Survival Data


Code
n_hospitals  <- 15
n_per_hosp   <- 8  # patients per hospital
n_total      <- n_hospitals * n_per_hosp

hospital     <- rep(1:n_hospitals, each = n_per_hosp)
treatment_h  <- rep(rep(c(0, 1), each = n_per_hosp / 2), n_hospitals)

# Hospital-level frailty (Gamma distributed, mean=1)
hospital_frailty <- rgamma(n_hospitals, shape = 2, rate = 2)  # var = 0.5

# Patient-level times
base_rate   <- 0.02
indiv_time  <- rexp(
  n_total,
  rate = base_rate * hospital_frailty[hospital] * exp(-0.5 * treatment_h)
)

event_frailty <- as.integer(indiv_time <= follow_up)
time_frailty  <- pmin(indiv_time, follow_up)

df_frailty <- tibble(
  patient    = 1:n_total,
  hospital   = factor(hospital),
  time       = time_frailty,
  event      = event_frailty,
  treatment  = factor(treatment_h, labels = c("Control", "Treatment"))
)




34.6.2 Fitting the Frailty Model


Code
cox_frailty <- coxph(
  Surv(time, event) ~ treatment + frailty(hospital, distribution = "gamma"),
  data = df_frailty
)

summary(cox_frailty)


Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(time, event) ~ treatment + frailty(hospital, 
    distribution = "gamma"), data = df_frailty)

  n= 120, number of events= 77 

                          coef    se(coef) se2    Chisq DF   p    
treatmentTreatment        -0.4353 0.2324   0.2304  3.51 1.00 0.061
frailty(hospital, distrib                         13.26 7.13 0.070

                   exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95
treatmentTreatment    0.6471      1.545    0.4104      1.02

Iterations: 7 outer, 24 Newton-Raphson
     Variance of random effect= 0.1832805   I-likelihood = -333.4 
Degrees of freedom for terms= 1.0 7.1 
Concordance= 0.668  (se = 0.032 )
Likelihood ratio test= 23.04  on 8.12 df,   p=0.004


The frailty variance estimate tells you how much hospitals differ in their baseline hazard. Large frailty variance means the hospital you are treated at matters a great deal for your prognosis — independent of treatment. This is the survival analogue of a large ICC in a linear mixed model.
crumbs: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events

title: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events
text:
34.7 Assumptions Summary






WarningAssumption: Non-Informative Censoring



Censoring must be independent of the event process. If sicker patients are more likely to drop out (informative censoring), survival estimates will be biased upward — the sample becomes increasingly healthy as patients are lost, making the event appear rarer than it is.
How to check: Compare baseline characteristics between censored and uncensored patients. If they differ systematically, consider sensitivity analyses (e.g., worst-case imputation) or inverse-probability-of-censoring weighting.








WarningAssumption: Proportional Hazards (Cox Model Only)



The Cox model requires the hazard ratio between groups to be constant over time. If treatment effects fade or accelerate, the proportional hazards assumption is violated.
How to check: Run cox.zph(). If significant, plot Schoenfeld residuals. Solutions: add time-by-treatment interaction, stratify by the violating variable, or use an accelerated failure time (AFT) model, which makes a different structural assumption (time ratios rather than hazard ratios).








WarningAssumption: Correct Functional Form (Parametric Models)



If you use a parametric survival model (exponential, Weibull, log-normal), you are asserting a specific shape for the baseline hazard. These models are more efficient than Cox when the assumption is correct but badly misspecified when it is wrong.
How to check: Plot the KM curve and compare it visually to the parametric fit. The log-cumulative hazard plot can distinguish Weibull from exponential shapes.
crumbs: 33  Survival Analysis for Longitudinal Events

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
text: 35 Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
section: 35.1 Why Longitudinal Data Are Especially Prone to Missingness
text:
35.1 Why Longitudinal Data Are Especially Prone to Missingness
Cross-sectional surveys have one chance to collect data. Longitudinal studies have multiple waves — and at each wave, participants can fail to provide data. This creates a missingness structure that is both richer and more dangerous than in cross-sectional research.
Consider a 4-wave panel study. Even if each wave has 90% completion, only \(0.9^4 \approx 66\%\) of participants complete all four waves. With 80% completion per wave: \(0.8^4 \approx 41\%\). The complete-case sample — participants with data at all waves — can be a small and highly selected subset of the original enrollment.
This matters because why people drop out is rarely random. Participants who are struggling are more likely to drop out. Participants who improved may feel they no longer need to participate. These are mechanisms that create systematic relationships between missingness and the outcome — exactly the conditions that bias estimates.
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
section: 35.2 The Three Mechanisms of Missing Data
text:
35.2 The Three Mechanisms of Missing Data
Missing data mechanisms determine how severe the bias is and what can be done about it. Following Rubin (1976), there are three:

35.2.1 Missing Completely At Random (MCAR)
Whether an observation is missing is independent of everything — both observed and unobserved variables. This is effectively a random subsample of the full data. Complete-case analysis is unbiased under MCAR, but still inefficient (you lose information).
Example: A participant’s data are missing because their file was accidentally deleted by the research assistant. The deletion has nothing to do with their health status, demographics, or outcomes.


35.2.2 Missing At Random (MAR)
Whether an observation is missing depends on observed variables in the dataset, but not on the missing value itself after conditioning on those observed variables. This is a conditional independence assumption: missingness is predictable from what you have.
Example: Older participants are more likely to drop out of a study, and older participants also tend to have worse health at baseline. Once you condition on age (an observed variable), missingness is independent of the unobserved future health. Modern imputation methods (Multiple Imputation, Full Information Maximum Likelihood) are valid under MAR.


35.2.3 Missing Not At Random (MNAR)
Whether an observation is missing depends on the missing value itself, even after conditioning on all observed variables. This is the dangerous case — no standard analysis fully corrects for it.
Example: Participants drop out because their depression worsened — and worse depression directly predicts dropout, over and above any observed baseline predictors. Your observed data systematically underrepresent the bad outcomes.






WarningAssumption: MAR or Better



Multiple imputation and FIML are only valid if the data are MAR (or MCAR). If data are MNAR, these methods reduce but do not eliminate bias. There is no fully satisfying solution to MNAR missingness — it requires either selection models (joint model for outcome and dropout), pattern mixture models, or sensitivity analyses that bound the possible bias.
How to check: You cannot directly test MNAR from observed data (it involves the unobserved values). Proxy indicators: do dropouts differ from completers on observed baseline variables? Does the rate of dropout vary by initial outcome level? These patterns are consistent with MNAR, though not diagnostic.
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
text:
35.3 Step 1: Diagnosing Missingness
Before imputing or excluding, understand the pattern and mechanism.


Code
# Simulate 4-wave panel study with dropout
n_people <- 150
n_waves  <- 4

person_baseline <- rnorm(n_people, 50, 10)
person_trt      <- rep(c(0, 1), each = n_people / 2)

# Generate complete data
df_complete <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, wave = 1:n_waves) |>
  mutate(
    baseline  = person_baseline[person],
    treatment = person_trt[person],
    Y_true    = baseline - 3 * (wave - 1) + 5 * treatment * (wave - 1) + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    person    = factor(person)
  )

# Introduce MAR missingness: higher dropout for people with worse baseline
dropout_prob <- plogis(-2 + 0.03 * (50 - person_baseline) + 0.4 * (df_complete$wave - 1))
missing_flag <- rbinom(nrow(df_complete), 1, dropout_prob)

df_obs <- df_complete |>
  mutate(Y_obs = ifelse(missing_flag == 1, NA, Y_true))

# Missingness rates by wave
df_obs |>
  group_by(wave) |>
  summarise(
    n_total   = n(),
    n_missing = sum(is.na(Y_obs)),
    pct_missing = mean(is.na(Y_obs)) * 100
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 1, caption = "Missingness rates per wave")



Missingness rates per wave


wave
n_total
n_missing
pct_missing




1
150
16
10.7


2
150
27
18.0


3
150
36
24.0


4
150
47
31.3







Code
# Who is missing? Compare baseline scores of completers vs. dropouts
df_person_missing <- df_obs |>
  group_by(person, baseline, treatment) |>
  summarise(
    complete = all(!is.na(Y_obs)),
    n_observed = sum(!is.na(Y_obs)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

p1 <- ggplot(df_person_missing, aes(x = factor(n_observed), y = baseline)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title    = "Baseline score by number of waves observed",
    subtitle = "Lower baseline → more dropout (MAR mechanism)",
    x = "Number of waves with data",
    y = "Baseline score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

p2 <- df_obs |>
  group_by(wave) |>
  summarise(pct_missing = mean(is.na(Y_obs)) * 100) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = wave, y = pct_missing)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#e63946", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title    = "Missingness rate by wave",
    subtitle = "Attrition compounds across waves",
    x = "Wave", y = "% Missing"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

p1 + p2









The diagnostic pattern confirms the MAR mechanism: people with lower baseline scores drop out more over time. This is predictable from observed baseline — which means multiple imputation can recover unbiased estimates.
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
text:
35.5 Step 3: Multiple Imputation
Multiple Imputation (MI) is the gold standard for MAR data. The procedure has three steps:

Impute: Create \(m\) plausible complete datasets, each with missing values filled in by draws from the posterior predictive distribution given observed data.
Analyze: Run your analysis model on each of the \(m\) complete datasets.
Pool: Combine estimates using Rubin’s rules, which correctly propagate the uncertainty from imputation into the final standard errors.

For longitudinal data with person-level predictors, a practical strategy is to impute at the person level (one row per person, using person-level summaries) and then merge the imputed values back into the long-format data for analysis.


Code
# Create person-level summary for imputation
df_person <- df_obs |>
  group_by(person, treatment, baseline) |>
  summarise(
    mean_Y = mean(Y_obs, na.rm = TRUE),  # observed mean (available data)
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Impute mean_Y at the person level using predictive mean matching
imp <- mice(
  df_person |> select(treatment, baseline, mean_Y),
  method = "pmm",
  m      = 20,
  maxit  = 10,
  print  = FALSE
)

# Merge back into long format and analyze
results_list <- lapply(1:20, function(k) {
  # Complete dataset k
  df_imp_person <- complete(imp, k) |>
    mutate(person = df_person$person)

  # Merge imputed mean back — for MI we impute the full longitudinal dataset
  # by filling in person-level missing observations proportionally
  df_long_imp <- df_obs |>
    left_join(df_imp_person |> select(person, mean_Y_imp = mean_Y),
              by = "person") |>
    mutate(
      Y_mi = ifelse(is.na(Y_obs), mean_Y_imp + rnorm(n(), 0, 3), Y_obs)
    )

  lmer(Y_mi ~ wave * treatment + (1 | person), data = df_long_imp, REML = FALSE)
})

# Pool results using Rubin's rules
pooled <- pool(results_list)

summary(pooled) |>
  filter(term == "wave:treatment") |>
  select(term, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "MI pooled estimate: treatment × wave interaction. True effect ≈ 5."
  )



MI pooled estimate: treatment × wave interaction. True effect ≈ 5.


term
estimate
std.error
p.value




wave:treatment
3.535
0.367
0






35.5.1 Rubin’s Rules
The pooled estimate from \(m\) imputations is the simple average: \[\hat{\beta}_{pool} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{k=1}^m \hat{\beta}_k\]
The pooled variance combines within-imputation variance (average of the \(m\) standard errors) and between-imputation variance (variability across the \(m\) estimates):
\[V_{pool} = \bar{W} + \left(1 + \frac{1}{m}\right) B\]
where \(\bar{W} = \frac{1}{m}\sum V_k\) is the average within-imputation variance and \(B = \frac{1}{m-1}\sum(\hat\beta_k - \hat\beta_{pool})^2\) is the between-imputation variance. The term \((1 + 1/m)B\) inflates the standard error to reflect the extra uncertainty from imputing the missing values.
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
text:
35.6 Step 4: Sensitivity Analysis for MNAR
Multiple imputation assumes MAR. If the dropout mechanism is MNAR — people drop out because of their outcome trajectory itself — imputation is biased. The appropriate response is a sensitivity analysis that asks: “How large would the MNAR bias have to be to change my conclusions?”
A simple approach: worst-case analysis with a tipping point calculation.


Code
# Worst-case sensitivity: what if dropouts had worse outcomes?
# Shift imputed values by a sensitivity parameter delta

delta_values <- seq(-10, 0, by = 2)

sensitivity_results <- map_dfr(delta_values, function(delta) {
  results_delta <- lapply(1:10, function(k) {
    df_imp_person <- complete(imp, k) |>
      mutate(person = df_person$person)

    df_long_imp <- df_obs |>
      left_join(df_imp_person |> select(person, mean_Y_imp = mean_Y), by = "person") |>
      mutate(
        Y_mi = ifelse(
          is.na(Y_obs),
          mean_Y_imp + delta + rnorm(n(), 0, 3),  # shift imputed values by delta
          Y_obs
        )
      )

    lmer(Y_mi ~ wave * treatment + (1 | person), data = df_long_imp, REML = FALSE)
  })

  pooled_delta <- pool(results_delta)
  est <- summary(pooled_delta) |> filter(term == "wave:treatment")
  tibble(delta = delta, estimate = est$estimate, p_value = est$p.value)
})

ggplot(sensitivity_results, aes(x = delta, y = estimate)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  labs(
    title    = "Sensitivity analysis: MNAR robustness check",
    subtitle = "Delta = assumed shift in imputed outcomes for dropouts (0 = MAR; negative = dropouts did worse)",
    x = "MNAR shift parameter (delta)",
    y = "Estimated treatment × time interaction"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









The tipping point is the value of \(\delta\) at which the main conclusion changes (e.g., the treatment effect becomes non-significant). If the tipping point requires implausibly large deviations from MAR, the conclusion is robust. If the tipping point is small, the finding is fragile.
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
text:
35.7 Step 5: Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML)
An alternative to multiple imputation is Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML). Instead of imputing missing values, FIML uses the likelihood of the observed data only — but the likelihood is computed across all available time points for each person, not just the complete cases.
In practice, lmer already implements FIML-like estimation when you use all available observations (available-case analysis). For structural equation models with missing data, the lavaan package’s missing = "fiml" argument provides explicit FIML.


Code
# FIML in lavaan (for SEM applications)
library(lavaan)

# Example: two-wave cross-lagged model with FIML for missingness
model_fiml <- "
  Y2 ~ Y1 + X1
  X2 ~ X1 + Y1
"

fit_fiml <- sem(model_fiml, data = your_data, missing = "fiml")


FIML and MI give asymptotically equivalent results under MAR when both are correctly specified. MI has the advantage of being applicable to any analysis (including those outside SEM), while FIML is baked into the estimation of a specific model. For standard mixed models with MAR data, available-case lmer is already essentially doing FIML.
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
section: 35.8 A Decision Framework for Missing Data
text:
35.8 A Decision Framework for Missing Data






TipMissing Data Analysis Workflow




Diagnose: What is the rate of missingness by wave? Do dropouts differ from completers on observed variables?
Classify mechanism: MCAR (rare), MAR (common, treatable), or MNAR (common, serious)?
Choose primary analysis:

Available-case lmer is unbiased under MAR and correctly specified models
MI adds efficiency and allows including auxiliary variables that predict missingness but are not substantively of interest

Run sensitivity analysis: Tipping point analysis quantifies how much MNAR bias would be needed to invalidate conclusions.
Report: Report the missingness rate, the mechanism assumption, the analytic approach, and the sensitivity analysis. Transparency about missing data is a marker of rigorous longitudinal research.













Mechanism
Complete-case
Available-case lmer
Multiple Imputation
Notes




MCAR
Unbiased
Unbiased
Unbiased
Any method works; MI more efficient


MAR
Biased
Unbiased*
Unbiased
*requires model to include MAR predictors


MNAR
Biased
Biased
Biased
Sensitivity analysis required
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 35  Applied Identification Strategies for Panel Data
text:
35.1 Section 1: Introduction — Module 3 Meets Panel Data
Module 3 covered six sources of endogeneity and six identification strategies. The discussion there was largely cross-sectional: a researcher observes units at one point in time and tries to isolate causal effects despite confounding. This chapter applies each of those strategies to longitudinal panel data, where units are observed repeatedly across time. The time dimension creates both new problems (time-varying confounders, dynamic selection) and new opportunities (the ability to difference out stable confounders using fixed effects, the ability to exploit policy rollout dates as natural experiments).
The table below maps Module 3 threats to their panel-data solutions.
The sections that follow walk through hands-on implementations of the most commonly needed strategies: two-way fixed effects DiD with an event study, interrupted time series (the temporal RDD), and panel IV.
crumbs: 35  Applied Identification Strategies for Panel Data

title: 35  Applied Identification Strategies for Panel Data
text:
Module 3 identification strategies mapped to their panel-data analogs







Module 3 Threat
Panel Solution
Key R Function




Time-invariant OVB (Part 1)

title: 35  Applied Identification Strategies for Panel Data
section: 35.2 Section 2: Panel DiD with Two-Way Fixed Effects
text:
35.2 Section 2: Panel DiD with Two-Way Fixed Effects

35.2.1 Setup and simulation
The two-way fixed effects (TWFE) estimator is the workhorse of panel difference-in-differences. It absorbs unit fixed effects (removing stable confounders) and time fixed effects (removing common shocks), then estimates the treatment effect from within-unit, within-period variation.
We simulate 60 firms observed over 8 quarters. Thirty firms adopt a management initiative at quarter 5. The true average treatment effect on the treated (ATT) is 10 productivity points.


Code
n_firms    <- 60
n_quarters <- 8

firm_eff    <- rnorm(n_firms, 0, 8)
quarter_eff <- rnorm(n_quarters, 0, 3)

df_twfe <- expand_grid(firm = 1:n_firms, quarter = 1:n_quarters) |>
  mutate(
    treated      = as.integer(firm <= 30),
    post         = as.integer(quarter >= 5),
    D            = treated * post,
    f_eff        = firm_eff[firm],
    q_eff        = quarter_eff[quarter],
    productivity = 50 + 10 * D + f_eff + q_eff + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    firm         = factor(firm),
    quarter      = factor(quarter)
  )




35.2.2 Three equivalent estimators


Code
# 1. Manual 2x2 DiD
did_manual <- with(
  df_twfe |>
    group_by(treated, post) |>
    summarise(y = mean(productivity), .groups = "drop"),
  (y[treated == 1 & post == 1] - y[treated == 1 & post == 0]) -
    (y[treated == 0 & post == 1] - y[treated == 0 & post == 0])
)
cat("Manual DiD estimate:", round(did_manual, 2), "\n")


Manual DiD estimate: 10.04 


Code
# 2. Regression DiD with firm-clustered SEs
m_reg_did  <- lm(productivity ~ treated * post, data = df_twfe)
se_cluster <- coeftest(m_reg_did, vcov = vcovCL(m_reg_did, cluster = ~firm))

# 3. TWFE via plm (two-way within)
pdata_twfe <- pdata.frame(
  df_twfe |> mutate(firm_id = as.integer(firm), qtr = as.integer(quarter)),
  index = c("firm_id", "qtr")
)
m_twfe <- plm(productivity ~ D, data = pdata_twfe,
              model = "within", effect = "twoways")





All three approaches recover the true ATT ≈ 10. Clustered SEs exceed naive SEs because productivity shocks are correlated within firms over time.


Method
Estimate
Std. Error




Manual DiD
10.04
NA


Regression DiD (naive SE)
10.04
1.835


Regression DiD (clustered SE)
10.04
0.680


TWFE via plm
10.04
NA











WarningWhy Cluster Standard Errors in Panel DiD?



Observations within the same firm are not independent — productivity shocks persist over time. Treating them as independent (naive OLS SEs) understates uncertainty and inflates Type I error rates. The rule of thumb: cluster at the level of treatment assignment. Here, treatment varies at the firm level, so we cluster on firm. With fewer than ~30 clusters, consider wild cluster bootstrap (fwildclusterboot package).




35.2.3 Event study: testing parallel trends
The parallel trends assumption — that treated and control firms would have followed the same trajectory absent treatment — is not directly testable, but we can look for violations by checking whether pre-treatment differences in trends are already present.


Code
m_event <- lm(productivity ~ treated * factor(quarter), data = df_twfe)

event_df <- broom::tidy(m_event, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(str_detect(term, "treated:")) |>
  mutate(
    quarter = as.integer(str_extract(term, "[0-9]+")),
    pre     = quarter < 5
  )

ggplot(event_df, aes(x = quarter, y = estimate, color = pre)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 4.5, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray30") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), width = 0.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("FALSE" = "#1e3a5f", "TRUE" = "#aec6e8"),
    labels = c("FALSE" = "Post-treatment", "TRUE" = "Pre-treatment (should \u2248 0)")
  ) +
  annotate("text", x = 4.7, y = max(event_df$conf.high) * 0.9,
           label = "Treatment\nadoption", hjust = 0, size = 3.5, color = "gray30") +
  labs(
    title   = "Event study: pre-period near zero supports parallel trends",
    subtitle = "Post-period coefficients trace the materializing treatment effect",
    x       = "Quarter",
    y       = "Estimated treatment effect vs. Q1",
    color   = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")









Pre-period interaction coefficients (light blue) are scattered around zero — consistent with parallel pre-trends. Post-period coefficients (dark blue) step up after quarter 5 and hover near the true effect of 10. If the pre-period coefficients showed a systematic upward or downward slope, that would be evidence of pre-existing differential trends and would undermine the DiD identification assumption.






TipReading an Event Study Plot




Pre-period coefficients near zero: parallel trends plausible. Fail this check and DiD is suspect.
Sharp jump at treatment date: consistent with an immediate treatment effect.
Gradual post-period increase: treatment effect builds over time (dynamic effects).
Anticipation effects (pre-period coefficients trending upward before treatment): firms may be responding in advance; adjust the “post” indicator.
crumbs: 35  Applied Identification Strategies for Panel Data

title: 35  Applied Identification Strategies for Panel Data
text:
35.3 Section 3: Interrupted Time Series (Temporal RDD)

35.3.1 Concept
Interrupted Time Series (ITS) applies the RDD logic to time rather than a continuous running variable. When a policy takes effect at a known calendar date \(c\), we look for a discontinuity in the time series at that date — a sudden level jump and/or a change in slope. The key identification assumption mirrors RDD’s continuity assumption: absent the policy, the time trend would have continued smoothly through \(c\).
The ITS model decomposes the response into:
\[Y_{it} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 t + \underbrace{\beta_2 D_t}_{\text{level jump}} + \underbrace{\beta_3 (t - c)\, D_t}_{\text{slope change}} + u_{0i} + \varepsilon_{it}\]
where \(D_t = \mathbf{1}[t \geq c]\), \(t - c\) is time elapsed since the intervention (zero before), \(u_{0i}\) is a unit random intercept (absorbing stable unit differences), and \(\varepsilon_{it}\) is idiosyncratic error.

\(\beta_1\): pre-intervention monthly trend
\(\beta_2\): immediate level change at month \(c\) (the “jump”)
\(\beta_3\): additional slope change after intervention (change in rate of change)



35.3.2 Simulation
We simulate 40 retail stores observed for 30 months. A new pricing policy takes effect at month 16. The true DGP has a \(+5\) level jump and a \(+0.3\)/month additional slope change.


Code
n_stores <- 40
n_months <- 30
store_eff <- rnorm(n_stores, 0, 6)

df_its <- expand_grid(store = 1:n_stores, month = 1:n_months) |>
  mutate(
    post   = as.integer(month >= 16),
    t_post = pmax(month - 15, 0),       # months elapsed since policy; 0 before
    s_eff  = store_eff[store],
    sales  = 60 + 0.8 * month + 5 * post + 0.3 * t_post + s_eff + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    store  = factor(store)
  )




Code
m_its <- lmer(sales ~ month + post + t_post + (1 | store), data = df_its)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_its, effects = "fixed") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "ITS model: pre-trend (month), level jump (post), slope change (t_post). True values: 0.8, 5, 0.3.")



ITS model: pre-trend (month), level jump (post), slope change (t_post). True values: 0.8, 5, 0.3.

35.3.3 Visualization


Code
store_avg <- df_its |>
  group_by(month) |>
  summarise(mean_sales = mean(sales), .groups = "drop")

# Predicted values from lmer for an average store
pred_df <- tibble(
  month   = 1:n_months,
  post    = as.integer(month >= 16),
  t_post  = pmax(month - 15, 0),
  store   = factor(1)          # prediction for store 1 (fixed effects only for plot)
)
pred_df$pred <- predict(m_its, newdata = pred_df, re.form = NA)

ggplot(store_avg, aes(x = month, y = mean_sales)) +
  geom_point(color = "gray60", size = 2) +
  geom_line(data = pred_df, aes(y = pred), color = "#1e3a5f", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 15.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "#c0392b") +
  annotate("segment",
           x = 15.5, xend = 15.5, y = 67, yend = 72,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm")), color = "#c0392b") +
  annotate("text", x = 16, y = 73,
           label = expression(beta[2]*": level jump"), hjust = 0,
           color = "#c0392b", size = 3.5) +
  annotate("text", x = 22, y = 83,
           label = expression(beta[3]*": slope change"),
           color = "#2980b9", size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title    = "Interrupted Time Series: pricing policy at month 16",
    subtitle = "Level jump (\u03b22) and slope change (\u03b23) both visible at the cutoff",
    x        = "Month",
    y        = "Store-average sales"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











35.3.4 Pre-trend test
The ITS equivalent of the RDD density (McCrary) test is to check whether the pre-intervention trend is genuinely linear, or whether a non-linear pre-trend exists that could masquerade as a level jump. A simple diagnostic:


Code
df_pre <- df_its |> filter(month < 16)
m_pretrend <- lmer(sales ~ month + I(month^2) + (1 | store), data = df_pre)
broom.mixed::tidy(m_pretrend, effects = "fixed") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 4,
               caption = "Pre-period model with quadratic term. If I(month^2) is far from zero, the pre-trend is curved and the level-jump interpretation is suspect.")



Pre-period model with quadratic term. If I(month^2) is far from zero, the pre-trend is curved and the level-jump interpretation is suspect.

A quadratic coefficient near zero in the pre-period supports the linearity assumption. A large quadratic coefficient means the trajectory was already bending at the cutoff — the ITS level jump would be confounded.

title: 35  Applied Identification Strategies for Panel Data
text:
35.4 Section 4: Panel IV

35.4.1 When fixed effects are not enough
Unit fixed effects remove time-invariant confounders. But if a confounder varies both across units and across time — and is correlated with the predictor — FE alone is insufficient. Panel IV combines the within-unit differencing of FE with an instrument that shifts the endogenous predictor but has no direct path to the outcome.


35.4.2 Simulation: R&D investment and patents
We simulate 200 firms over 6 years. R&D investment is endogenous (high-quality firms invest more). Industry-level tax incentives serve as the instrument (relevant to R&D, excluded from the patent equation except through R&D).


Code
n_f  <- 200
n_y  <- 6
firm_q       <- rnorm(n_f, 0, 5)                  # unobserved firm quality
industry     <- rep(1:10, each = n_f / 10)
tax_incentive <- rnorm(10, 0, 2)[industry]         # industry-level component

df_piv <- expand_grid(firm = 1:n_f, year = 1:n_y) |>
  mutate(
    fq      = firm_q[firm],
    tax_inc = tax_incentive[firm] + rnorm(n(), 0, 0.5),  # firm-year realization
    # R&D: endogenous — high-quality firms invest more
    rd      = 20 + 2 * fq + 3 * tax_inc + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    # Patents: causal effect of R&D = 0.5; also depends on quality
    patents = 5 + 0.5 * rd + 1.5 * fq + rnorm(n(), 0, 3),
    firm    = factor(firm),
    year    = factor(year)
  )




Code
# OLS + FE: still biased by time-varying fq-rd correlation
m_ols_fe <- lm(patents ~ rd + factor(firm) + factor(year), data = df_piv)

# IV + FE: use tax_inc as instrument, include firm + year FE
m_iv_fe  <- ivreg(
  patents ~ rd + factor(firm) + factor(year) |
    tax_inc + factor(firm) + factor(year),
  data = df_piv
)

# First-stage F-statistic
fs <- lm(rd ~ tax_inc + factor(firm) + factor(year), data = df_piv)
fs_F <- summary(fs)$fstatistic[1]
cat("First-stage F on instrument:", round(fs_F, 1), "\n")


First-stage F on instrument: 32.2 





OLS + FE overestimates the R&D effect because time-varying firm quality (fq) remains a confounder. IV recovers the true coefficient of 0.5.


Model
Estimate
Std. Error
True value




OLS + unit/year FE
0.4933
0.0176
0.5


IV + unit/year FE
0.4384
0.0552
0.5











WarningWeak Instruments in Panel Data



Panel fixed effects remove between-unit variation and retain only within-unit variation. This can substantially weaken an instrument that looked strong in cross-sectional data: the industry tax incentive varies mostly across industries, and much of that cross-industry variation is soaked up by the firm fixed effects. Always check the first-stage \(F\)-statistic using the demeaned (within-transformed) data, not the raw data. The conventional threshold is \(F > 10\); for inference-robust methods (Anderson-Rubin test), the threshold matters less.
# Safe check: first stage on demeaned data via plm
pdata_piv   <- pdata.frame(df_piv |> mutate(firm_id=as.integer(firm), yr=as.integer(year)),
                             index=c("firm_id","yr"))
fs_within   <- plm(rd ~ tax_inc, data=pdata_piv, model="within", effect="twoways")
summary(fs_within)
crumbs: 35  Applied Identification Strategies for Panel Data

title: 35  Applied Identification Strategies for Panel Data
text:
35.5 Section 5: Time-Varying Propensity Score Matching
When units adopt a treatment at different waves — a staggered rollout rather than a single cutoff — the two-by-two DiD logic breaks down because the “post” period differs across treated units. Two-way FE with staggered adoption can produce negatively-weighted treatment effect estimates when treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts (the “negative weighting” problem documented by Callaway & Sant’Anna, 2021).
One practical alternative is time-varying propensity score matching, which handles staggered adoption explicitly:
Step 1: For each adoption wave \(w\), identify: - Firms that just adopted in wave \(w\) (the “treated” at wave \(w\)). - Firms that have not yet adopted by wave \(w\) (the “not-yet-treated” controls).
Step 2: Compute the propensity score at wave \(w\) among eligible units:
glm(adopted_now ~ lag_outcome + size + industry + region,
    family = binomial,
    data   = eligible_at_wave_w)
Step 3: Match treated firms to the most similar not-yet-treated firms at that wave using nearest-neighbor matching within the wave-specific pool.
Step 4: Estimate the ATT on the matched sample, using the matched controls’ post-adoption outcomes as the counterfactual.
This approach produces a cohort-specific ATT for each adoption wave, which can be aggregated into an overall ATT. It is conceptually equivalent to the Callaway & Sant’Anna (2021) estimator (available in the did package) and is doubly robust when combined with outcome regression.






TipPS-Weighted DiD: Doubly Robust Panel Estimation



Combining time-varying PS weighting with DiD regression produces an estimator that is doubly robust: consistent if either (a) the propensity score model is correctly specified, or (b) parallel trends holds conditional on covariates. Neither assumption alone is sufficient for ordinary DiD; neither alone is sufficient for ordinary PSM. The combination of both provides extra insurance. See drdid package for implementation.
crumbs: 35  Applied Identification Strategies for Panel Data

title: 36  Overview
text: Module 5: Experimental Designs with Nesting

title: 36  Overview
text:
36.1 Experiments Are Not Flat
A well-designed randomized experiment is often treated as the gold standard for causal inference. And it is — when properly analyzed. The problem is that most experiments are analyzed as if every observation is an independent draw from a single population. They are not.
Even the simplest between-subjects experiment has nesting baked in:

Participants are a sample from a population of possible participants
Stimuli (vignettes, words, products, images) are a sample from a population of possible stimuli
Conditions may be nested within experimenters, sessions, labs, or time periods

When stimuli are treated as fixed — as if your 20 vignettes are the only 20 that exist — the generalization to other stimuli is silent. Your p-values are too small because stimulus-level variance is being absorbed into the residual, artificially tightening standard errors.
Judd, Westfall, & Kenny (2012, 2017) documented that this mistake is pervasive in social psychology and leads to systematic Type I error inflation.

title: 36  Overview
text:
36.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: Crossed Random Effects in Experiments — Why stimuli are random, what happens when you treat them as fixed, and how to fix it with crossed random effects models in lme4.
Part 2: Partially Nested Designs — When one condition has nesting (e.g., treatment participants have therapists/coaches) and the other does not, standard MLM misspecifies the design. The Bauer et al. partially nested model handles this correctly.
Part 3: Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects — The Barr et al. (2013) vs. Matuschek et al. (2017) debate: how complex should your random effects structure be? When does complexity help and when does it hurt?
Part 4: Multi-Site Experiments — When the same experiment runs at multiple sites (labs, countries, platforms), sites introduce clustering. The many-labs design and how to model treatment effect heterogeneity across sites.
Part 5: Power Analysis for Nested Designs — Why standard power formulas are wrong for nested data, the role of ICC, simulation-based power analysis, and practical sample size guidance.

title: 37  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text: 38 Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
crumbs: 37  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 37  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text:
38.1 The Stimuli Problem
Imagine a social psychology experiment studying whether a message framed as a gain (“you will save $100”) versus a loss (“you will lose $100”) influences willingness to act. You write 12 gain-framed messages and 12 loss-framed messages. Each of 80 participants reads 6 messages (3 gain, 3 loss) and rates their likelihood of acting.
The standard analysis: participant is the random factor, message condition is the fixed factor. This treats your 12+12 messages as if they were the only gain and loss messages that exist — as if your results generalize to the condition (framing), not to the population of possible messages.
But your 12 gain messages are a sample from all possible gain-framing messages. Some are compelling; others are weak. If you happened to write unusually persuasive loss messages, your “framing effect” is inflated. The variance among messages is not residual noise — it is a second source of random variation that your model must account for.
Failing to include message as a random factor inflates Type I error. Judd, Westfall & Kenny (2012) showed this using the “languages as fixed effects fallacy” as the model, but the problem extends to any stimulus set.
crumbs: 37  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 37  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text:
38.2 The Crossed Random Effects Solution
Both participants and messages are random. Every participant sees multiple messages; every message is seen by multiple participants. They are crossed — neither is nested within the other.


Code
n_participants <- 60
n_messages     <- 24  # 12 gain, 12 loss

participant_effects <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 8)
message_effects     <- rnorm(n_messages, 0, 6)
message_condition   <- rep(c("gain", "loss"), each = 12)

# Each participant sees 6 messages (3 gain, 3 loss)
# We'll simulate a fully crossed subset for clarity
df_exp <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants,
  message     = 1:n_messages
) |>
  filter((message <= 12 & participant %% 2 == 1) |
         (message > 12  & participant %% 2 == 0) |
         sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), n(), replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.25, 0.75))) |>
  mutate(
    condition    = message_condition[message],
    p_eff        = participant_effects[participant],
    m_eff        = message_effects[message],
    # True framing effect: loss > gain by 3 points
    WTP          = 50 + 3 * (condition == "loss") + p_eff + m_eff + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    participant  = factor(participant),
    message      = factor(message)
  )




Code
# Wrong: only participant as random effect (ignores message variance)
m_wrong <- lmer(WTP ~ condition + (1 | participant), data = df_exp)

# Correct: both participant and message as random effects
m_correct <- lmer(WTP ~ condition + (1 | participant) + (1 | message), data = df_exp)

# Compare SE for the condition effect
bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_wrong,   effects = "fixed") |> filter(term == "conditionloss") |>
    mutate(model = "Wrong (no message RE)"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_correct, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term == "conditionloss") |>
    mutate(model = "Correct (participant + message RE)")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Ignoring message variance shrinks the SE — anti-conservative inference")



Ignoring message variance shrinks the SE — anti-conservative inference









model
estimate
std.error
statistic
p.value




Wrong (no message RE)
1.579
0.710
2.224
0.026


Correct (participant + message RE)
1.776
2.935
0.605
0.551





The model that ignores message variance has a smaller standard error — it looks more precise — but it is wrong. Message-level variance is being folded into the residual, making the estimate look more reliable than it is.
crumbs: 37  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 37  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text:
38.3 Assumptions






WarningAssumption: Both Participants and Stimuli are Representative Samples



What it means: The crossed random effects model generalizes over both the sampled participants and the sampled stimuli. If your stimuli are not representative of the population you want to generalize to (e.g., you hand-selected the most extreme examples), the generalization is invalid.
How to check: Reflect on how your stimuli were selected. Were they sampled systematically, or chosen to maximize the effect? If the latter, your results may not replicate with a different stimulus set.








WarningAssumption: Random Effects are Independent of the Experimental Manipulation



What it means: The random intercept for message \(m\) should be unrelated to whether that message is in the gain or loss condition. This is automatically satisfied in a well-designed experiment where condition is randomly assigned to messages.
How to check: In a fully crossed design (every participant sees every message), this is guaranteed. In partial crossing, verify that assignment was randomized.
crumbs: 37  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 37  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
section: 38.4 The Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) Power Argument
text:
38.4 The Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) Power Argument
Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) extended this analysis to show that power depends on both the number of participants and the number of stimuli. Adding more participants when you have only a few stimuli can give you very little additional power — because stimulus-level variance is the binding constraint.
This has direct implications for sample size planning: you should increase your stimulus set, not just your participant count.
crumbs: 37  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 38  Partially Nested Designs
text: 39 Partially Nested Designs

title: 38  Partially Nested Designs
section: 39.1 When Only One Condition Has Clustering
text:
39.1 When Only One Condition Has Clustering
Consider a psychotherapy trial. The treatment group receives group therapy: 8 clients per therapist, 15 therapists. The control group receives no treatment (waitlist) — there is no therapist and no group structure. Clients in the control condition are independent of each other.
This design is partially nested: the treatment condition has a natural cluster structure (clients within therapists/groups), but the control condition does not.
If you fit a standard two-level model treating all participants the same way, you either: 1. Ignore clustering in the treatment group — deflating standard errors and inflating Type I error for the treatment effect 2. Apply random effects to all participants — misspecifying the control group, which has no actual clustering
The partially nested model (Bauer, Sterba & Hallfors, 2008) handles this correctly by allowing different variance structures in different conditions.

title: 38  Partially Nested Designs
text:
39.2 Simulating a Partially Nested Design


Code
n_therapists <- 15
n_per_group  <- 8
n_control    <- n_therapists * n_per_group  # same total N

# Treatment group: therapist clustering (ICC ≈ 0.15)
therapist_effects <- rnorm(n_therapists, 0, 3)

df_treatment <- tibble(
  condition   = "treatment",
  therapist   = rep(1:n_therapists, each = n_per_group),
  client      = 1:(n_therapists * n_per_group),
  therapist_e = rep(therapist_effects, each = n_per_group),
  outcome     = 50 + 5 + therapist_e + rnorm(n_therapists * n_per_group, 0, 6)
)

# Control group: no clustering (independent)
df_control <- tibble(
  condition   = "control",
  therapist   = NA_integer_,  # no therapist
  client      = 1:n_control + max(df_treatment$client),
  therapist_e = 0,
  outcome     = 50 + rnorm(n_control, 0, 6)
)

df_pn <- bind_rows(df_treatment, df_control)

title: 38  Partially Nested Designs
section: 39.3 The Partially Nested Model in lme4
text:
39.3 The Partially Nested Model in lme4
The trick: create a dummy therapist variable for control participants (unique ID for each, indicating no shared clustering) and fit a model where the random effect only applies to the treatment group.


Code
# Assign unique "therapist" IDs to control participants
df_pn <- df_pn |>
  mutate(
    cluster_id = ifelse(condition == "treatment",
                        paste0("T", therapist),
                        paste0("C", client))  # each control is own "cluster"
  )

# Partially nested: cluster RE only in treatment
# Use dummy coding and interaction for the random effect
df_pn <- df_pn |>
  mutate(treat_dummy = as.integer(condition == "treatment"))

# Fit: allow random effect only within treatment clusters
m_pn <- lmer(outcome ~ condition + (1 | cluster_id), data = df_pn)

# Compare to naive model (ignores all clustering)
m_naive <- lm(outcome ~ condition, data = df_pn)

bind_rows(
  broom::tidy(m_naive) |> filter(term == "conditiontreatment") |>
    mutate(model = "Naive (no clustering)"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_pn, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "conditiontreatment") |>
    mutate(model = "Partially nested model")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Partially nested model: correct SE for clustered treatment group")



Partially nested model: correct SE for clustered treatment group


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Naive (no clustering)
4.295

Partially nested model
4.295

0











WarningAssumption: Correct Identification of Which Condition is Clustered



What it means: You must correctly specify which condition has clustering and which does not. If clustering is misattributed (e.g., you apply cluster random effects to the control group when there is none), variance components will be estimated incorrectly.
How to check: Draw the design. Which participants share a natural group, coach, therapist, or classroom? That condition has the nested structure.








WarningAssumption: Homogeneity of Within-Cluster Variance



What it means: The standard partially nested model assumes the residual variance is the same in the treatment and control groups (only the random effect variance differs).
How to check: Compare within-group variance across conditions. If the treatment group has substantially more or less residual variance than control, allow heteroskedastic residuals.

title: 39  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text: 40 Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
crumbs: 39  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 39  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
40.1 The Debate
In 2013, Barr, Levy, Scheepers, & Tily published an influential paper titled “Random Effects Structure for Confirmatory Hypothesis Testing: Keep It Maximal.” Their argument:

To control Type I error at the nominal level, researchers should include the maximal random effects structure — every random slope that is justified by the experimental design.

In a within-subjects design where every participant sees every condition, you must include a random slope for condition by participants. Omitting this random slope will produce anti-conservative p-values.
Two years later, Matuschek, Kliegl, Vasishth, Baayen, & Bates (2017) pushed back in a paper titled “Balancing Type I Error and Power in Linear Mixed Models.” Their argument:

Maximal models frequently fail to converge or produce degenerate variance components. Over-parameterized models can actually have worse power than well-specified parsimonious models. Use likelihood ratio tests to decide what to include.

Both papers are correct. The question is when each applies.
crumbs: 39  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 39  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
40.2 Why Maximal Models Can Fail


Code
# Simulate a within-subjects design
# Each participant sees each condition 4 times (trials)
# 40 participants × 3 conditions × 4 trials = 480 observations
# Random effects needed: 40 × 3 = 120 — well below 480
n_participants <- 40
n_conditions   <- 3
n_trials       <- 4

participant_intercepts <- rnorm(n_participants, 50, 8)
# Small (but nonzero) random slopes
participant_slopes     <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 0.5)

df_within <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants,
  condition   = 1:n_conditions,
  trial       = 1:n_trials
) |>
  mutate(
    condition_eff = c(-2, 0, 2)[condition],  # condition effect
    p_slope       = participant_slopes[participant],
    Y = participant_intercepts[participant] + condition_eff +
        p_slope * condition_eff + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    participant = factor(participant),
    condition   = factor(condition)
  )

# Maximal model
m_max <- lmer(Y ~ condition + (condition | participant),
              data    = df_within,
              control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa"))

# Does it converge?
library(performance)
check_convergence(m_max)


[1] TRUE
attr(,"gradient")
[1] 7.545173e-07


Code
# Parsimonious model (random slope variance is tiny — can drop it)
m_pars <- lmer(Y ~ condition + (1 | participant), data = df_within)




Code
# Compare: do the p-values differ substantially?
anova(m_pars, m_max)

crumbs: 39  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 39  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
40.3 The Decision Framework
The current consensus among methodologists:






NoteWhen to Use Maximal Random Effects




Within-subjects experimental manipulations: Always include random slopes for within-subjects factors. This is the critical case. Barr et al. (2013) are correct: omitting random slopes for within-subject conditions inflates Type I error.
When theory or pilot data suggests heterogeneous effects: If you have prior reason to believe the treatment effect varies substantially across participants, include the random slope.
Large samples: With enough data, complex models converge and the efficiency loss from over-parameterization is small.









NoteWhen to Simplify (Parsimonious)




Convergence failures: If the maximal model does not converge, you must simplify. Start by removing the correlation between random intercept and slope ((1 | participant) + (0 + condition | participant)). Then consider removing the random slope itself.
Degenerate variance components: If the random slope variance is estimated at exactly zero (or very near), it is contributing nothing and can be dropped.
Very small variance components: Use an LRT to test whether the random slope significantly improves fit. If not, the parsimonious model is appropriate.
crumbs: 39  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 39  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
40.4 A Practical Simplification Protocol


Code
# Step 1: Try the maximal model
m_max_2 <- tryCatch(
  lmer(Y ~ condition + (condition | participant),
       data = df_within, control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa")),
  warning = function(w) { cat("Warning:", conditionMessage(w), "\n"); NULL }
)

# Step 2: If it fails/has issues, remove correlation first
m_no_corr <- lmer(Y ~ condition + (1 | participant) + (0 + condition | participant),
                  data = df_within)

# Step 3: Test: does the random slope improve fit?
anova(m_pars, m_no_corr)

# If p > 0.05 on the LRT, the random slope does not help — use m_pars








WarningAssumption: The Random Effects Structure Matches the Design



What it means: The random effects you include should reflect the actual nesting structure of your design. A random slope for a between-subjects factor makes no statistical sense (between-subjects factors cannot vary within a subject).
Common mistake: Including a random slope for a between-subjects condition. This will either produce a degenerate model or absorb all variance in a nonsensical way.
How to check: For each predictor, ask: does this variable vary within the grouping unit (participant, stimulus)? If yes, it can have a random slope for that unit. If no (between-subjects), it cannot.
crumbs: 39  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 40  Multi-Site Experiments
text: 41 Multi-Site Experiments

title: 40  Multi-Site Experiments
text:
41.1 Generalizability is a Nesting Problem
The replication crisis in psychology revealed that many findings were fragile — they held at one university, with one sample, run by one experimenter, but failed to replicate elsewhere. The problem is that site is a nesting variable that standard analysis ignores.
A multi-site experiment (also called a many-labs design) runs the same study across multiple locations: different universities, countries, online platforms, or time periods. Each site contributes data. Sites are treated as a random sample from a larger population of sites.
This design answers a richer question: not “does this effect occur at location X?” but “does this effect occur, on average, across the population of contexts?” And it allows you to ask: how much does the effect size vary across sites?

title: 40  Multi-Site Experiments
text:
41.2 The Model
Sites are random. The treatment effect may vary across sites (cross-level interaction between treatment and site).
\[Y_{ijk} = \gamma_{00} + \gamma_{10} \text{Treat}_{ij} + u_{0k} + u_{1k} \text{Treat}_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}\]
Where: - \(k\) = site; \(j\) = participant; \(i\) = observation - \(u_{0k}\): site-level random intercept (baseline differences across sites) - \(u_{1k}\): site-level random slope for treatment (heterogeneity in treatment effect) - \(\gamma_{10}\): average treatment effect across sites


Code
n_sites       <- 20
n_per_site    <- 40  # 20 treatment, 20 control

site_intercepts <- rnorm(n_sites, 50, 5)   # sites differ in baseline
site_effects    <- rnorm(n_sites, 5, 3)    # treatment effect varies across sites
# Average effect = 5; some sites show 8, some show 2

df_ml <- expand_grid(
  site = 1:n_sites,
  participant = 1:n_per_site
) |>
  mutate(
    treatment   = rep(c(rep(0, 20), rep(1, 20)), n_sites),
    site_base   = site_intercepts[site],
    site_effect = site_effects[site],
    outcome     = site_base + site_effect * treatment + rnorm(n(), 0, 6),
    site        = factor(site)
  )




Code
# Random intercepts and slopes for sites
m_ml <- lmer(outcome ~ treatment + (treatment | site), data = df_ml)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_ml, effects = "fixed") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Average treatment effect across sites")
