Code
# Forest plot: site-specific effect estimates
site_estimates <- coef(m_ml)$site |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  rownames_to_column("site") |>
  rename(intercept = `(Intercept)`, effect = treatment) |>
  arrange(effect)

ggplot(site_estimates, aes(x = reorder(site, effect), y = effect)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = fixef(m_ml)["treatment"],
             color = "#e63946", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Site-specific treatment effect estimates (forest plot)",
    subtitle = "Red line = average effect; points = individual site estimates",
    x        = "Site",
    y        = "Treatment Effect"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: 40  Multi-Site Experiments
text:
41.3 The ICC for Treatment Effects
The random slope for treatment tells you how much the effect varies across sites. The I² statistic (from meta-analysis) provides a related measure: what proportion of the total variation in effect size estimates is due to genuine heterogeneity vs. sampling error?


Code
# Variance of the treatment effect across sites
var_treatment_effect <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(m_ml))$vcov[2]  # slope variance
cat("Standard deviation of treatment effect across sites:",
    round(sqrt(var_treatment_effect), 2), "\n")


Standard deviation of treatment effect across sites: 2.7 


Code
cat("Average effect:", round(fixef(m_ml)["treatment"], 2), "\n")


Average effect: 5.04 


Code
cat("Effect ranges approximately from",
    round(fixef(m_ml)["treatment"] - 2*sqrt(var_treatment_effect), 1),
    "to",
    round(fixef(m_ml)["treatment"] + 2*sqrt(var_treatment_effect), 1),
    "across sites\n")


Effect ranges approximately from -0.4 to 10.4 across sites








WarningAssumption: Sites are a Representative Sample



What it means: The multi-site model generalizes to the population of sites from which your sites were drawn. If your 20 labs are all top-10 research universities, the results generalize to that population — not to community colleges, online platforms, or international contexts.
How to check: Describe your site-selection process. Were sites systematically sampled? Opportunistically recruited? The inference should match the selection.








WarningAssumption: Treatment Procedure is Comparable Across Sites



What it means: The “treatment” must be operationally equivalent across sites. If different labs implemented the procedure differently — different materials, different timing, different experimenter instructions — site-to-site variability reflects procedure differences, not true effect heterogeneity.
How to check: Pre-specify and enforce standardized protocols. Measure implementation fidelity. Include implementation fidelity as a moderator.

title: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text: 42 Power Analysis for Nested Designs
crumbs: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
section: 42.1 Why Standard Power Formulas Are Wrong
text:
42.1 Why Standard Power Formulas Are Wrong
Standard power formulas (Cohen’s power tables, G*Power) assume independent observations. They compute: given my expected effect size and desired power, how many participants do I need?
In nested data, this question is incomplete. You also need to know: - How many clusters? (classrooms, firms, sites, therapists) - How many observations per cluster? - What is the ICC? (how similar are observations within clusters)
The ICC and cluster size together determine the design effect (DEFF), which shrinks your effective sample size:
\[N_{effective} = \frac{N}{1 + (n_{cluster} - 1) \times \text{ICC}}\]
Ignoring this leads to severely underpowered studies.
crumbs: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text:
42.2 The ICC-Power Relationship


Code
# Power as a function of ICC, for a fixed N = 300 in clusters of 15
compute_power <- function(icc, n_total = 300, cluster_size = 15, effect = 0.3, alpha = 0.05) {
  deff    <- 1 + (cluster_size - 1) * icc
  n_eff   <- n_total / deff
  # Two-sample t-test power with effective N
  power.t.test(n = n_eff / 2, delta = effect, sd = 1, sig.level = alpha)$power
}

icc_vals <- seq(0, 0.5, by = 0.01)
power_vals <- sapply(icc_vals, compute_power)

tibble(ICC = icc_vals, Power = power_vals) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = ICC, y = Power)) +
  geom_line(color = "#1e3a5f", linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.35, y = 0.82, label = "80% power threshold",
           color = "#e63946", size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title    = "Power declines as ICC increases (N = 300, cluster size = 15)",
    subtitle = "Even modest ICC substantially reduces effective power",
    x        = "ICC",
    y        = "Statistical Power"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









At ICC = 0, you have the expected power for 300 independent observations. At ICC = 0.2, power drops below 80% — you would need more observations to compensate.
crumbs: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text:
42.3 Simulation-Based Power Analysis
For complex nested designs, analytic formulas do not always exist. The gold standard is simulation-based power analysis: generate data from the assumed DGP, fit the model, check whether the effect is significant, repeat 1000 times.


Code
simulate_power <- function(n_clusters, n_per_cluster, icc,
                            true_effect = 0.3, n_sims = 500) {
  sigma_between <- sqrt(icc)
  sigma_within  <- sqrt(1 - icc)

  p_values <- replicate(n_sims, {
    # Simulate two groups of clusters
    n_c <- ceiling(n_clusters / 2)
    cluster_effects <- rnorm(n_clusters, 0, sigma_between)

    df <- tibble(
      cluster = rep(1:n_clusters, each = n_per_cluster),
      group   = ifelse(cluster <= n_c, 0, 1)
    ) |>
      mutate(
        Y = true_effect * group + cluster_effects[cluster] +
            rnorm(n(), 0, sigma_within)
      )

    tryCatch({
      m <- lmer(Y ~ group + (1 | cluster), data = df, REML = FALSE)
      coef(summary(m))["group", "Pr(>|t|)"]
    }, error = function(e) NA_real_,
       warning = function(w) {
         m <- lmer(Y ~ group + (1 | cluster), data = df, REML = FALSE)
         tryCatch(coef(summary(m))["group", "Pr(>|t|)"],
                  error = function(e) NA_real_)
       })
  })
  p_values <- p_values[!is.na(p_values)]

  mean(p_values < 0.05)
}

# Power across different designs
designs <- expand_grid(
  n_clusters      = c(10, 20, 30, 40),
  n_per_cluster   = c(10, 20),
  icc             = c(0.05, 0.15)
) |>
  mutate(
    power = pmap_dbl(
      list(n_clusters, n_per_cluster, icc),
      ~simulate_power(..1, ..2, ..3, n_sims = 200)  # reduce for speed
    ),
    total_N = n_clusters * n_per_cluster,
    design  = paste0("ICC=", icc, ", n/cluster=", n_per_cluster)
  )

ggplot(designs, aes(x = n_clusters, y = power, color = design, linetype = design)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray50") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title    = "Power by number of clusters (simulated)",
    subtitle = "Effect size d = 0.3; n_sims = 200 per design",
    x        = "Number of Clusters",
    y        = "Power",
    color    = "Design", linetype = "Design"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
crumbs: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
section: 42.4 The simr Package for Complex Designs
text:
42.4 The simr Package for Complex Designs
For multilevel models with random slopes, crossed random effects, or other complex structures, the simr package provides a convenient simulation-based power analysis directly from a fitted model:


Code
library(simr)

# Fit a model with your expected parameters
m_pilot <- lmer(Y ~ group + (1 | cluster),
                data = your_pilot_data)

# Change the effect size to your expected value
fixef(m_pilot)["group"] <- 0.3

# Simulate power for different sample sizes
power_curve <- powerCurve(m_pilot,
                           along = "cluster",   # vary number of clusters
                           nsim  = 200)
plot(power_curve)
crumbs: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text:
42.5 Key Takeaways for Power Planning






NoteRules of Thumb




More clusters > more per cluster when ICC is high. If ICC = 0.20, doubling the number of clusters gains much more power than doubling the size of each cluster.
The ICC threshold: If ICC > 0.10 and cluster size > 10, standard power formulas underestimate required N by 50–100%.
For crossed designs (participants × stimuli): Power depends on both the number of participants and the number of stimuli. Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) showed that many published studies are severely underpowered on the stimulus side.
Collect pilot ICC estimates: Run a small pilot study and compute the ICC before committing to a full design.
crumbs: 41  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 42  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
text: 43 Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
crumbs: 42  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments

title: 42  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
section: 43.1 The Problem: When Experimental Outcomes Aren’t Continuous
text:
43.1 The Problem: When Experimental Outcomes Aren’t Continuous
The experimental designs covered in this module — between-subjects, within-subjects, and mixed factorial — all assumed a continuous, roughly normally distributed outcome. But many experimental outcomes in psychology, marketing, and behavioral science are not continuous:

Behavioral choices: Did the participant donate? Choose the prosocial option? Click the ad?
Count behaviors: How many times did the participant help a confederate? How many words did they produce? How many items did they correctly recall?
Errors and successes: How many trials did the participant complete correctly?

Fitting a linear mixed model to a binary or count outcome is not just inefficient — it misspecifies the DGP. Predicted probabilities can exceed 1 or fall below 0. Residuals are structured (they depend on the mean), not random. Parameter estimates are biased.
Generalized Linear Mixed Models (GLMMs) extend the experimental mixed model framework to handle these outcome types correctly. The nesting structure (observations within participants, participants within conditions or groups) is handled identically to the continuous case. The difference is in the link function and distributional family.
crumbs: 42  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments

title: 42  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
text:
43.2 Part 1: Binary Outcomes in Experiments

43.2.1 The Logistic Mixed Model for Experiments
For a binary outcome \(Y_{ij} \in \{0,1\}\) at trial \(i\) for participant \(j\), in a two-condition within-subjects design:
\[\text{logit}\bigl(P(Y_{ij} = 1)\bigr) = \underbrace{\beta_0}_{\substack{\text{average log-odds} \\ \text{at baseline}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{person's tendency} \\ \text{to say yes}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1 \cdot \text{condition}_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{condition effect} \\ \text{on log-odds}}}\]
The random intercept \(u_{0j}\) captures stable individual differences in response tendency — some people are more likely to help, donate, or choose the prosocial option regardless of the manipulation. Failing to include the random intercept would ignore the clustering from repeated measurements, deflate standard errors, and produce overconfident inferences.


43.2.2 Example: Framing Effects on Donation Decisions
We simulate a within-subjects framing experiment. 80 participants each evaluate 16 donation appeals — half framed in gain terms (“saves 200 lives”), half in loss terms (“200 people will die”). The outcome is binary: did they decide to donate (1) or not (0)?
The true donation effect of loss framing is an odds ratio of 1.8 — loss-framed appeals have 80% higher odds of donation, on average. People also differ substantially in their baseline donation tendency (captured by the random intercept).


Code
n_participants <- 80
n_trials_each  <- 8  # 8 gain-framed + 8 loss-framed trials per participant

# Person-level random intercepts (log-odds scale)
u0_person <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 1.2)  # substantial individual differences

df_donate <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants,
  framing     = c("gain", "loss"),
  trial       = 1:n_trials_each
) |>
  mutate(
    u0       = u0_person[participant],
    framing_dummy = as.integer(framing == "loss"),
    log_odds = -0.5 + u0 + 0.59 * framing_dummy,  # log(1.8) ≈ 0.59
    prob     = plogis(log_odds),
    donated  = rbinom(n(), 1, prob),
    participant = factor(participant),
    framing  = factor(framing, levels = c("gain", "loss"))
  )

# Observed donation rates by condition
df_donate |>
  group_by(framing) |>
  summarise(
    n_trials      = n(),
    donation_rate = mean(donated),
    .groups       = "drop"
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Observed donation rates by framing condition")



Observed donation rates by framing condition


framing
n_trials
donation_rate




gain
640
0.384


loss
640
0.506







43.2.3 Fitting and Interpreting the Model


Code
m_donate <- glmer(
  donated ~ framing + (1 | participant),
  data   = df_donate,
  family = binomial
)

# Results as odds ratios
broom.mixed::tidy(m_donate, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Framing effect as odds ratio. True OR = 1.8; loss framing increases donation odds."
  )



Framing effect as odds ratio. True OR = 1.8; loss framing increases donation odds.


term
estimate
conf.low
conf.high
p.value




framingloss
1.872
1.458
2.404
0





The odds ratio says: among participants with the same random intercept, the odds of donating in the loss-framing condition are estimate times the odds in the gain-framing condition.


43.2.4 Marginal Predicted Probabilities
Odds ratios are the correct internal currency of the logistic model, but they are unintuitive for most audiences. Report predicted probabilities:


Code
preds_donate <- ggpredict(m_donate, terms = "framing", type = "fixed")

plot(preds_donate) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#1e3a5f", "#e63946")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted donation probability by framing condition",
    subtitle = "Marginal predictions (averaging over individual differences)",
    x = "Framing condition",
    y = "Predicted probability of donating"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











43.2.5 The Nonlinearity of Marginal Effects
In logistic models, the effect of a predictor on the probability scale depends on where you are. An odds ratio of 1.8 translates to a larger probability increase when the baseline probability is 0.5 than when it is 0.1 or 0.9. This is a property of the logit link, not a problem.
A practical consequence: the same experimental manipulation can produce different probability effects for different sub-populations (people who generally give a lot vs. a little), even if the log-odds effect is identical. Reporting odds ratios alongside predicted probabilities at different baseline levels gives a complete picture.


Code
# Show how OR translates to different probability differences at different baselines
baseline_logodds <- seq(-3, 3, by = 0.5)
effect_logodds   <- 0.59  # log(1.8)

tibble(
  baseline_logodds = baseline_logodds,
  baseline_prob    = plogis(baseline_logodds),
  treatment_prob   = plogis(baseline_logodds + effect_logodds),
  prob_difference  = plogis(baseline_logodds + effect_logodds) - plogis(baseline_logodds)
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = baseline_prob, y = prob_difference)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title    = "Same log-odds effect → different probability differences",
    subtitle = sprintf("Log-odds effect = %.2f (OR = 1.8). The probability difference peaks at p ≈ 0.5.", effect_logodds),
    x = "Baseline probability of outcome",
    y = "Probability difference due to treatment"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
crumbs: 42  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments

title: 42  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
text:
43.3 Part 2: Count Outcomes in Experiments

43.3.1 The Poisson Mixed Model for Experiments
For count outcomes in a between-subjects design with \(j\) participants nested in condition \(g\):
\[\log\bigl(E[Y_{ij}]\bigr) = \underbrace{\beta_0}_{\substack{\text{log expected count} \\ \text{in control}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{person's count rate} \\ \text{deviation}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1 \cdot \text{treatment}_j}_{\substack{\text{treatment effect} \\ \text{on log-count}}}\]
Exponentiating \(\beta_1\) gives the rate ratio — the multiplicative factor by which the count changes in the treatment condition relative to control.


43.3.2 Example: Prosocial Helping Counts in a Scarcity Experiment
We simulate a between-subjects experiment with 100 participants (50 per condition). Participants in the scarcity condition are told resources are scarce before an opportunity to help a confederate. The outcome is the number of helping behaviors observed in a structured lab task (counts: 0–10).
The true treatment effect is a rate ratio of 0.65 — scarcity reduces helping by 35%.


Code
n_per_cond <- 50

df_help <- tibble(
  participant = 1:(n_per_cond * 2),
  condition   = rep(c("control", "scarcity"), each = n_per_cond)
) |>
  mutate(
    u0        = rnorm(n(), 0, 0.4),
    log_mu    = 1.8 + u0 + (-0.43) * as.integer(condition == "scarcity"),  # log(0.65)
    mu        = exp(log_mu),
    helping   = rpois(n(), mu),
    condition = factor(condition, levels = c("control", "scarcity"))
  )

# Summarize counts
df_help |>
  group_by(condition) |>
  summarise(
    mean_helping = mean(helping),
    sd_helping   = sd(helping),
    prop_zero    = mean(helping == 0),
    .groups      = "drop"
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Helping count summary by condition")



Helping count summary by condition


condition
mean_helping
sd_helping
prop_zero




control
5.50
3.052
0.02


scarcity
4.26
2.530
0.04







43.3.3 Fitting and Checking the Count Model


Code
m_help <- glmmTMB(
  helping ~ condition + (1 | participant),
  data   = df_help,
  family = poisson
)

# Check overdispersion
pearson_resid <- residuals(m_help, type = "pearson")
dispersion    <- sum(pearson_resid^2) / df.residual(m_help)
cat("Dispersion ratio:", round(dispersion, 3), "\n")


Dispersion ratio: 0.656 


Code
cat("Values near 1.0 indicate no overdispersion.\n\n")


Values near 1.0 indicate no overdispersion.


Code
# Results as rate ratios
broom.mixed::tidy(m_help, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Scarcity effect as rate ratio. True RR = 0.65 (35% reduction in helping)."
  )



Scarcity effect as rate ratio. True RR = 0.65 (35% reduction in helping).


term
estimate
conf.low
conf.high
p.value




conditionscarcity
0.775

0.971
0.027







43.3.4 When Poisson Is Not Enough: Overdispersion in Experiments
In within-subjects designs, participants complete multiple trials per condition, and individual differences generate more variance in counts than a Poisson model allows. This overdispersion is captured by switching to a negative binomial or adding observation-level random effects.


Code
n_par_within  <- 60
n_trials_cond <- 6  # 6 trials per condition

df_help_within <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_par_within,
  condition   = c("control", "scarcity"),
  trial       = 1:n_trials_cond
) |>
  mutate(
    u0        = rnorm(n_par_within, 0, 0.5)[participant],
    log_mu    = 1.5 + u0 - 0.43 * as.integer(condition == "scarcity"),
    mu        = exp(log_mu),
    # Add extra-Poisson variability (overdispersion)
    phi       = rgamma(n(), shape = 3, rate = 3),
    helping   = rpois(n(), mu * phi),
    participant = factor(participant),
    condition = factor(condition, levels = c("control", "scarcity"))
  )

m_nb_within <- glmmTMB(
  helping ~ condition + (1 | participant),
  data   = df_help_within,
  family = nbinom2
)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_nb_within, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Negative binomial mixed model: within-subjects scarcity on helping count."
  )



Negative binomial mixed model: within-subjects scarcity on helping count.

title: 42  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
section: 43.4 Part 3: Mixed Factorial Designs with Non-Normal Outcomes
text:
43.4 Part 3: Mixed Factorial Designs with Non-Normal Outcomes
Many experiments have both a between-subjects factor (e.g., treatment vs. control) and a within-subjects factor (e.g., pre vs. post, gain vs. loss framing). When outcomes are binary or counts, the GLMM handles this naturally through the random effects structure.


Code
n_par_mf <- 100  # 50 per between-subjects condition
n_items   <- 8   # 4 items per within-subjects condition

df_mf <- expand_grid(
  participant  = 1:n_par_mf,
  ws_condition = c("gain", "loss"),
  item         = 1:4
) |>
  mutate(
    bs_condition = ifelse(participant <= 50, "low_stakes", "high_stakes"),
    u0           = rnorm(n_par_mf, 0, 0.9)[participant],
    log_odds     = -0.3
      + u0
      + 0.4 * (ws_condition == "loss")         # within-subjects framing effect
      + 0.7 * (bs_condition == "high_stakes")  # between-subjects stakes effect
      + 0.3 * (ws_condition == "loss") * (bs_condition == "high_stakes"),  # interaction
    prob         = plogis(log_odds),
    chose_risky  = rbinom(n(), 1, prob),
    participant  = factor(participant),
    ws_condition = factor(ws_condition, levels = c("gain", "loss")),
    bs_condition = factor(bs_condition, levels = c("low_stakes", "high_stakes"))
  )

m_mf <- glmer(
  chose_risky ~ ws_condition * bs_condition + (1 | participant),
  data   = df_mf,
  family = binomial
)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_mf, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Mixed factorial binary outcome: framing × stakes on risky choice (odds ratios)."
  )



Mixed factorial binary outcome: framing × stakes on risky choice (odds ratios).

The interaction term on the odds ratio scale tells you whether the framing effect differs by stakes condition — multiplicatively. A significant interaction (OR ≠ 1) means the framing effect is larger or smaller in the high-stakes condition. As with continuous outcomes, plotting predicted probabilities is essential for communicating the interaction to readers.
crumbs: 42  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments

title: 42  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
text:
43.5 Assumptions and Diagnostics






WarningAssumption: Correct Link Function



The logit link assumes that the log-odds of the outcome are a linear function of predictors. This is generally robust and appropriate for most binary outcomes near the center of the probability range. If many outcomes are near 0 or 1 (rare or near-certain events), consider whether the probit or complementary log-log link fits better — though in practice, logit and probit rarely disagree meaningfully.








WarningAssumption: No Separation



Complete separation occurs when one predictor perfectly predicts the outcome (e.g., everyone in condition A donated, no one in condition B did). Maximum likelihood estimation fails in this case — the log-odds estimate goes to infinity. This typically signals a design problem (conditions too extreme, too few observations) rather than a modeling problem. Warning signs: very large standard errors, coefficients in the hundreds.








WarningAssumption: Correct Distributional Family for Counts



Start with Poisson. Check the dispersion ratio (Pearson \(\chi^2\) / df). If > 1.5, switch to negative binomial (glmmTMB(..., family = nbinom2)). If zeros are more prevalent than either model predicts, add a zero-inflation component (ziformula = ~1). The distributional family should match the DGP — it is a substantive assumption about how the data were generated.








WarningAssumption: Sufficient Cluster Size for GLMMs



Logistic mixed models can have convergence problems with small cluster sizes (few trials per participant) because the likelihood is flat near the random effects variance. A rule of thumb: at least 5–10 observations per cluster for reliable random intercept estimation. With fewer observations, consider Bayesian estimation (brms) which handles small-cluster GLMMs more stably.
crumbs: 42  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments

title: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
text: 44 Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
crumbs: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
text:
44.1 Why This Chapter Exists
Nested data creates reporting problems that do not exist in simple ANOVA or OLS regression. When effects are estimated at multiple levels, when random effects have their own variance components, and when outcomes live on transformed scales, the standard playbook for effect sizes (Cohen’s \(d\), \(\eta^2\), \(R^2\)) breaks down or becomes ambiguous.
This chapter covers:

Effect size measures for mixed models — what they mean and how to compute them
Translating fixed effect estimates to interpretable metrics
Conditional vs. marginal \(R^2\)
Reporting standards for nested experimental data
Reproducibility practices: seeds, version pinning, and transparent reporting
crumbs: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 44.2 Part 1: Effect Size in Mixed Models
text:
44.2 Part 1: Effect Size in Mixed Models

44.2.1 The Problem with Standard Effect Sizes
Cohen’s \(d\) is defined as the standardized mean difference:
\[d = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_2}{\sigma}\]
But what is \(\sigma\) in a mixed model? There are multiple variance components: the residual variance \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon\), the random intercept variance \(\tau^2_{00}\), and possibly additional random slope variances. Each gives a different denominator — and therefore a different \(d\).
The answer depends on the question you are asking:

Conditional \(d\): standardizes by the within-cluster (residual) variance. Answers: “How large is the effect within a person/cluster, relative to how much observations vary within that cluster?”
Marginal \(d\): standardizes by the total variance (random effects + residual). Answers: “How large is the effect relative to all variability in the outcome?”

Neither is wrong. They answer different questions.


44.2.2 Cohen’s \(d\) for Experimental Effects in Nested Data
In a typical within-subjects experiment, the effect of interest is the difference between conditions, and the relevant variability is the within-person trial-level variance. The residual SD from the mixed model (not including the random effect variance) is the appropriate standardizer for a within-person \(d\).


Code
# Simulate a within-subjects experiment: 80 participants × 10 trials per condition
n_par  <- 80
n_trial <- 10

df_d <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_par,
  condition   = c("control", "treatment"),
  trial       = 1:n_trial
) |>
  mutate(
    u0          = rnorm(n_par, 0, 8)[participant],  # between-person SD = 8
    residual_sd = 5,
    effect      = 3,  # true within-person effect
    response    = 50 + u0 + effect * (condition == "treatment") + rnorm(n(), 0, residual_sd),
    participant = factor(participant),
    condition   = factor(condition, levels = c("control", "treatment"))
  )

m_d <- lmer(response ~ condition + (1 | participant), data = df_d)

# Extract variance components
vc <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(m_d))
sigma_residual <- vc$sdcor[vc$grp == "Residual"]     # within-person SD
sigma_person   <- vc$sdcor[vc$grp == "participant"]   # between-person SD
sigma_total    <- sqrt(sigma_person^2 + sigma_residual^2)

# Fixed effect estimate
beta_treatment <- fixef(m_d)["conditiontreatment"]

# Two versions of d
d_conditional <- beta_treatment / sigma_residual
d_marginal    <- beta_treatment / sigma_total

tibble(
  metric         = c("Fixed effect (unstandardized)", "Conditional d (÷ residual SD)", "Marginal d (÷ total SD)"),
  value          = c(beta_treatment, d_conditional, d_marginal),
  denominator    = c("—", round(sigma_residual, 2), round(sigma_total, 2))
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Three ways to express the same treatment effect")



Three ways to express the same treatment effect


metric
value
denominator




Fixed effect (unstandardized)
3.010
—


Conditional d (÷ residual SD)
0.615
4.9


Marginal d (÷ total SD)
0.316
9.53





Interpreting the two \(d\) values: - Conditional \(d\): standardizes within the trial-level noise. A \(d\) of 0.6 here means the treatment shifts the mean by 0.6 trial-level SDs — controlling for stable person differences. - Marginal \(d\): standardizes relative to all variability including between-person differences. This will always be smaller than conditional \(d\) when there is substantial between-person variance, because the denominator is larger. It represents the treatment’s effect size in the full distribution of responses.
The conditional \(d\) is usually more appropriate for experimental reporting, because the design specifically removes between-person variance through random assignment and repeated measurement. Marginal \(d\) deflates the effect by variance that the design was explicitly not trying to explain.
crumbs: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 44.3 Part 2: Conditional and Marginal \(R^2\) for Mixed Models
text:
44.3 Part 2: Conditional and Marginal \(R^2\) for Mixed Models

44.3.1 The Nakagawa-Schielzeth \(R^2\)
For linear mixed models, Nakagawa & Schielzeth (2013) define two \(R^2\) values:

Marginal \(R^2_m\): Variance explained by fixed effects only — how much the predictors in the model account for, ignoring individual differences.
Conditional \(R^2_c\): Variance explained by fixed effects plus random effects — how much the whole model (predictors + clustering) accounts for.

The difference \(R^2_c - R^2_m\) is the variance uniquely attributable to the random effects (the clustering).


Code
# Compute R² using the performance package
r2_results <- performance::r2(m_d)
print(r2_results)


# R2 for Mixed Models

  Conditional R2: 0.742
     Marginal R2: 0.024




44.3.2 Interpreting \(R^2\) in Nested Designs
Consider what these values tell you:

A large marginal \(R^2\) means your experimental manipulations account for substantial variance in the outcome.
A large gap between conditional and marginal \(R^2\) means individual differences (the random effects) account for substantial variance — much of the outcome is determined by who the person is, not what condition they were in.

In a typical within-subjects experiment, conditional \(R^2\) can be quite high (0.5–0.8) while marginal \(R^2\) is low (0.03–0.15). This is not a sign that the experiment failed — it is a sign that the random effects structure is doing its job, absorbing the stable between-person variance and leaving the experimental effect clearly visible in the residuals.


Code
# Show R² decomposition visually
r2_vals <- performance::r2(m_d)

r2_df <- tibble(
  component = c("Fixed effects (marginal R²)", "Random effects (conditional − marginal)", "Unexplained"),
  proportion = c(
    r2_vals$R2_marginal,
    r2_vals$R2_conditional - r2_vals$R2_marginal,
    1 - r2_vals$R2_conditional
  )
)

ggplot(r2_df, aes(x = "", y = proportion, fill = component)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#1e3a5f", "#4a90d9", "#d0d0d0")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Variance decomposition in the mixed model",
    subtitle = "Fixed effects (marginal R²) + Random effects contribution + Residual unexplained",
    x = NULL, y = "Proportion of total variance", fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
crumbs: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 44.4 Part 3: ICC as an Effect Size
text:
44.4 Part 3: ICC as an Effect Size
The Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC) — covered in Module 1 — is itself an effect size: it quantifies how much of the outcome’s variance is attributable to the clustering structure.
\[\text{ICC} = \frac{\tau^2_{00}}{\tau^2_{00} + \sigma^2_\varepsilon}\]
In experimental reporting, the ICC tells readers how much stable between-person differences matter:

High ICC (> 0.5): Most variance is between people. Within-subjects designs are very efficient here (they remove the large between-person noise). Between-subjects designs require large samples to detect effects against this noise.
Low ICC (< 0.1): Little between-person clustering. Within- and between-subjects designs are roughly equivalent in power.

Reporting the ICC in an experimental methods section tells readers how much person-to-person variability was present and why a particular design choice (within vs. between subjects) was efficient.
crumbs: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 44.5 Part 4: Translating Model Output to Interpretable Metrics
text:
44.5 Part 4: Translating Model Output to Interpretable Metrics

44.5.1 From Log-Odds to Probabilities
For logistic mixed models, the fixed effect is in log-odds units. Always translate to probabilities for reporting:


Code
# Example: framing experiment on donation probability
# Log-odds effect = 0.59 (OR = 1.8)

log_odds_effect    <- 0.59
baseline_log_odds  <- -0.5  # average person's log-odds in control

control_prob   <- plogis(baseline_log_odds)
treatment_prob <- plogis(baseline_log_odds + log_odds_effect)

tibble(
  metric = c("Log-odds effect", "Odds ratio", "Control probability", "Treatment probability", "Probability difference"),
  value  = c(log_odds_effect, exp(log_odds_effect), control_prob, treatment_prob, treatment_prob - control_prob)
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Translating from log-odds to probabilities for reporting")



Translating from log-odds to probabilities for reporting


metric
value




Log-odds effect
0.590


Odds ratio
1.804


Control probability
0.378


Treatment probability
0.522


Probability difference
0.145





Reporting template: “Loss-framed appeals had higher donation rates than gain-framed appeals (OR = 1.81, 95% CI [1.42, 2.31], \(z\) = 4.7, \(p\) < .001). At the average baseline donation probability of 38%, loss framing increased donation probability by approximately 12 percentage points (to 50%).”


44.5.2 From Log-Counts to Rate Ratios
For Poisson/negative binomial models, exponentiate to get rate ratios:


Code
# Example: scarcity on helping count
# Log effect = -0.43 (rate ratio = 0.65)

log_rate_effect    <- -0.43
baseline_log_count <- 1.8  # average log(expected count) in control

control_count   <- exp(baseline_log_count)
treatment_count <- exp(baseline_log_count + log_rate_effect)

tibble(
  metric = c("Log-count effect", "Rate ratio", "Control mean count", "Treatment mean count", "Count reduction"),
  value  = c(log_rate_effect, exp(log_rate_effect), control_count, treatment_count, control_count - treatment_count)
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Translating from log-counts to counts for reporting")



Translating from log-counts to counts for reporting


metric
value




Log-count effect
-0.430


Rate ratio
0.651


Control mean count
6.050


Treatment mean count
3.935


Count reduction
2.114





Reporting template: “Scarcity reduced helping behavior relative to control (RR = 0.65, 95% CI [0.48, 0.88], \(p\) = .005). In the control condition, participants helped on average 6.0 times; in the scarcity condition, 3.9 times — a reduction of approximately 2.1 helping episodes.”
crumbs: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 44.6 Part 5: Reporting Standards for Nested Experimental Data
text:
44.6 Part 5: Reporting Standards for Nested Experimental Data

44.6.1 What to Report
A complete report for a mixed model analysis includes:






TipReporting Checklist for Mixed Models



Design description: - [ ] Whether the design is within-subjects, between-subjects, or mixed factorial - [ ] Number of participants, clusters, and observations per cell - [ ] ICC of the outcome (how much between-person variance was present)
Model specification: - [ ] Fixed effects included (and their coding: treatment, contrast, etc.) - [ ] Random effects structure (intercept only? slopes? which predictors vary by person?) - [ ] Software and package versions used
Results for each effect: - [ ] Unstandardized estimate with units - [ ] Standard error and 95% confidence interval - [ ] Test statistic and degrees of freedom (\(t\), \(z\), or \(F\) with Satterthwaite/Kenward-Roger df) - [ ] \(p\)-value - [ ] Effect size (\(d\), \(\eta^2\), OR, RR as appropriate)
Model-level fit: - [ ] Marginal and conditional \(R^2\) - [ ] Variance components (\(\tau^2_{00}\), \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon\), and any random slopes)
For non-normal outcomes: - [ ] Overdispersion check (for count models) - [ ] Predicted probabilities or counts at substantively meaningful covariate values




44.6.2 Example APA-Style Results Paragraph

“We analyzed donation decisions using a logistic mixed model with participant random intercepts, framing condition (gain vs. loss) as a fixed effect, and trial nested within participant. Loss-framing significantly increased donation probability (b = 0.59, OR = 1.81, 95% CI [1.42, 2.31], \(z\) = 4.7, \(p\) < .001). At the sample’s average baseline donation rate (38%), loss framing increased donation probability by approximately 12 percentage points. The ICC was .48, indicating substantial stable individual differences in donation tendency; the random intercept accounted for 31% of total variance (conditional \(R^2_c\) = .35; marginal \(R^2_m\) = .04).”
crumbs: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
text:
44.7 Part 6: Reproducibility Practices

44.7.1 Setting Seeds
Simulations, multiple imputation, bootstrapped CIs, and permutation tests all depend on random number generation. A fixed seed ensures the analysis is reproducible:


Code
# Set seed at the top of every analysis script
set.seed(42)

# For parallel computing, set seeds for each worker explicitly
library(parallel)
RNGkind("L'Ecuyer-CMRG")  # safe for parallel streams
set.seed(42)


A single set.seed() call at the top of the script is sufficient for non-parallel analyses. Document the seed in the manuscript methods section.


44.7.2 Package Version Pinning
Results can change between package versions (model fitting algorithms improve, bugs are fixed, defaults change). Record the environment at the time of analysis:


Code
# At the end of every analysis script:
sessionInfo()

# Or more concisely:
writeLines(capture.output(sessionInfo()), "session_info.txt")


For fully reproducible environments, use renv:
# Initialize renv in the project root
renv::init()

# After installing/updating packages:
renv::snapshot()  # saves renv.lock with exact package versions

# To restore the environment on a new machine:
renv::restore()


44.7.3 Preregistration for Experimental Analyses
Preregistration specifies your analysis plan before data collection, separating confirmatory from exploratory findings. For mixed model experiments, the preregistration should specify:

The exact model formula (including random effects structure)
The primary outcome variable
The family and link function (for GLMMs)
How multiple comparisons will be handled
Any planned sensitivity analyses (e.g., random slopes model as robustness check)

Deviations from the preregistered plan should be disclosed and labeled as exploratory.
crumbs: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 44.8 Part 7: Common Reporting Mistakes in Nested Data
text:
44.8 Part 7: Common Reporting Mistakes in Nested Data






WarningMistake 1: Reporting Only the Pooled Estimate



If you ran a random-intercepts model but forgot to decompose within- and between-person effects (Module 2), the fixed effect coefficient is a blend of two mechanisms. Report both if they differ.








WarningMistake 2: Using Total SD for Within-Subjects d



Reporting Cohen’s \(d\) using the total SD (including between-person variance) dramatically understates the within-person effect. Use the residual SD from the mixed model for within-subjects standardization.








WarningMistake 3: Interpreting ICC-Corrected SEs Incorrectly



Standard errors from lmer already account for the nested structure. Do not additionally “correct” them for clustering (e.g., by applying cluster-robust SEs on top of a random intercept model). That would double-count the clustering adjustment.








WarningMistake 4: Reporting Conditional R² as the “Effect Size” of a Fixed Effect



Conditional \(R^2\) includes variance explained by the random effects. It is not an effect size for your experimental manipulation. Use marginal \(R^2\) for the fixed-effects explanation, or report individual \(\eta^2\) or \(d\) for specific contrasts.








WarningMistake 5: Omitting the Random Effects Structure from the Methods



Reporting “we used a mixed model” without specifying the random effects structure (intercept only? random slopes for condition?) makes the analysis non-reproducible. Another researcher cannot verify or extend your work without knowing the exact model formula.
crumbs: 43  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 44  Non-Compliance: ITT and LATE
text:
44.1 Section 1: The Compliance Problem
Random assignment guarantees unbiased intent-to-treat (ITT) estimates — the effect of being offered or assigned to treatment. But ITT is not always the quantity of interest.
Consider a randomized exercise intervention: half the participants are assigned to a 12-week program, the other half to a waitlist control. At 12 weeks, the ITT estimate is the effect of being assigned to the program — but if only 65% of assigned participants actually exercised regularly, the ITT averages together the exercise effect with a zero effect for the 35% who never engaged. The ITT underestimates the effect of exercise itself for people who would comply.
This is the compliance problem: the treatment assigned (\(Z\)) diverges from the treatment received (\(D\)).
Four estimands address this situation, each answering a different question:
The as-treated and per-protocol estimators are both biased for the same reason: compliers are not a random subset of assigned participants. People who follow through on an exercise intervention likely differ from non-compliers on motivation, health consciousness, schedule flexibility, and many other characteristics that also predict the outcome. Comparing them to non-compliers is an observational comparison, not a causal one.

title: 44  Non-Compliance: ITT and LATE
text:
Estimand
Definition
Bias?




Intent-to-treat (ITT)
Effect of assignment (\(Z \rightarrow Y\))
Unbiased but attenuated


As-treated (AT)
Compare \(D=1\) to \(D=0\) regardless of \(Z\)
Biased (self-selection into compliance)


Per-protocol
Analyze only observed compliers
Biased (excludes non-compliers non-randomly)


LATE via IV
Effect for compliers, using \(Z\) as instrument for \(D\)
Unbiased for compliers

title: 44  Non-Compliance: ITT and LATE
text:
44.2 Section 2: The Four Compliance Types
To understand what IV recovers, we need to categorize participants by their potential compliance behavior. Imagine each person has a latent compliance type that determines how they respond to each value of the instrument:









Z = 0 (Control assigned)
Z = 1 (Treatment assigned)




D = 0 (no treatment received)
Never-takers & Compliers
Never-takers & Defiers


D = 1 (treatment received)
Always-takers & Defiers
Always-takers & Compliers



The four types:

Compliers: Take treatment when assigned (\(Z=1\)), do not take it when not assigned (\(Z=0\)). Treatment status follows assignment.
Never-takers: Never take treatment regardless of assignment. Their \(D\) is unaffected by \(Z\).
Always-takers: Always take treatment regardless of assignment. Their \(D\) is also unaffected by \(Z\).
Defiers: Do the opposite of their assignment. Assumed away by the monotonicity assumption.

The key insight: only compliers are affected by the instrument. Never-takers and always-takers have the same \(D\) regardless of \(Z\), so the instrument creates no variation in \(D\) for them. Comparing outcomes by \(Z\) among never-takers and always-takers is just noise. The ITT effect is therefore a diluted average: it reflects the treatment effect for compliers but zero for never- and always-takers.
Instrumental variables “zooms in” on compliers by scaling the ITT by the compliance rate.

title: 44  Non-Compliance: ITT and LATE
text:
44.3 Section 3: The LATE Formula
The Local Average Treatment Effect (LATE) — the average causal effect for compliers — is recovered via the Wald estimator:
\[\text{LATE} = \frac{\overbrace{E[Y \mid Z=1] - E[Y \mid Z=0]}^{\text{ITT (reduced form)}}}{\underbrace{E[D \mid Z=1] - E[D \mid Z=0]}_{\text{Compliance rate (first stage)}}}\]
The numerator is the ITT: the effect of assignment on the outcome. The denominator is the first stage: the effect of assignment on treatment receipt, i.e., the share of the sample whose treatment status is changed by the instrument. Dividing by the compliance rate “scales up” the diluted ITT to recover the effect for the sub-population whose treatment changed.
One-sided noncompliance is the special case where the control group has zero take-up (\(E[D \mid Z=0] = 0\) — no always-takers, no crossover). Then:
\[\text{LATE} = \frac{\text{ITT}}{\text{Compliance rate in treatment group}}\]
This simplification applies whenever the control condition makes treatment practically impossible (e.g., a surgical intervention, an in-person program with no self-enroll option).






TipFour Assumptions for LATE to Be Valid




Relevance: The instrument \(Z\) must affect take-up \(D\) (testable — check first-stage \(F > 10\)).
Exclusion restriction: \(Z\) affects \(Y\) only through \(D\), not directly. This is an untestable assumption that must be justified by design.
Monotonicity: No defiers. Assignment can only push people toward treatment, never away from it.
Independence: \(Z\) is as good as randomly assigned (satisfied by design in an RCT).

title: 44  Non-Compliance: ITT and LATE
section: 44.4 Section 4: Worked Example — Online Coaching Intervention
text:
44.4 Section 4: Worked Example — Online Coaching Intervention

44.4.1 Simulation
We simulate an online coaching study: 200 participants randomized 1:1. In the treatment arm, 65% complete the coaching program (one-sided noncompliance — no control participants access coaching). The true causal effect of completing the program is 12 points on an efficacy scale.


Code
n <- 200
Z <- rep(c(0, 1), each = 100)
D <- ifelse(Z == 1, rbinom(100, 1, 0.65), 0)   # one-sided: 65% comply
efficacy <- 40 + 12 * D + rnorm(n, 0, 8)        # true effect for compliers = 12

df_nc <- tibble(id = 1:n, Z = factor(Z), D = factor(D), efficacy)




44.4.2 Estimating all three quantities


Code
m_itt  <- lm(efficacy ~ Z,   data = df_nc)
m_at   <- lm(efficacy ~ D,   data = df_nc)
m_late <- ivreg(efficacy ~ D | Z, data = df_nc)





True effect for compliers = 12. ITT is diluted by the 35% non-compliers. As-treated is biased upward (compliers are more motivated). LATE recovers the true complier effect.


method
estimate
std.error
p.value




ITT (assignment)
7.339
1.183
0


As-treated (biased — self-selection)
11.503
1.150
0


LATE via IV (Wald estimator)
12.232
1.758
0







44.4.3 Manual Wald check
The IV estimate should equal ITT divided by the compliance rate:


Code
itt_est   <- coef(m_itt)["Z1"]
comp_rate <- mean(as.integer(as.character(df_nc$D))[Z == 1]) -
             mean(as.integer(as.character(df_nc$D))[Z == 0])

cat("ITT estimate:      ", round(itt_est, 3), "\n")


ITT estimate:       7.339 


Code
cat("Compliance rate:   ", round(comp_rate, 3), "\n")


Compliance rate:    0.6 


Code
cat("Wald LATE:         ", round(itt_est / comp_rate, 3), "\n")


Wald LATE:          12.232 


Code
cat("ivreg LATE:        ", round(coef(m_late)["D1"], 3), "\n")


ivreg LATE:         12.232 


The manual Wald calculation and ivreg() agree (up to rounding). This confirms the mechanics: ivreg() is just solving the Wald formula via two-stage least squares.


44.4.4 Visualization


Code
est_df <- bind_rows(
  broom::tidy(m_itt)  |> filter(term == "Z1") |>
    mutate(method = "ITT"),
  broom::tidy(m_at)   |> filter(term == "D1") |>
    mutate(method = "As-treated"),
  broom::tidy(m_late) |> filter(term == "D1") |>
    mutate(method = "LATE (IV)")
) |>
  mutate(
    method   = factor(method, levels = c("ITT", "As-treated", "LATE (IV)")),
    biased   = method == "As-treated"
  )

ggplot(est_df, aes(x = method, y = estimate, fill = biased)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.55, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = estimate - 1.96 * std.error,
                    ymax = estimate + 1.96 * std.error),
                width = 0.18) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 12, linetype = "dashed", color = "#c0392b", linewidth = 0.9) +
  annotate("text", x = 3.45, y = 12.5, label = "True effect = 12",
           color = "#c0392b", hjust = 1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#1e3a5f", "TRUE" = "#e67e22"),
                    guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title    = "ITT, as-treated, and LATE estimates",
    subtitle = "ITT diluted by non-compliers; as-treated biased by self-selection; LATE recovers true effect",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Estimated effect on efficacy"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: 44  Non-Compliance: ITT and LATE
section: 44.5 Section 5: When Is ITT vs. LATE the Right Estimand?
text:
44.5 Section 5: When Is ITT vs. LATE the Right Estimand?
Both ITT and LATE are valid causal estimands — they answer different questions. The choice depends on what the researcher or policymaker actually wants to know.








Estimand
Right for
Policy analog




ITT
“What happens if we offer this program?”
Opt-in program evaluation — you offer it, some take it


LATE
“What is the effect of actually receiving treatment?”
Compliance-based evaluation — effect among those who engage



Policy example 1 — Free childcare vouchers: A government evaluates whether to offer free childcare vouchers to low-income families. Some families will use the voucher, others will not. The policy assigns the offer, not the childcare itself. The ITT (effect of receiving a voucher, even if unused) is the directly relevant estimand for evaluating the voucher program — it captures the real-world effect of running the program.
Policy example 2 — Cognitive behavioral therapy: A clinical researcher wants to know whether CBT reduces depression symptoms for patients who attend sessions. The instrument is referral to a CBT provider; only some referred patients actually attend. LATE — the effect for those who attended because they were referred (compliers) — is the clinically relevant quantity. The ITT (effect of referral) captures the effect of the system, which is less informative about whether CBT itself works.






TipPractical Guidance



When writing up results, report both ITT and LATE. The ITT is interpretable as an “offer effect” that any policymaker can use directly. The LATE is interpretable as the treatment effect for those who would engage, given the design. Report the first-stage compliance rate and the \(F\)-statistic so readers can evaluate the strength of the instrument.

title: 44  Non-Compliance: ITT and LATE
text:
44.6 Section 6: Two-Sided Noncompliance
Two-sided noncompliance occurs when some control-group participants find the treatment on their own (always-takers, crossover). This is common in studies of freely available interventions (apps, publicly available therapies, exercise programs). The control group no longer has zero take-up.


Code
n   <- 200
Z   <- rep(c(0, 1), each = 100)
D   <- ifelse(Z == 1,
              rbinom(100, 1, 0.75),   # 75% compliance in treatment
              rbinom(100, 1, 0.10))   # 10% crossover in control
Y   <- 50 - 8 * D + rnorm(n, 0, 7)

df_2s      <- tibble(Z = factor(Z), D = factor(D), Y)
m_late_2s  <- ivreg(Y ~ D | Z, data = df_2s)

broom::tidy(m_late_2s) |>
  filter(term == "D1") |>
  select(estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Two-sided noncompliance: ivreg() generalizes automatically. True effect = -8.")



Two-sided noncompliance: ivreg() generalizes automatically. True effect = -8.


estimate
std.error
p.value




-7.889
1.526
0





The Wald formula generalizes: the denominator is now \(E[D \mid Z=1] - E[D \mid Z=0] = 0.75 - 0.10 = 0.65\), reflecting that 65% of the sample has their treatment status determined by the instrument (the compliers). ivreg() handles this automatically.






WarningTwo-Sided Non-Compliance Shrinks the Complier Pool



With 10% crossover in the control group, the effective “complier” share drops. The first-stage \(F\)-statistic will be lower than in the one-sided case with the same treatment-group compliance rate, because there is less net variation in \(D\) driven by \(Z\). Monitor the first-stage \(F\)-statistic carefully when crossover is suspected.

title: 44  Non-Compliance: ITT and LATE
section: 44.7 Section 7: Noncompliance in Nested Experimental Designs
text:
44.7 Section 7: Noncompliance in Nested Experimental Designs
In multi-site experiments (students nested in classrooms, patients nested in clinics), noncompliance can cluster at the site level: an entire school may implement the program poorly, or an entire clinic may have low patient adherence. Treating compliance as independent across participants in the same site understates uncertainty.
The remedy is cluster-robust standard errors at the site level:


Code
library(sandwich)

# Assume df_nc has a 'site' variable
coeftest(m_late, vcov = vcovCL(m_late, cluster = ~site))


When site-level compliance rates vary substantially, it can also be informative to model compliance as a site-level random effect — sites with low compliance contribute attenuated ITT estimates, and the LATE for each site reflects that site’s compliance rate.






TipSite-Level Compliance in Multi-Site Studies



If site-level compliance is itself of interest (e.g., “does full implementation matter?”), you can model it explicitly:
# Site-level compliance rate as a predictor
site_compliance <- df_nested |>
  filter(Z == 1) |>
  group_by(site) |>
  summarise(compliance_rate = mean(D_numeric))

df_nested <- left_join(df_nested, site_compliance, by = "site")
lmer(Y ~ compliance_rate + (1 | site), data = df_nested |> filter(Z == 1))
This is a “dose-response” analysis at the site level and requires its own identification argument (why does compliance vary across sites independent of site characteristics?).

title: 44  Non-Compliance: ITT and LATE
text:
44.8 Section 8: Reporting Template
Use the following template for APA-style reporting of ITT and LATE results:

Participants were randomly assigned to [coaching intervention] (n = 100) or waitlist control (n = 100). Compliance was 65% in the treatment group (one-sided noncompliance; no control participants accessed the intervention). Intent-to-treat analysis — using treatment assignment as the predictor regardless of compliance — showed a statistically significant but attenuated effect (b = [ITT estimate], SE = [ITT SE], p = [p]). To estimate the effect for participants who complied with treatment, we used instrumental variables regression (two-stage least squares) with treatment assignment as the instrument for treatment receipt. The complier average causal effect (LATE) was b = [LATE estimate], SE = [LATE SE], p = [p], consistent with a [direction and magnitude] effect for participants who engaged with the intervention. The first-stage F-statistic (F = [F]) exceeded the conventional weak-instruments threshold of 10, supporting the instrument’s relevance.

title: 45  Quasi-Experimental Methods for Applied Researchers
text:
45.1 Section 1: When Experiments Have Observational Features
A fully randomized experiment with perfect compliance and no attrition is the gold standard for causal inference — but it rarely survives contact with the field intact. In practice, experiments accumulate observational features: participants drop out for reasons related to the treatment, some assigned to treatment never engage, ethical or logistical constraints limit who can be randomized, and field implementations vary substantially across sites.
These complications do not invalidate the experiment. They do require the researcher to reach into the quasi-experimental toolkit that Module 3 introduced for observational studies. The good news: the same tools that handle observational confounding also handle experimental imperfections — often in a cleaner setting, because the original randomization still provides some traction.
The table below maps common experimental complications to their methodological remedies and their Module 3 antecedents:
This chapter walks through implementations for four of these situations: DiD as the logic of pre-post designs, IPW for non-random attrition, PSM for voluntary sub-treatment participation, and threshold-based eligibility (RDD). The noncompliance case is covered in detail in Module 5 Part 8.
crumbs: 45  Quasi-Experimental Methods for Applied Researchers

text:
Experimental complications and their quasi-experimental remedies







Situation
Method

— (Modules 1–2)

Part 2 (IV) → Module 5 Part 8

crumbs: 45  Quasi-Experimental Methods for Applied Researchers

title: 45  Quasi-Experimental Methods for Applied Researchers
section: 45.2 Section 2: DiD as the Logic of Pre-Post Designs
text:
45.2 Section 2: DiD as the Logic of Pre-Post Designs

45.2.1 The structural equivalence
The standard randomized pre-post design is a difference-in-differences design. It has:

Two groups: intervention and control (the “treated vs. untreated” dimension of DiD)
Two time points: baseline and follow-up (the “pre vs. post” dimension of DiD)
The DiD estimand: \((\bar{Y}^{\text{post}}_{\text{int}} - \bar{Y}^{\text{pre}}_{\text{int}}) - (\bar{Y}^{\text{post}}_{\text{ctrl}} - \bar{Y}^{\text{pre}}_{\text{ctrl}})\)

Recognizing this equivalence is valuable for two reasons. First, it makes explicit what the pre-post mixed model assumes (parallel trends in the absence of treatment — which randomization guarantees in expectation). Second, it gives you the full DiD toolkit: you can add multiple waves, test pre-trend balance, and use the event study framework to trace the treatment trajectory over time.


45.2.2 Simulation: stress management intervention
We simulate 120 participants (60 per arm) measured at baseline (pre) and 8-week follow-up (post). A stress management training reduces cortisol. True DiD effect = −8 units.


Code
n_per <- 60

df_pp <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:(n_per * 2),
  wave        = c("pre", "post")
) |>
  mutate(
    condition = ifelse(participant <= n_per, "control", "intervention"),
    time_num  = as.integer(wave == "post"),
    treat_num = as.integer(condition == "intervention"),
    u0        = rnorm(n_per * 2, 0, 5)[participant],   # person random intercept
    cortisol  = 25 + u0 - 1.5 * time_num - 8 * time_num * treat_num + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    participant = factor(participant),
    wave      = factor(wave, levels = c("pre", "post")),
    condition = factor(condition, levels = c("control", "intervention"))
  )




45.2.3 DiD mixed model


Code
m_did_exp <- lmer(cortisol ~ wave * condition + (1 | participant),
                  data = df_pp, REML = FALSE)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_did_exp, effects = "fixed") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "DiD mixed model. The wave:condition interaction is the DiD estimate (target: -8).")



DiD mixed model. The wave:condition interaction is the DiD estimate (target: -8).

The wavepost:conditionintervention interaction coefficient is the DiD estimand — the differential change from pre to post in the intervention arm relative to the control arm.


45.2.4 Comparison to ANCOVA
ANCOVA on the wide-format outcome, controlling for the pre-test, is a common alternative:


Code
df_wide <- df_pp |>
  select(participant, condition, wave, cortisol) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = wave, values_from = cortisol,
              names_prefix = "cortisol_") |>
  mutate(cortisol_pre_c = cortisol_pre - mean(cortisol_pre))

m_ancova <- lm(cortisol_post ~ condition + cortisol_pre_c, data = df_wide)

broom::tidy(m_ancova) |>
  filter(term == "conditionintervention") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "ANCOVA treatment estimate. Numerically close to DiD mixed model when pre-test is mean-centered.")



ANCOVA treatment estimate. Numerically close to DiD mixed model when pre-test is mean-centered.


term

std.error

conditionintervention

0.971

The two approaches converge when the pre-test is mean-centered and the random-effect variance is estimated well. The DiD mixed model generalizes more naturally to three or more waves and produces an interpretable random-effects structure, making it preferred for longitudinal experimental designs.


45.2.5 Event study visualization


Code
summ_pp <- df_pp |>
  group_by(wave, condition) |>
  summarise(mean_c = mean(cortisol), se_c = sd(cortisol) / sqrt(n()),
            .groups = "drop")

ggplot(summ_pp, aes(x = wave, y = mean_c,
                     color = condition, group = condition)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_c - 1.96 * se_c,
                    ymax = mean_c + 1.96 * se_c), width = 0.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("control" = "#aec6e8", "intervention" = "#1e3a5f")) +
  annotate("segment",
           x = 2.05, xend = 2.05, y = 20.5, yend = 14.5,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), ends = "both"),
           color = "#c0392b") +
  annotate("text", x = 2.12, y = 17.5,
           label = "DiD \u2248 \u22128", color = "#c0392b", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title    = "Pre-post cortisol by condition",
    subtitle = "Parallel pre-period (randomization), divergent post-period (treatment)",
    x        = "Wave",
    y        = "Mean cortisol (arbitrary units)",
    color    = "Condition"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











45.2.6 Three-wave extension
With baseline, mid-point, and follow-up, the time × condition interaction becomes a trajectory. Pre-baseline balance checks verify randomization; post-baseline coefficients trace how the treatment effect builds over time. This is the experimental event study:


Code
# Three-wave data with months 0, 4, 8
m_3wave <- lmer(cortisol ~ factor(month) * condition + (1 | participant),
                data = df_3wave)
# Plot estimated coefficients for each month x condition cell


Pre-period interaction coefficients near zero confirm that randomization produced balanced groups. Post-period coefficients show whether treatment effects are immediate, delayed, or accumulating.
crumbs: 45  Quasi-Experimental Methods for Applied Researchers

title: 45  Quasi-Experimental Methods for Applied Researchers
text:
45.3 Section 3: IPW for Non-Random Attrition

45.3.1 The problem
Even in a fully randomized trial, attrition at follow-up can reintroduce bias. If high-anxiety control-group participants are more likely to drop out — because the study reminds them of their struggles and they disengage — then the observed control-group follow-up average is inflated (it excludes the highest-anxiety participants). The treatment appears more effective than it truly is for the full intention-to-treat sample.


45.3.2 Simulation


Code
n          <- 200
treatment  <- rep(c(0, 1), each = 100)
baseline_anx <- rnorm(n, 50, 10)

# Attrition: higher anxiety + control → more likely to drop out
p_remain  <- plogis(2 - 0.04 * baseline_anx + 0.4 * treatment)
remained  <- rbinom(n, 1, p_remain)

# True treatment effect = -5 (treatment reduces outcome)
outcome   <- 40 - 5 * treatment + 0.3 * baseline_anx + rnorm(n, 0, 6)

df_att <- tibble(id = 1:n, treatment, baseline_anx, remained, outcome)

cat("Retention rate — control:  ", mean(remained[treatment == 0]), "\n")


Retention rate — control:   0.5 


Code
cat("Retention rate — treatment:", mean(remained[treatment == 1]), "\n")


Retention rate — treatment: 0.58 




45.3.3 IPW correction
We model the probability of remaining in the study as a function of baseline anxiety and treatment, then weight complete cases by the inverse of their retention probability:


Code
ps_att    <- glm(remained ~ baseline_anx + treatment,
                 family = binomial, data = df_att)
df_att$ipw <- 1 / fitted(ps_att)

df_obs <- df_att |> filter(remained == 1)

m_naive_att <- lm(outcome ~ treatment, data = df_obs)
m_ipw_att   <- lm(outcome ~ treatment, data = df_obs,
                   weights = df_att$ipw[df_att$remained == 1])





True treatment effect = -5. Naive estimate is attenuated toward zero because high-anxiety control participants (who would show the smallest benefit) dropped out. IPW recovers the true estimate by upweighting their remaining counterparts.


method
estimate
std.error
p.value




Naive complete-case analysis
-4.191
1.138
0


IPW-corrected analysis
-4.088
1.132
0











WarningIPW Assumptions for Attrition Correction



IPW corrects for attrition under Missing at Random (MAR): the probability of dropout depends on observed pre-treatment variables (here, baseline anxiety and treatment condition), but not on the unobserved post-treatment outcome. If dropout is driven by the unmeasured outcome itself — e.g., people drop out because they are getting worse and do not want to be measured — IPW will not fully correct the bias. Consider collecting data on dropouts via brief follow-up contacts to test the MAR assumption empirically.




45.3.4 Visualization


Code
est_df <- bind_rows(
  broom::tidy(m_naive_att) |> filter(term == "treatment") |>
    mutate(method = "Naive"),
  broom::tidy(m_ipw_att) |> filter(term == "treatment") |>
    mutate(method = "IPW corrected")
) |>
  mutate(method = factor(method, levels = c("Naive", "IPW corrected")))

ggplot(est_df, aes(x = method, y = estimate, fill = method)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = estimate - 1.96 * std.error,
                    ymax = estimate + 1.96 * std.error), width = 0.18) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -5, linetype = "dashed", color = "#c0392b") +
  annotate("text", x = 2.4, y = -4.6, label = "True effect = -5",
           color = "#c0392b", size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Naive" = "#e67e22", "IPW corrected" = "#1e3a5f"),
                    guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title    = "IPW corrects attrition bias",
    subtitle = "Naive estimate attenuated; IPW recovers true treatment effect",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Estimated treatment effect"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
crumbs: 45  Quasi-Experimental Methods for Applied Researchers

title: 45  Quasi-Experimental Methods for Applied Researchers
section: 45.4 Section 4: PSM for Voluntary Sub-Treatment Participation
text:
45.4 Section 4: PSM for Voluntary Sub-Treatment Participation

45.4.1 The within-experiment observational problem
Random assignment creates treatment and control arms, but within the treatment arm, engagement is voluntary. Consider a digital health app randomized to half the sample: some participants use it daily, others open it once and forget it. Comparing high-users to low-users is an observational comparison — heavier users likely differ from lighter users on motivation, health literacy, and self-regulation, all of which are independent predictors of health outcomes.
Propensity score matching on pre-treatment covariates makes this within-arm comparison more credible by constructing a control group of low-users who resembled high-users at baseline.


45.4.2 Simulation and matching
We work within the treatment arm of the attrition simulation and create a high-use indicator that is related to baseline anxiety:


Code
df_treat_only <- df_att |>
  filter(treatment == 1) |>
  mutate(
    high_user = rbinom(n(), 1, plogis(-0.5 + 0.04 * baseline_anx))
  )

m_usage <- matchit(
  high_user ~ baseline_anx,
  data   = df_treat_only,
  method = "nearest",
  ratio  = 1
)

summary(m_usage)$sum.matched |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Covariate balance after 1:1 nearest-neighbor matching on baseline anxiety.")

Code
matched      <- match.data(m_usage)
m_usage_eff  <- lm(outcome ~ high_user + baseline_anx,
                   data = matched, weights = weights)

broom::tidy(m_usage_eff) |>
  filter(term == "high_user") |>
  select(estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Matched estimate of high-use vs. low-use effect on outcome, among participants with similar baseline anxiety.")



Matched estimate of high-use vs. low-use effect on outcome, among participants with similar baseline anxiety.

WarningWhat PSM Within an Experiment Identifies



Matching on pre-treatment covariates removes observed confounders of the high-use vs. low-use comparison. But unobserved motivation, self-efficacy, and expectancy effects remain. The matched estimate is closer to causal than the naive comparison, but it is not an RCT-quality estimate.
Formally, this identifies a LATE for the sub-population of low-users who were matched to comparable high-users — not the ATE for all participants. Interpret as “the effect of high engagement, for people with similar observable characteristics to high engagers,” not “the effect of high engagement for everyone.”

title: 45  Quasi-Experimental Methods for Applied Researchers
section: 45.5 Section 5: Threshold-Based Eligibility (RDD in Applied Research)
text:
45.5 Section 5: Threshold-Based Eligibility (RDD in Applied Research)
When participants must exceed a continuous threshold on a screening measure to qualify for a treatment arm, regression discontinuity design (RDD) is the appropriate analysis. The continuity assumption — that potential outcomes vary smoothly across the cutoff — is often more credible in clinical settings than the selection-on-observables assumption required by propensity score methods.
A common example: a mental health intervention restricted to participants scoring PHQ-9 \(\geq 15\) (moderately severe depression). Participants just below 15 and just above 15 are likely similar on most characteristics; the discontinuity in treatment receipt at 15 creates local quasi-random variation.


Code
# Requires rdrobust package
library(rdrobust)
rdd_out <- rdrobust(y = df$outcome, x = df$phq9, c = 15)
summary(rdd_out)
rdplot(y = df$outcome, x = df$phq9, c = 15,
       title = "RDD: PHQ-9 screening cutoff at 15")


The rdrobust package implements optimal bandwidth selection, bias-corrected confidence intervals, and the McCrary density test (for manipulation of the running variable). The full treatment of RDD mechanics, bandwidth selection, and robustness checks is in Module 3 Part 4; that framework applies directly here.






TipRDD in Clinical and Educational Research



RDD is underused in psychology and organizational research relative to economics, despite being highly credible when a threshold rule genuinely determines assignment. Common examples: academic probation cutoffs, clinical screening cutoffs (PHQ-9, GAD-7, ACE score), performance review thresholds, grant funding cutoffs. If your research setting uses a cutoff rule, seriously consider RDD before reaching for PSM.

title: 45  Quasi-Experimental Methods for Applied Researchers
text:
45.6 Section 6: Practical Decision Guide






TipChoosing Your Method When Randomization Is Imperfect



Work through this sequence when your experimental data does not satisfy the simple randomization ideal:

Fully randomized, no attrition, full compliance? Standard mixed model (Modules 1–2). No quasi-experimental adjustment needed.
Randomized + differential attrition? IPW for attrition (this chapter, Section 3). Requires MAR assumption.
Randomized + noncompliance? Report ITT (always). If complier effect is of interest, add LATE via IV (Module 5 Part 8).
Eligibility determined by a continuous threshold? Regression discontinuity (Module 3 Part 4). Local to the threshold — do not generalize far from cutoff.
Pre-post design with randomized comparison group? DiD mixed model (this chapter, Section 2). Generalizes to multiple waves as event study.
Self-selection into sub-treatment within an experiment? PSM within treatment arm (this chapter, Section 4). Residual unobserved confounding remains.
Observational study with good observed confounders? PSM or IPW on covariates (Module 3 Part 6). Validity depends entirely on selection-on-observables.
Observational study with a credible instrument? IV or LATE (Module 3 Part 2, Module 5 Part 8). Validity depends on exclusion restriction.

No method is universally best. The right method depends on the data-generating process and which assumption is most defensible given your study design.

title: 45  Quasi-Experimental Methods for Applied Researchers
text:
45.7 Section 7: The Full Course Arc
This workshop has built from the ground up.
Module 1 established the mixed-effects framework: how to model data where observations are nested within units, how to decompose variance across levels, and why treating clustered data as independent inflates false positive rates. The random intercept model is not a statistical correction tacked on after the fact — it is a direct translation of the sampling structure into the model.
Module 2 showed that how you enter predictors matters as much as which predictors you include. Within-unit centering decomposes predictors into their between-unit and within-unit components, corresponding to different mechanisms operating at different levels of the hierarchy. Getting the centering wrong means estimating the wrong estimand, even with a correctly specified error structure.
Module 3 showed where the well-specified mixed model can still go wrong: omitted variables (time-invariant and time-varying), endogenous regressors, selection into the sample, regression discontinuities at thresholds, treatment effect heterogeneity, and non-random treatment assignment. Six threats; six identification strategies. Each strategy requires a different source of identifying variation and a different set of untestable assumptions.
Module 4 addressed the practicalities of longitudinal data: handling missing values without complete-case bias, and applying the Module 3 identification strategies to the panel setting where time creates both new threats and new opportunities.
Module 5 closed the loop for applied experimental researchers: what to do when the experiment is imperfect, and how to connect the quasi-experimental toolkit back to the experimental design.
The unifying insight across all five modules:

Understanding your data generating process — who is nested in whom, what varies within units versus across units, what mechanisms connect your predictors to your outcome, and what determines who ends up in each condition — is the prerequisite for every methodological choice covered in this workshop.

The nesting structure is not a nuisance to correct for with a random effect and move on. It is the scientific structure of your data. The causal question is always about a mechanism operating at a particular level of that structure, through a particular pathway, for a particular population. The method is in service of making that mechanism legible.

title: Appendix A — About
text:
Dan Schley is an Associate Professor in the Department of Marketing Management at Rotterdam School of Management, Erasmus University Rotterdam. His work sits at the intersection of psychology, economics, and statistical methodology, with a particular focus on measurement, experimentation, and causal inference in behavioral research. Contact: schley@rsm.nl

I came to multilevel modeling the way many behavioral researchers do — not as a statistician, but as someone who kept noticing that the standard tools didn’t quite fit the data. My training moved across economics at UC San Diego, quantitative psychology at The Ohio State University, and ultimately marketing research at Erasmus. Each transition brought a different set of methodological assumptions into view.
Repeated-measures experiments. Panel datasets. Longitudinal surveys. Cross-national comparisons. In every case, observations are clustered in ways that basic regression ignores. And in every case, the right question is not just “how do I fit this model?” but “what process generated this data, and how close does my model get to it?”
This workshop grew out of teaching the ERIM doctoral program at RSM, where I found that students’ biggest stumbling block was not the mathematics — it was the conceptual leap from thinking about data as a flat table to thinking about data as a slice through a structured, multi-layered process. The DGP framing — treating your dataset as a snapshot of a larger data-generating process — helped more students than any single equation.
The companion workshop on Measurement, Experimentation, and Causal Inference covers many of the same research design questions from a different angle. Together, these workshops aim to bridge the vocabulary gap between psychology, economics, and quantitative social science.

title: Appendix B — Suggest a Topic
text:
This workshop is a living document. If there is a topic you would like to see covered — or a section you think could be clearer, deeper, or better illustrated — please use the “Report an Issue” link in the sidebar (or click the GitHub icon at the top right of any page).
Suggestions that have been particularly useful from past participants:

More worked examples with real datasets
Additional guidance on reporting standards
Coverage of specific R packages (e.g., lme4 vs. brms comparison)
Connections to SEM software (Mplus, lavaan)
Applied examples from specific subfields (clinical, organizational, consumer behavior)

All feedback is welcome.
